lizards.desc: Long-bodied lizard with short legs and a very long prehensile tail. Often found in gardens. Will bite and hold on tenaciously if grabbed. Frequently seen in mating pairs locked together for hours.

fm.Color: Brown to olive with dark crossbands; lateral fold along sides
fm.Habitat: Gardens, leaf litter, under boards, rock walls, dense vegetation
fm.Behavior: Slow-moving; bites and HOLDS if grabbed; mating pairs lock jaws for hours
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, snails, slugs, small lizards, bird eggs
fm.vs: Northern Alligator Lizard has dark EYES (vs pale in Southern). Legless lizards lack external legs entirely.
fm.Urban: One of the most common garden lizards. Often found under potted plants and debris.

lizards.hp: Important garden predator — eats slugs, snails, and pest insects. The long prehensile tail aids climbing.

lizards.desc: The nominate species of the legless lizard complex after the 2013 split. Range barely enters northern LA County. Sandy substrates in coastal scrub. Declining throughout its range from habitat loss.
lizards.hp: Range largely north of LA County — Ventura/SB coast. Northern edge of range may overlap with A. stebbinsi in NW LA County. Conservation status under review following the species complex split.

fm.Color: Silvery to brown above, yellow below
fm.Habitat: Coastal scrub, sandy soils — NW LA County range edge
fm.vs: Indistinguishable from A. stebbinsi in the field. Range determines ID.

lizards.desc: LA County's most commonly encountered legless lizard. Snake-like limbless lizard with SHINY polished-metal scales. Lives underground in loose sandy soil and leaf litter. Not a snake — has moveable eyelids and can shed its tail. Recently split from the former A. pulchra complex in 2013.
lizards.hp: Split from the former A. pulchra complex in 2013 — five new species described from what was once considered a single species. A. stebbinsi is the coastal SoCal form. Habitat loss to development is the primary threat.

fm.Color: Silvery gray above, yellow below. Smooth shiny scales.
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, sandy soils, leaf litter, gardens — under boards, rocks, compost
fm.Behavior: Burrows in loose soil; surfaces after rain and at dusk. Wriggles rapidly when uncovered.
fm.Diet: Insect larvae, small invertebrates in soil
fm.vs: Northern Legless Lizard (A. pulchra) is more northern range. Distinguished by genetics, not field marks. Snakes lack eyelids.
fm.Urban: Found when digging in coastal foothill gardens — under potted plants, compost, and mulch. Often mistaken for a snake.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Multiple Anniella species declining from coastal sage scrub habitat loss to development throughout LA Basin.

lizards.desc: Spectacular large lizard of rocky desert habitats in eastern LA County. Males are brilliantly colored with turquoise, orange, and yellow. Two black collar bands around neck are diagnostic. Bipedal runner — stands up and sprints on hind legs when fleeing.
lizards.hp: Rocky desert specialist at eastern edge of LA County range. Extreme heat tolerance — active in temperatures that force other lizards into shade. Bipedal running is a derived adaptation shared with Leopard Lizards in the family Crotaphytidae.

fm.Color: Males brilliant turquoise, orange, yellow; TWO black collar bands diagnostic
fm.Habitat: Rocky desert slopes, boulder fields — eastern LA County Mojave fringe
fm.Behavior: Perches prominently on boulders; bipedal sprinting escape; head-bobbing territory display
fm.Diet: Large insects, other lizards, flowers — opportunistic predator
fm.vs: Leopard Lizard has spots not collars, prefers flats. Collared Lizard prefers rocky slopes and has diagnostic collar bands.

lizards.desc: Large, fast predatory lizard of desert flats and creosote bush scrub. Distinguished by leopard-like dark spots and extremely long tail. Aggressive predator that eats other lizards, including juvenile rattlesnakes. Females develop bright orange-red bars when gravid.
lizards.hp: Top predator among LA County lizards — regularly eats Side-blotched Lizards, Zebra-tailed Lizards, and even small snakes. Gravid females develop vivid orange bars that signal breeding condition and may deter male harassment.

fm.Size: 3.5-5.5 in SVL — one of LA's largest lizards
fm.Color: Gray-tan with dark leopard spots; gravid females show ORANGE bars
fm.Habitat: Desert flats, creosote bush scrub — Antelope Valley, western Mojave
fm.Behavior: Bipedal running at high speed; ambush predator from bush shade
fm.Diet: Other lizards, large insects, small snakes — top lizard predator
fm.vs: Much larger than Side-blotched. Collared Lizard has distinct collar bands and is more rock-associated.

lizards.desc: Southern California subspecies of Western Banded Gecko — the coastal lowland population. Ground-dwelling nocturnal gecko of rocky desert edges. Waves tail over body when threatened, mimicking a scorpion. Moveable eyelids distinguish it from all other LA geckos.

fm.Color: Pale with dark crossbands; moveable eyelids
fm.Habitat: Rocky desert edges, dry washes — under rocks and debris in coastal lowland and foothill zones
fm.Behavior: NOCTURNAL; waves tail slowly over body when threatened; ground-dwelling (does NOT climb walls)
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, baby scorpions — nocturnal ground forager
fm.vs: Mediterranean Gecko climbs WALLS near lights and chirps. Banded Gecko is ground-dwelling, banded, silent, and in wilder habitats.

lizards.hp: Coastal subspecies at western range limit. Tail-waving defense may mimic scorpion posture — deters predators unfamiliar with actual scorpion stings. Active after warm summer rains.

lizards.desc: Delicate native gecko with banded pattern. Ground-dwelling — does NOT climb walls like the Mediterranean Gecko. Waves tail over body when threatened. Desert-edge species entering eastern LA County.

fm.Color: Pale with dark brown BANDS (crossbands); moveable eyelids (unlike most geckos)
fm.Habitat: Rocky desert edges, dry washes, under rocks and debris — ground-dwelling
fm.Behavior: NOCTURNAL; waves tail slowly over body when threatened; ground-dwelling (does NOT climb walls)
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, baby scorpions
fm.vs: Mediterranean Gecko climbs WALLS near lights, has bumpy skin, and chirps. Banded Gecko is ground-dwelling, banded, and silent.
fm.Urban: Desert-edge foothill communities — found under rocks and debris in yards. Ground-dwelling, unlike wall-climbing geckos. Tail-waving defense behavior distinctive.

lizards.hp: Native gecko of arid habitats. Unlike introduced Mediterranean Gecko, this species is ground-dwelling and found in natural habitats, not on buildings.

lizards.desc: Large robust lizard of rocky desert outcrops. Inflates body in rock crevices to wedge itself in when threatened. Herbivorous — eats flowers and leaves. Eastern LA County desert margins.

fm.Size: 11–18 in total length — largest native lizard in LA County range
fm.Color: Males: black head/body with red-brown tail. Females/juveniles: banded
fm.Habitat: Rocky desert outcrops, boulder piles — eastern LA County desert margins
fm.Behavior: INFLATES body to wedge in rock crevices; exclusively rock-dwelling; basking on boulders
fm.Diet: HERBIVOROUS — flowers, leaves, fruit (unusual for lizards)
fm.vs: Only large robust lizard in rocky desert. No other LA lizard approaches this size in desert habitats.

lizards.hp: One of few vegetarian lizards — diet almost exclusively flowers, leaves, and fruit of desert plants. Wedges into rock crevices and inflates body as anti-predator defense. Desert rock specialist declining where boulder habitat is disturbed by off-road recreation.

lizards.desc: Pale desert lizard tolerating extreme heat — active at body temperatures that would kill other lizards (115°F+). Creosote bush specialist. Eastern LA County desert fringe, Antelope Valley.

fm.Color: Pale gray with tan reticulated pattern; row of enlarged dorsal scales down back
fm.Habitat: Creosote bush scrub, sandy flats — eastern LA County desert
fm.Behavior: Tolerates extreme heat (active at 115°F+); HERBIVOROUS; burrows in sand; very fast runner
fm.Diet: Creosote bush flowers and leaves — primarily herbivorous (unusual for lizards)
fm.vs: Only pale desert lizard with dorsal crest of enlarged scales. Chuckwalla is much larger and rock-dwelling.

lizards.hp: Heat specialist active at body temperatures (46°C) lethal to other lizards. Diet tightly linked to creosote bush flowers and leaves — obligate associate of creosote scrub habitat. One of few herbivorous lizards in North America.

lizards.desc: Flat spiny 'horny toad' — California's most iconic lizard and a Species of Special Concern. Squirts BLOOD from its eyes when threatened. Declining due to habitat loss and Argentine ant invasion eliminating native harvester ants.

fm.Color: Brown/tan camouflaged with dark spots; crown of HORNS on head; flat body
fm.Habitat: Open sandy areas, coastal sage scrub — requires native harvester ant colonies
fm.Behavior: Freezes and relies on camouflage; squirts BLOOD from eyes as last defense
fm.Diet: Almost exclusively native HARVESTER ANTS (Pogonomyrmex); some other insects
fm.vs: No other LA lizard has horns and a flat body. Desert Horned Lizard (P. platyrhinos) is a Mojave species with different range.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Declining steeply due to: 1) Argentine ant invasion (displaces native harvester ants), 2) habitat loss, 3) road mortality, 4) collection.
fm.Urban: Formerly common in vacant lots throughout LA Basin. Now restricted to remaining sage scrub fragments.

lizards.hp: Specialized ant predator — eats 200+ harvester ants per day. Argentine ant invasion is eliminating food supply across LA Basin. San Gabriel Mountains foothill populations face less Argentine ant pressure but more fire and recreation threats.

lizards.desc: Flat-bodied horned lizard of the Mojave Desert — eastern LA County. Shorter horns than Coast Horned Lizard. Excellent camouflage on desert pavement. Relies on harvester ants as primary food — declining where ants are displaced by Argentine ants.
lizards.hp: Mojave Desert species at western range edge in LA County. Unlike Coast Horned Lizard, this species is more desert-adapted and has shorter temporal horns. Both species depend on native harvester ants and decline when Argentine ants invade.

fm.Color: Gray-tan matching desert soil; dark blotches; SHORTER horns than Coast Horned Lizard
fm.Habitat: Desert pavement, creosote flats — eastern LA County Mojave
fm.Diet: Harvester ants (primary), other insects
fm.vs: Coast Horned Lizard has LONGER horns and occupies coastal/foothill habitat. Desert Horned Lizard is Mojave-restricted with shorter horns.

lizards.desc: Largest spiny lizard in LA County range — pale desert species. Straw-yellow to tan. Eastern LA County desert margins. Perches on Joshua trees and fence posts.

fm.Size: 7–12 in total length — largest local Sceloporus
fm.Color: Pale straw-yellow to tan with dark crossbands; males with blue-purple belly and throat
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, Joshua tree woodland — perches on Joshua trees, yucca, fence posts. Antelope Valley.
fm.Behavior: Basking on elevated perches; territorial; aggressive toward other lizards
fm.Diet: Insects, flowers, leaves, smaller lizards — omnivorous
fm.vs: Paler and LARGER than Fence Lizard. Desert habitat. Perches on JOSHUA TREES rather than fences.

lizards.hp: Desert-adapted — tolerates high temperatures. Perches on Joshua trees where it is the dominant lizard. Eastern LA County only.

lizards.desc: Large dark spiny lizard on boulder faces. Larger and darker than Western Fence Lizard. Males have intense blue belly and throat patches. Southern LA County rocky habitats.

fm.Size: 7–11 in total length — larger than Fence Lizard
fm.Color: Dark brown-black with darker crossbands; males with vivid BLUE belly and blue THROAT patch
fm.Habitat: Large boulders, rock outcrops, cliff faces — specifically on GRANITE surfaces
fm.Behavior: Basking on large rock faces; territorial push-ups; strong clinger to vertical rock
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, some plant material
fm.vs: LARGER and DARKER than Western Fence Lizard. On large BOULDER faces (Fence Lizard prefers fences, walls, logs). Males have blue extending to THROAT.
fm.Urban: Boulder outcrops in foothill communities — retaining walls, large rock gardens. Larger and darker than the ubiquitous Western Fence Lizard.

lizards.hp: Rock-face specialist — clings to vertical granite with strong claws. Larger territory requirements than Fence Lizard due to rock-face habitat distribution.

lizards.desc: Tiny delicate lizard clinging to desert shrub branches. Very long tail (2x body). So well-camouflaged on bark that it is nearly invisible. Desert washes.

fm.Color: Gray matching bark; very slender; extremely LONG tail
fm.Habitat: Desert washes — clings to branches of creosote, mesquite, palo verde
fm.Behavior: Arboreal on desert shrubs; extremely cryptic on bark; tiny and fast
fm.Diet: Small insects, spiders — gleaned from branches
fm.vs: Much smaller and more slender than any Sceloporus. Arboreal on DESERT SHRUBS. Very long tail.

lizards.hp: Desert shrub specialist — camouflage on bark is extraordinary. Rarely seen unless specifically searched for on desert wash shrubs.

lizards.desc: Small ubiquitous lizard of open rocky habitats. Males have three color morphs (orange/blue/yellow throats) that play a rock-paper-scissors mating game — one of the most famous examples of frequency-dependent selection.

fm.Color: Brown-gray with spots; dark BLOTCH behind front leg (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Open rocky ground, desert edges, coastal sage scrub
fm.Behavior: Active on ground level; faster and more nervous than fence lizards
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, mites
fm.vs: Western Fence Lizard is larger (6–9 in) with BLUE belly patches. Side-blotched has dark BLOTCH behind armpit and no blue belly.
fm.Urban: Common in undeveloped lots, hillsides, trail margins.

lizards.hp: Most abundant lizard in LA County — the baseline species against which all other lizards are compared for identification. Territorial males display with push-ups. Three throat color morphs (orange/blue/yellow) maintained by frequency-dependent selection — a textbook evolutionary ecology example.

lizards.desc: Small high-elevation fence lizard of the San Gabriel and San Bernardino mountains. Recently split from S. graciosus. Blue belly patches smaller than Western Fence Lizard. Found in sagebrush and open conifer woodland above 4,000 ft. Important indicator species for intact montane sage scrub.
lizards.hp: SGM montane specialist recently elevated to full species. Replaces S. graciosus in southern California mountains. Climate change threatens high-elevation habitat — monitoring this species is key to understanding montane ecosystem health.

fm.Size: 2-2.5 in SVL — smaller than Western Fence Lizard
fm.Color: Gray-brown with dark lateral band, blue belly patches smaller than Fence Lizard
fm.Habitat: Montane sagebrush, open conifer woodland above 4,000 ft — SGM and San Bernardinos
fm.Behavior: Basking on rocks in sagebrush clearings; push-up displays like Fence Lizard
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders — sit-and-wait predator
fm.vs: Western Fence Lizard is larger, lower elevation, bolder blue belly. Sagebrush Lizard is higher altitude, smaller, more gray.

lizards.desc: The most common lizard in LA County — seen on every sunny fence, wall, and rock. Males do push-ups to display bright blue belly patches. Their blood kills Lyme disease bacteria in ticks, reducing Lyme prevalence.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with dark wavy crossbars; males with bright BLUE belly and throat patches
fm.Habitat: Fences, walls, rocks, logs, fallen wood — everywhere sunny
fm.Behavior: Males do territorial PUSH-UPS on elevated perches; basking on warm surfaces
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, scorpions
fm.vs: Side-blotched Lizard is smaller (4–6 in) with dark BLOTCH behind armpit. Fence Lizard is larger with BLUE belly patches.
fm.Urban: The #1 most common urban lizard in LA. On virtually every sunny fence and retaining wall.

lizards.hp: LA County's most commonly seen lizard after Side-blotched. Blue belly patches and push-up territorial displays are iconic. Blood protein kills Lyme disease spirochetes in attached ticks — epidemiologically significant in reducing Lyme risk in western states.

lizards.desc: Fast desert lizard that runs on hind legs at top speed and curls tail forward to display bold BLACK AND WHITE ZEBRA STRIPES on underside. Speed runner of open desert flats.

fm.Color: Pale gray-tan above; underside of tail boldly barred with BLACK AND WHITE zebra stripes (displayed when running)
fm.Habitat: Open desert flats, sandy washes — needs bare ground for running. Antelope Valley.
fm.Behavior: RUNS BIPEDALLY at high speed; curls tail forward to flash zebra stripes; head-bobs and wrist-waves in courtship
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, small lizards, flowers
fm.vs: Only LA lizard that curls tail to display ZEBRA stripes underneath. Runs on hind legs at high speed.

lizards.hp: Bipedal running and tail display are anti-predator behaviors — the zebra stripes may confuse pursuing predators or signal that pursuit is futile.

lizards.desc: Sleek smooth-scaled lizard. Juveniles have vivid BLUE tails that distract predators — the tail breaks off and wriggles while the lizard escapes. Adults lose the blue color.

fm.Color: Adults: olive-brown, uniform. Juveniles: dark with bright BLUE tail and pale stripes (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Rocky hillsides, oak woodland, under logs and rocks
fm.Behavior: Secretive; smooth body slides easily through leaf litter
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders
fm.vs: Western Whiptail has rough GRANULAR scales and checkered pattern. Skinks have SMOOTH shiny scales.

lizards.hp: Autotomy — tail breaks off at fracture planes when grabbed. Detached tail wriggles for minutes, distracting predators while lizard escapes.

lizards.desc: Smooth glossy lizard with bold stripes running from head to tail. Juveniles have vivid BLUE tails — a predator-distraction mechanism identical to Gilbert's Skink. Adults lose the blue. Mountain oak woodland and chaparral.

fm.Color: Brown with broad dark sides bordered by pale cream STRIPES; juveniles with bright BLUE tail
fm.Habitat: Under rocks and logs in oak woodland, chaparral, rocky hillsides — SGM foothills
fm.Behavior: Secretive; smooth body slides through leaf litter; tail autotomy — blue tail wriggles as decoy
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, isopods
fm.vs: Gilbert's Skink has similar blue juvenile tail but is larger and less boldly striped as adult. Western Skink retains clear STRIPES into adulthood.

lizards.hp: Smooth scales reduce friction in leaf litter. Blue juvenile tail is a classic example of predator-distraction autotomy — detached tail wriggles for minutes.

lizards.desc: Coastal subspecies of Western Whiptail found in remaining sage scrub lowlands. Reduced striping compared to inland populations — adults more uniformly gray. Same rapid-darting, tongue-flicking foraging behavior. Declining with coastal sage scrub conversion.

fm.Color: Checkered pattern, reduced striping in adults
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral edges — lowland sandy substrates
fm.Behavior: Rapid darting bursts across open ground; constant tongue-flicking (chemosensory foraging)
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders — active patrol forager
fm.vs: Orange-throated Whiptail has bright ORANGE throat. Coastal Western lacks orange and has more reduced striping.

lizards.hp: Coastal lowland population declining with sage scrub habitat loss. Active forager on sandy substrates — patrols territory continuously rather than sit-and-wait hunting.

lizards.desc: Coastal subspecies of Western Whiptail inhabiting sage scrub and chaparral of the LA coastal lowlands. Adults are more uniformly gray than inland populations, with weakly defined or absent dorsal stripes. Fast-moving active forager of open sandy ground.

fm.Color: Adults more uniformly gray with reduced striping compared to inland populations
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral edges — open sandy areas in coastal lowlands
fm.Behavior: Same rapid darting behavior as other whiptails; active ground-level forager
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders — active patrol forager on sandy substrates
fm.vs: Western Whiptail inland has bolder stripes and checkered pattern. Coastal form is more uniformly gray.

lizards.hp: Coastal subspecies restricted to lowland sage scrub — habitat shrinking from development. Unstriped adults can be confused with juvenile alligator lizards, but the darting speed and tongue-flicking behavior are diagnostic.

lizards.desc: Blue-tailed whiptail with orange throat. Coastal sage scrub specialist. Species of Special Concern — declining with habitat loss. Southern LA County.

fm.Color: Dark with 6 pale stripes; bright ORANGE throat in adults; juveniles with BLUE tail
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral edges — open areas with sandy substrate
fm.Behavior: Fast active forager like other whiptails; tongue-flicking
fm.Diet: Insects, especially termites — active forager
fm.vs: Western Whiptail lacks orange throat. Orange throat + coastal sage scrub habitat diagnostic.
fm.Conservation: SSC. Declining from coastal sage scrub loss to development.

lizards.hp: Coastal sage scrub obligate — one of the most habitat-specific lizards in LA County. Declining with development of remaining sage scrub.

lizards.desc: Fast-moving striped lizard that runs in quick bursts across open ground. Constantly flicks its forked tongue — chemosensory like a snake. The fastest lizard in LA.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with light stripes and dark bars; checkered pattern
fm.Habitat: Open sandy areas, desert scrub, chaparral clearings, trail margins
fm.Behavior: Extremely FAST — runs in rapid darting bursts; constantly tongue-flicking
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, scorpions — active forager (not sit-and-wait)
fm.vs: Gilbert's Skink has smooth SHINY scales and blue tail. Whiptails have rough GRANULAR scales and checkered pattern.
fm.Urban: Common on sandy lots and trail edges in foothill communities.

lizards.hp: Active forager — unlike sit-and-wait fence lizards, whiptails continuously patrol their territory searching for prey.

lizards.desc: Small secretive lizard living under Joshua tree bark, fallen yucca, and debris. Live-bearing. Surprisingly common — hundreds per acre in good habitat — but almost never seen without looking under objects.

fm.Color: Olive-brown with small dark spots; soft velvety skin; vertical pupils
fm.Habitat: Under Joshua tree bark, fallen yucca, cardboard, debris — desert margins
fm.Behavior: Secretive; lives in family groups under cover objects; LIVE-BEARING; rarely ventures onto surface
fm.Diet: Termites, ants, small insects
fm.vs: Smaller and more olive than Granite Night Lizard. Soft skin + vertical pupils under desert debris.

lizards.hp: Among the most abundant lizards per acre — but almost never seen. Hundreds can occupy a single fallen Joshua tree.

lizards.desc: Flat-bodied secretive lizard living in rock crevices. Gives live birth (viviparous). Nocturnal — rarely seen without flipping rocks. Named for granite habitat.

fm.Color: Yellow-brown with large dark spots; FLAT body for rock crevices; vertical pupils
fm.Habitat: Granite outcrops, rock crevices — lives between exfoliating rock flakes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; extremely flat body squeezes into rock crevices; LIVE-BEARING (viviparous)
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders
fm.vs: Vertical pupils + flat body + rock crevice habitat. Only night lizard in LA County mountains.

lizards.hp: Viviparous — gives live birth, unusual among lizards. Extremely flat body is an adaptation for life in rock crevices.

lizards.desc: Large night lizard endemic to the Channel Islands — San Clemente, San Nicolas, and Santa Barbara islands. Much larger than the mainland Desert Night Lizard. Federally Threatened but recovering after removal of feral animals from island habitats. Live-bearing, long-lived (over 30 years).
lizards.hp: FT species recovering dramatically after island restoration. Feral cat and goat removal allowed populations to rebound. One of the longest-lived lizards in North America — individuals documented at 33+ years old.

fm.Size: 3-4.5 in SVL — much larger than Desert Night Lizard
fm.Color: Brown to gray with dark spots, large head
fm.Habitat: Channel Islands ONLY — rocky outcrops, cactus patches, shore debris
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides under rocks and debris by day. Very tame — tolerates close approach.
fm.Diet: Insects, marine invertebrates, seeds, flowers — omnivorous
fm.vs: Much LARGER than Desert Night Lizard. Island-restricted. Desert Night Lizard is mainland, smaller, associated with Joshua trees.
fm.Conservation: Federally Threatened. Recovering after island ecosystem restoration.

lizards.desc: Large chameleon from Yemen — pet trade escapes establishing small breeding populations in LA. The only chameleon breeding in the wild in California. Color-changing, independently moving eyes, projectile tongue.

fm.Color: Green with bands; can CHANGE COLOR; tall CASQUE (helmet) on head; zygodactylous feet; prehensile tail
fm.Habitat: Trees, shrubs — arboreal. Small breeding populations in several LA neighborhoods
fm.Behavior: Arboreal; independently rotating EYES; projectile TONGUE catches prey at distance; slow deliberate movement
fm.Diet: Insects, small lizards, plant matter
fm.vs: The ONLY chameleon in LA. Tall head casque, zygodactylous feet, color-changing, prehensile tail — utterly unlike any native lizard.

lizards.hp: Pet trade escape — cannot establish permanent populations in LA's climate but individual escapees survive months to years. CDFW tracks sightings. Striking appearance generates many iNat observations relative to actual population.

lizards.desc: Invasive lizard from the Caribbean rapidly spreading through urban LA. Males display bright ORANGE-RED throat fan (dewlap). Displacing native fence lizards in some areas. First detected in LA in the 2000s.

fm.Color: Brown with lighter pattern; males have bright ORANGE-RED dewlap (throat fan displayed in territory/courtship)
fm.Habitat: Urban vegetation, fences, walls, nurseries — spreading via nursery plant trade
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; males display DEWLAP; perches on low vegetation and fences; territorial
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders — competes with native fence lizards
fm.vs: Western Fence Lizard has BLUE belly and no dewlap. Brown Anole has ORANGE-RED dewlap and no blue belly.
fm.Urban: Spreading rapidly through LA — transported in nursery plants. Now established in multiple neighborhoods.

lizards.hp: Invasive — spreading rapidly via nursery plant trade. In Florida, has caused native Green Anoles to shift to higher perches. Potential similar impact on LA native lizards.

lizards.desc: Introduced chameleon-like lizard from the southeastern US. Changes color from bright green to brown. Males display a pink dewlap (throat fan) during territorial displays. Establishing in scattered LA locations — documented by NHM RASCals project.
lizards.hp: Introduced via pet trade. NHM Urban Nature Research Center tracking spread. Competes with native lizards. In southeastern US, displaced by invasive Brown Anole — may face same competitive pressure in LA from other introduced anoles.

fm.Size: 5-8 in total length including long tail
fm.Color: Bright GREEN changing to brown; males have PINK dewlap throat fan
fm.Habitat: Trees, shrubs, fences, walls — arboreal in suburban vegetation
fm.Behavior: Males bob head and flash pink dewlap. Color change responds to temperature, stress, social context.
fm.Diet: Small insects, spiders — arboreal hunter
fm.vs: Brown Anole (not yet in LA) is stocky and brown. Italian Wall Lizard is ground-dwelling. Green Anole is arboreal and changes color.
fm.Urban: Establishing in scattered neighborhoods. Report sightings to NHM RASCals iNaturalist project.

lizards.desc: Third species of introduced house gecko in LA — parthenogenetic (all-female, no males needed). Smaller and more slender than Mediterranean or Tropical House Geckos. Translucent pinkish skin. Hunts insects around porch lights at night.
lizards.hp: All-female parthenogenetic species — a single individual can found an entire population. Third Hemidactylus species in LA after H. turcicus (Mediterranean) and H. frenatus (Tropical). All three compete for the porch-light insect niche.

fm.Size: 4-5 in total length — smaller than other house geckos
fm.Color: Translucent pinkish-gray, no tubercles (bumpy scales)
fm.Habitat: Buildings, walls, fences near lights — strictly urban
fm.Diet: Small insects attracted to lights at night
fm.vs: Mediterranean Gecko has bumpy tubercle scales. Tropical Gecko is larger and chirps loudly. Indo-Pacific is smooth-skinned, translucent, silent.
fm.Urban: Around porch lights at night. All-female species — every individual can reproduce.

lizards.desc: Small translucent nocturnal gecko found on walls near porch lights throughout LA. Introduced from the Mediterranean. Audible chirping calls at night. The only commonly encountered gecko in urban LA.

fm.Color: Translucent pinkish-gray with dark spots; large EYES with vertical pupils
fm.Habitat: Building walls, fences near lights — nocturnal, attracted to insects at porch lights
fm.Behavior: NOCTURNAL; chirping calls at night; toe pads allow climbing vertical surfaces including glass
fm.Diet: Moths, flies, and other insects attracted to lights
fm.vs: Western Banded Gecko is native, banded, ground-dwelling, and found in wilder habitats — not on buildings.
fm.Urban: Thriving in urban LA — on walls of buildings throughout the basin. Listen for chirping at night near porch lights.

lizards.hp: Introduced from Mediterranean. Established throughout urban SoCal. Coexists with native lizards — largely partitions habitat by being nocturnal and wall-dwelling.

lizards.desc: Second introduced gecko species in LA — more tropical than Mediterranean Gecko. Aggressive — displaces Mediterranean Gecko where both occur. Loud CHIRPING calls. Spreading in warm urban areas.

fm.Color: Pale gray-tan with darker markings; more uniform than Mediterranean Gecko; belly partially translucent
fm.Habitat: Building walls near lights — same habitat as Mediterranean Gecko but more aggressive
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; loud CHIRPING calls; aggressively displaces Mediterranean Gecko; territorial near light sources
fm.Diet: Insects attracted to lights
fm.vs: Mediterranean Gecko has more distinct spotting and is less vocal. Tropical House Gecko is smoother and LOUDER.

lizards.hp: Aggressively displaces Mediterranean Gecko where ranges overlap. The most widespread gecko in the world — established in tropical and subtropical cities globally.

lizards.desc: European lizard with small colonies reported in scattered LA locations. Similar to Italian Wall Lizard but more variable coloring. Fast-reproducing invasive.

fm.Color: Highly variable — brown to green with dark markings; pale belly; sleek European lizard build
fm.Habitat: Urban walls, gardens, rubble — similar to Italian Wall Lizard
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, small invertebrates
fm.vs: Very similar to Italian Wall Lizard. Both are European Lacertidae established in LA.

lizards.hp: Second Podarcis species potentially establishing in LA. European wall lizards are among the most successful invasive reptiles worldwide.

lizards.desc: Introduced European lizard established in San Pedro and other LA neighborhoods. Green to brown with variable markings. Rapid reproducer — colonies expanding.

fm.Color: Green to brown with variable dark markings; pale belly; sleek European lizard build
fm.Habitat: Urban walls, gardens, rubble piles — San Pedro and surrounding areas
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; fast; basking on walls and rocks; rapid reproducer
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, small snails, plant material
fm.vs: More slender and elongated than native fence lizards. Green coloring (in some individuals) unusual for LA native lizards.
fm.Urban: Established colony in San Pedro since at least 2004. Spreading to adjacent neighborhoods.

lizards.hp: One of the most successful invasive lizards worldwide. Established in multiple US cities. San Pedro population growing and spreading.

lizards.desc: Small introduced lizard from Arizona establishing in scattered LA locations. Resembles a small fence lizard but is more slender with a longer tail. Males have blue-green throat patches. Tree-dwelling — prefers rough-barked trees and rock walls.
lizards.hp: Introduced from Arizona/Sonoran Desert. Establishing at scattered sites. Potential competitor with native Side-blotched Lizard and Western Fence Lizard in overlapping microhabitats.

fm.Size: 1.5-2.5 in SVL — smaller than Fence Lizard
fm.Color: Gray-brown, blue-green throat in males, irregular dorsal pattern
fm.Habitat: Rough-barked trees, rock walls — arboreal
fm.vs: Smaller and more slender than Western Fence Lizard. More arboreal than Side-blotched Lizard.

lizards.desc: Large robust gecko from the Mediterranean. Established in small colonies in LA. Bumpy skin with keeled scales. Larger and more robust than Mediterranean Gecko.

fm.Size: 5–6 in total length — larger than Mediterranean Gecko
fm.Color: Gray-brown with bumpy KEELED scales; large toe pads; broader head
fm.Habitat: Building walls, cliffs — similar to Mediterranean Gecko but less established
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; wall-climbing; audible calls
fm.Diet: Insects attracted to lights
fm.vs: Mediterranean Gecko has SMOOTH bumpy skin with small granular scales. Moorish Gecko has larger KEELED (ridged) bumpy scales and broader head.

lizards.hp: Second introduced gecko species in LA. Less established than Mediterranean Gecko. Monitor for range expansion.

lizards.desc: Introduced skink from East Africa establishing a population in the LA area. Juveniles have bright blue tails and five cream stripes — adults lose the pattern. Documented by NHM RASCals and under active monitoring for potential invasive impacts on native skinks.
lizards.hp: Introduced via pet trade. NHM monitoring population expansion. Potential competitor with native Gilbert's and Western Skinks. Reproduction confirmed in LA — not just one-off pet releases.

fm.Color: Juveniles: bright BLUE tail, five cream stripes. Adults: uniform olive-brown.
fm.Habitat: Rocky walls, rubble, garden debris — urban areas
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, small invertebrates
fm.vs: Native Gilbert's Skink also has blue juvenile tail but is stockier. Five-lined Skink has more distinct adult striping.
fm.Urban: Establishing in LA neighborhoods. Report sightings to NHM RASCals project.

lizards.desc: Large South American lizard — up to 4.5 feet. Pet trade escapes. NOT yet confirmed breeding in LA County but established in Florida and a watch-list species for SoCal. Would be a major invasive threat.

fm.Color: Black and white banding; heavy-bodied; strong jaws; forked tongue
fm.Habitat: Disturbed areas — if established, would occupy suburban edges and wildlands
fm.Diet: OMNIVOROUS — eggs, fruit, small mammals, birds, pet food — ecological nightmare if established
fm.vs: Much LARGER than any native lizard except Chuckwalla. Black and white banding. Forked tongue like a monitor.

lizards.hp: Watch-list invasive — established in Florida where it devastates native wildlife including sea turtle nests. Pet trade escapes in SoCal require monitoring.

snakes.desc: Beautiful slow-moving boa with rosy-pink longitudinal stripes. California's only native boa. Docile — almost never bites. Constricts prey. Found in rocky canyons and desert edges.

fm.Color: Three rosy-pink to rust LONGITUDINAL STRIPES on gray-blue background; smooth scales
fm.Habitat: Rocky canyons, boulder-strewn hillsides, desert edges
fm.Behavior: Slow-moving, docile; constricts prey; CREPUSCULAR/nocturnal; hides in rock crevices
fm.Diet: Small mammals, lizards, birds
fm.vs: No other LA snake has three LONGITUDINAL STRIPES of rosy-pink color. The only boa in California.
fm.Conservation: Collected for pet trade — protected by law. Do not collect or disturb.

snakes.hp: California's only native boa. Extremely docile. Heavy collection pressure for pet trade — populations declining near accessible areas.

snakes.desc: Small stout boa with blunt tail resembling the head — a two-headed snake defense. Extremely secretive — nocturnal, fossorial. At extreme range edge in highest SGM near San Bernardino County border. California's second native boa.

fm.Color: Uniform brown to olive; BLUNT tail resembles head (two-headed defense); small smooth scales; wrinkled skin
fm.Habitat: Mountain conifer forest above 5,000 ft — under logs, bark, in rocky crevices. Extreme eastern SGM only.
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; extremely secretive; FOSSORIAL; uses blunt tail as false head when threatened; constricts prey
fm.Diet: Small mammals, lizards, nestling birds — constricts prey in burrows
fm.vs: Rosy Boa has LONGITUDINAL STRIPES and is found at LOWER elevation. Rubber Boa is UNIFORM colored, at HIGH elevation, with BLUNT tail.
fm.Conservation: Threatened in California. Very restricted range.

snakes.hp: Blunt tail mimics head — when threatened, coils and presents tail while hiding real head. California's most endangered snake. At extreme range edge in LA County.

snakes.desc: Boldly banded black-and-white snake immune to rattlesnake venom — actively hunts and eats rattlesnakes. Also eats other snakes, lizards, and rodents. California's most famous snake. Harmless to humans.

fm.Color: Bold BLACK and WHITE (or cream) bands; some populations striped instead of banded
fm.Habitat: Diverse — chaparral, oak woodland, grassland, riparian, suburban edges
fm.Behavior: Active hunter; IMMUNE to rattlesnake venom; constricts prey including other snakes
fm.Diet: Snakes (including rattlesnakes), lizards, rodents, birds, eggs — ophiophagous
fm.vs: Banded pattern superficially resembles no venomous species in LA. The only black-and-white banded snake of this size.
fm.Urban: Common in foothill neighborhoods. Beneficial — eats rattlesnakes.

snakes.hp: Ophiophagous — eats other snakes including rattlesnakes. Immune to pit viper venom via modified blood proteins.

snakes.desc: Small nocturnal snake with dark neck blotches and vertical pupils (like a rattlesnake but completely harmless). Mildly venomous rear fangs for subduing lizards. Often mistaken for baby rattlesnake.

fm.Color: Pale gray with dark NECK BLOTCHES; VERTICAL pupils (unusual for non-venomous snake)
fm.Habitat: Rocky areas, desert margins, dry hillsides — under rocks during day
fm.Behavior: Strictly NOCTURNAL; slow-moving; rear-fanged but harmless to humans
fm.Diet: Lizards (primarily sleeping geckos), small snakes
fm.vs: Vertical pupils cause confusion with rattlesnakes, but Night Snake is SMALL (12–20 in), has NO rattle, and FLAT head without heat pits.
fm.Urban: Found in foothill neighborhoods under rocks and debris.

snakes.hp: Nocturnal specialist that hunts sleeping lizards. Vertical pupils are an adaptation for low-light vision, not an indicator of danger.

snakes.desc: Colorful gartersnake with red markings on sides between stripes. Rare in LA County — restricted to permanent water with dense riparian vegetation. Can eat toxic California Newts without harm.

fm.Color: Dark olive-black with red HEAD, three yellow-cream stripes, and red bars on sides between stripes
fm.Habitat: Permanent water with dense riparian vegetation — marshes, pond edges, stream banks. Very rare in LA.
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; semi-aquatic; resistant to tetrodotoxin — can eat California Newts that are lethal to other predators
fm.Diet: Frogs, tadpoles, fish, newts (IMMUNE to tetrodotoxin), worms, leeches — aquatic prey specialist
fm.vs: Two-striped Gartersnake has only LATERAL stripes (no center stripe) and no red. Red-sided has THREE stripes plus RED side markings.

snakes.hp: Tetrodotoxin resistance — one of the most famous examples of predator-prey coevolution. Can safely eat California Newts that contain enough toxin to kill multiple humans.

snakes.desc: Beautiful tricolored snake — red, black, and white bands. Mountain forests. Harmless — no coral snakes in California. Secretive and rarely seen. San Gabriel Mountains.

fm.Color: Bold RED, BLACK, and WHITE bands — tricolored like a coral snake
fm.Habitat: Mountain forests, rocky areas — SGM above 3,000 ft; secretive, under rocks and logs
fm.Behavior: Diurnal but secretive; rarely seen; constricts prey
fm.Diet: Lizards, small snakes, small mammals, bird eggs
fm.vs: No coral snakes in California — ALL tricolored snakes in LA are harmless. Red touches black (not yellow) in this species — but the rhyme is unreliable.

snakes.hp: Batesian mimic — tricolored pattern mimics coral snakes, but no coral snakes occur anywhere in California. Completely harmless.

snakes.desc: Large banded kingsnake — some taxonomists separate this from L. californiae. Powerfully constricts prey including rattlesnakes. Bold black and white/yellow bands.

fm.Color: Bold dark and light banding — geographic variation
fm.Habitat: Diverse habitats from coast to foothills
fm.Diet: Snakes including rattlesnakes, lizards, rodents, eggs
fm.vs: Taxonomy debated — some authorities synonymize with L. californiae.
fm.Urban: Foothill neighborhoods adjacent to open space. Beneficial — eats rattlesnakes. Sometimes enters garages and yards pursuing rodents. Do NOT kill — protected and beneficial.

snakes.hp: Powerful constrictor of other snakes. Rattlesnake venom immunity makes it an apex predator among LA snakes.

snakes.desc: Tiny secretive snake with sharp tail spine used to hold slippery slug prey. Coral-orange belly with black crossbars. Moist oak woodland.

fm.Color: Brown-gray above; bright CORAL-ORANGE belly with black crossbars; sharp tail spine
fm.Habitat: Under rocks and logs in moist oak woodland — cool, damp microsites
fm.Behavior: Secretive; active in cool moist weather (winter/spring); sharp tail spine anchors against slug prey
fm.Diet: SLUGS — specialist slug predator; uses tail spine to grip slippery prey
fm.vs: Ring-necked Snake has ORANGE RING around neck. Sharp-tailed Snake has no ring but has SHARP TAIL SPINE and coral belly with black bars.

snakes.hp: Slug specialist — the sharp tail spine braces against the slug's slime trail during constriction. One of the most slug-specialized snakes.

snakes.desc: Small nocturnal snake of desert habitats in eastern LA County. Recently split from H. ochrorhyncha (Coast Night Snake). Mildly venomous (rear-fanged) but harmless to humans. Dark neck blotches and vertical pupils are diagnostic.
snakes.hp: Recently split from H. ochrorhyncha based on molecular data. Desert populations differ from coastal populations in scale counts and coloration. Rear-fanged with mild venom effective on lizards and frogs — too small and rear-fanged to harm humans.

fm.Color: Gray-tan with dark blotches; dark neck patches; VERTICAL pupils
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, rocky areas — under rocks and debris at night
fm.Diet: Lizards, small frogs — rear-fanged with mild venom
fm.vs: Coast Night Snake (H. ochrorhyncha) is coastal/foothill. Desert Nightsnake is Mojave. Distinguished by range and genetics.

snakes.desc: Smooth glossy snake resembling a small gopher snake. Nocturnal. Sandy desert margins. Named for its exceptionally smooth, shiny scales.

fm.Color: Pale brown with darker brown blotches; very SMOOTH glossy scales (shinier than any other snake)
fm.Habitat: Sandy areas, desert scrub, grassland — nocturnal
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; burrows in sand; constricts prey; slow-moving and docile
fm.Diet: Lizards (especially sleeping lizards), small snakes, small mammals
fm.vs: Gopher Snake has KEELED (rough) scales and is diurnal. Glossy Snake has SMOOTH shiny scales and is nocturnal.

snakes.hp: Excavates sleeping lizards from sand — specialized nocturnal predator. The smoothest-scaled snake in LA County.

snakes.desc: Tiny highly variable desert snake — may be striped, banded, uniform, or any combination. Some mimic coral snakes. Under rocks in arid habitats. Harmless. Eastern LA County.

fm.Color: Extremely VARIABLE — orange, red, brown, gray; may be banded, striped, or uniform. Same population can have wildly different patterns.
fm.Habitat: Under rocks on dry rocky hillsides, desert scrub — secretive
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; secretive; fossorial; rarely seen on surface; harmless
fm.Diet: Scorpions, centipedes, spiders, insect larvae — small ground-dwelling invertebrates
fm.vs: Extreme color VARIABILITY within single populations is diagnostic. Tiny size + variable pattern + under rocks in desert.

snakes.hp: Most chromatically variable snake in North America — banded, striped, and uniform individuals can come from the same population. Genetic basis of color polymorphism is actively studied.

snakes.desc: Beautiful tricolored snake — red, black, and white bands (resembling a coral snake, though no coral snakes occur in LA). Fossorial. Nocturnal. Eastern LA County.

fm.Color: Red, black, and white/cream banding — TRICOLORED but NOT a coral snake
fm.Habitat: Desert grassland, sandy areas — eastern LA County
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal, semi-fossorial; rarely seen on surface during day
fm.Diet: Lizards, lizard eggs, small snakes
fm.vs: No coral snakes in LA County. The tricolored pattern is harmless mimicry.

snakes.hp: Tricolored pattern mimics coral snakes (Batesian mimicry) but no coral snakes occur in LA County. Harmless.

snakes.desc: Nocturnal rock-dwelling snake with V-shaped or lyre-shaped marking on head. Vertical pupils. Rear-fanged — mildly venomous but harmless to humans. Rocky desert canyons.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with dark brown blotches; V-shaped or LYRE-shaped marking on head; VERTICAL PUPILS
fm.Habitat: Rocky canyons, cliff faces, boulder piles — rock crevice specialist
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; excellent rock climber; rear-fanged but harmless to humans; constricts and envenomates lizards
fm.Diet: Lizards (especially sleeping geckos), small mammals, bats
fm.vs: Vertical pupils like Night Snake and rattlesnake, but has no rattle and head marking is LYRE-SHAPED.

snakes.hp: Lyre-shaped head marking and vertical pupils. Rock crevice specialist — hunts sleeping lizards and bats in cliff faces at night.

snakes.desc: Beautiful banded desert snake that literally SWIMS through sand using specialized flattened snout and countersunk jaw. Black, red/orange, and white bands. Harmless. Sandy desert flats.

fm.Color: White/cream with BLACK crossbands and RED/ORANGE saddles between some bands — tricolored
fm.Habitat: Sandy desert flats, dunes — SAND SWIMMER. Eastern LA County.
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; SWIMS THROUGH SAND using shovel-shaped snout and countersunk lower jaw; leaves no surface tracks
fm.Diet: Insects, scorpions, centipedes, buried insect larvae
fm.vs: Tricolored banding resembles coral snake but no coral snakes in CA. Shovel-shaped snout and sand-swimming unique. Countersunk lower jaw prevents sand entry.

snakes.hp: Sand-swimming specialist — the countersunk lower jaw and shovel snout are adaptations for locomotion through loose sand with zero surface trace.

snakes.desc: Semi-aquatic gartersnake of SGM mountain streams. THREE stripes (dorsal + two lateral) distinguish from Two-striped Gartersnake. Found at higher elevations than Two-striped.

fm.Color: Dark with THREE pale stripes — one down center of back + one on each side; dark spots between stripes
fm.Habitat: Mountain streams, meadows, ponds — SGM above 3,000 ft
fm.Behavior: Semi-aquatic; hunts in and near water; basks on streamside rocks; diurnal
fm.Diet: Frogs, tadpoles, fish, leeches, worms, slugs
fm.vs: TWO-STRIPED Gartersnake has only LATERAL stripes (no center stripe). Mountain Gartersnake has THREE stripes including CENTER dorsal stripe.

snakes.hp: Higher elevation than Two-striped Gartersnake — the two species partition stream habitat by elevation in the SGM.

snakes.desc: Very fast large snake. Rear body is braided like a coachwhip. Head is dark, body grades to reddish-tan. Aggressively defends when cornered but harmless. Among the fastest snakes.

fm.Color: Head dark brown/black grading to reddish-tan on body; braided scale pattern on tail
fm.Habitat: Open grassland, sage scrub, desert margins — needs open ground for speed
fm.Behavior: Extremely FAST — possibly the fastest North American snake; may climb bushes to survey
fm.Diet: Lizards, snakes, small mammals, birds, large insects
fm.vs: Striped Racer has cream SIDE STRIPES. Coachwhip is uniformly colored with gradation from dark head to light body.

snakes.hp: Speed hunter in open habitats. The braided tail scales that give it the name 'coachwhip' are a unique scale arrangement.

snakes.desc: Small secretive snake with bright ORANGE ring around neck and matching orange belly. Curls tail to flash orange underside when threatened — aposematic warning. Mildly venomous rear fangs but harmless to humans.

fm.Color: Dark gray-blue above; bright ORANGE ring behind head; orange-red belly with black spots
fm.Habitat: Under rocks, logs, bark, leaf litter — moist habitats in oak woodland and chaparral
fm.Behavior: Secretive; curls tail to display bright orange underside when threatened (aposematic)
fm.Diet: Salamanders, small snakes, lizards, slugs, worms, insects
fm.vs: No other small snake in LA has the combination of ORANGE NECK RING and ORANGE BELLY.
fm.Urban: Common under debris in gardens. Completely harmless.

snakes.hp: Mildly venomous rear fangs — effective on salamander and slug prey, completely harmless to humans. Very common but secretive.

snakes.desc: Large powerful constricting snake — the most commonly seen large snake in LA. Expert rattlesnake MIMIC: flattens head, hisses loudly, vibrates tail in dry leaves. Completely harmless. Excellent rodent controller.

fm.Size: 48–72 in — among the largest snakes in LA County
fm.Color: Yellow-tan with large dark brown BLOTCHES along back; dark line from eye to jaw
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, chaparral, oak woodland, agricultural areas, suburban edges
fm.Behavior: MIMICS rattlesnake: flattens head, hisses loudly, vibrates tail. Constricts prey. Excellent climber.
fm.Diet: Rodents (primary), birds, bird eggs, rabbits — most important rodent predator
fm.vs: Rattlesnake has RATTLE, TRIANGULAR head, VERTICAL pupils, HEAT PITS. Gopher Snake has NO rattle, ROUND pupils, NARROW head, no pits. NEVER kill a snake based on pattern alone.
fm.Urban: Common in suburban-wildland interface. Often enters garages and crawl spaces pursuing rodents.

snakes.hp: The most important rodent predator in SoCal. A single gopher snake eats hundreds of rodents per year. Protect — do not kill.

snakes.desc: Tiny snake with distinctive black cap on head. Lives underground — fossorial. Mildly venomous rear fangs effective on centipede prey. Harmless to humans. Under rocks in arid habitats.

fm.Color: Tan-brown body with BLACK CAP on head (diagnostic); white collar behind black cap
fm.Habitat: Under rocks, in rocky soil — arid hillsides, desert margins
fm.Behavior: Fossorial; nocturnal; rarely seen on surface; rear-fanged but harmless to humans
fm.Diet: Centipedes, centipede larvae — specialist predator
fm.vs: Black head cap is diagnostic. Only small snake with a distinct black cap. Harmless.

snakes.hp: Centipede specialist with mild rear-fang venom effective only on arthropod prey. Too small and rear-fanged to harm humans. Taxonomic relationship with T. planiceps under study — may represent the same species. Nocturnal and secretive, found under flat rocks.

snakes.desc: Small nocturnal desert snake with enlarged leaf-shaped nose scale for burrowing in sand. Dark blotches on pale body. Hunts sleeping lizards in their burrows. Eastern LA County desert.

fm.Color: Pale cream with large dark brown BLOTCHES; enlarged triangular LEAF-SHAPED nose scale (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Sandy desert — creosote scrub, Joshua tree woodland. Eastern LA County.
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; burrows using enlarged nose scale; hunts sleeping geckos and lizards in sand
fm.Diet: Lizards (especially banded geckos), lizard eggs — nocturnal specialist
fm.vs: Enlarged LEAF-SHAPED nose scale is unique among LA snakes. Pale with large dark blotches.

snakes.hp: The enlarged nose scale is used as a shovel for burrowing in loose sand. Specialist predator of sleeping banded geckos in their sand burrows.

snakes.desc: Sleek fast snake with a distinctive cream stripe along each side. Extremely fast — one of the fastest snakes in North America. Hunts by sight and speed, not ambush. Chaparral specialist.

fm.Color: Dark brown/black with prominent CREAM STRIPE along each side
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub, rocky hillsides
fm.Behavior: Extremely FAST; hunts by speed and sight; periscopes (raises head) to survey terrain
fm.Diet: Lizards (primary), snakes, small mammals, birds
fm.vs: Coachwhip is uniformly colored (no side stripes). Striped Racer has distinct CREAM LATERAL STRIPES.

snakes.hp: Visual hunter — among the fastest snakes in North America. Periscopes above brush to scan for prey. Chaparral specialist.

snakes.desc: Aquatic garter snake of streams and ponds. Species of Special Concern — declining due to habitat loss and competition from introduced bullfrogs and crayfish. The primary native aquatic snake of LA County.

fm.Color: Brown-olive with TWO pale dorsal stripes (no center stripe); dark spots between stripes
fm.Habitat: Streams, ponds, riparian corridors — always near water
fm.Behavior: Semi-aquatic; dives and swims when disturbed; active daytime near water
fm.Diet: Frogs, tadpoles, fish, leeches, worms
fm.vs: Mountain Garter Snake has THREE stripes (including center stripe). Two-striped has only LATERAL stripes.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Declining due to: 1) stream habitat loss, 2) introduced bullfrogs and crayfish, 3) urbanization of riparian corridors.
fm.Urban: Remaining populations in Malibu Creek, Santa Monica Mountains streams, upper LA River tributaries. Near water in suburban-wildland interface canyons.

snakes.hp: LA's primary native aquatic snake. Declining with native stream habitats. Competes with introduced species for prey.

snakes.desc: Small secretive snake with a distinctive black cap on head. Closely related to T. hobartsmithi (Smith's Black-headed Snake) — the two may be synonymized in future revisions. Found under rocks in coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Mildly venomous (rear-fanged) but harmless to humans.
snakes.hp: Taxonomic relationship with T. hobartsmithi remains under study — may represent geographic variation within a single species. Rear-fanged (opisthoglyphous) with mild venom effective on centipedes and scorpions. Too small and rear-fanged to harm humans.

fm.Color: Tan-brown body with SOLID BLACK cap on head; white collar behind cap
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral — under flat rocks on hillsides
fm.Diet: Centipedes, scorpions, insect larvae — specialist on venomous arthropods
fm.vs: Smith's Black-headed Snake has pointed black cap. Western has flatter cap. May be same species — taxonomy under review.

snakes.desc: Fast active snake with enlarged rostral (nose) scale used for digging. Pale with dark stripes. Dry brushy habitats. The enlarged nose patch is unique among LA snakes.

fm.Color: Pale gray-tan with dark dorsal stripe and pale side stripes; enlarged NOSE PATCH (large rostral scale)
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub, rocky hillsides
fm.Behavior: Fast active hunter; uses enlarged nose scale to dig in loose soil; diurnal
fm.Diet: Lizards, lizard eggs, small snakes, small mammals
fm.vs: Enlarged triangular NOSE PATCH is diagnostic — no other LA snake has this feature.

snakes.hp: Enlarged rostral scale is an adaptation for digging lizard eggs from sandy soil. Fast diurnal hunter of open brushy habitats.

snakes.desc: Fast slender snake — adults are uniform green-gray above with yellow belly. Juveniles have brown blotched pattern that fades completely with age. Fast active hunter in open habitats. Less common in LA than in NorCal.

fm.Color: Adults: uniform green-gray to olive above, YELLOW belly (no pattern). Juveniles: brown with dark blotches (pattern fades with age)
fm.Habitat: Open meadows, grasslands, chaparral clearings, woodland edges — needs open ground for speed
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; extremely FAST; hunts by sight and speed; curious — sometimes raises head to observe
fm.Diet: Lizards, small mammals, birds, insects, frogs — active visual hunter
fm.vs: Adult is UNIFORM olive with no pattern (unlike patterned gopher snake). Yellow belly. Juvenile blotched pattern fades entirely in adults.

snakes.hp: Active visual hunter — speed and eyesight distinguish hunting strategy from ambush predators like rattlesnakes. Uncommon in LA County.

snakes.desc: Tiny pink worm-like BLIND snake — the smallest snake in LA County. Eyes reduced to dark spots under scales. Fossorial — lives entirely underground eating ant and termite larvae. Often mistaken for an earthworm.

fm.Color: Pink to brown, shiny; vestigial EYES (dark spots under scales); blunt head and tail look identical
fm.Habitat: Underground — found under rocks, in ant nests, in soil crevices. Dry hillsides.
fm.Behavior: Completely FOSSORIAL; blind; found at surface after warm rains; often mistaken for worm; can't bite
fm.Diet: Ant and termite LARVAE and pupae exclusively — follows ant chemical trails underground
fm.vs: Only 'blind snake' in LA. Looks like shiny pink EARTHWORM but has scales and tiny dark eye spots. Blunt tail end mimics head for predator confusion.

snakes.hp: Among the most specialized snakes — eats exclusively ant and termite broods underground. Follows chemical pheromone trails to locate ant nests. Often found inside ant colonies.

snakes.desc: DANGEROUSLY VENOMOUS — the most toxic rattlesnake in North America. Mojave Desert species found in eastern LA County. Venom contains both neurotoxic (Mojave toxin A) and hemotoxic components. Greenish coloration and narrow black-and-white tail bands help distinguish from other rattlesnakes. ALL rattlesnake bites require immediate emergency care — call 911.
snakes.hp: Most potent venom of any North American rattlesnake — neurotoxic component (Mojave toxin A) can cause respiratory paralysis. Some populations have hemotoxic-dominant venom. CroFab antivenom is effective. Critical to distinguish from less-dangerous Speckled Rattlesnake in overlapping desert habitat.

fm.Color: Greenish-gray to olive with dark diamond-shaped blotches; NARROW black-and-white tail bands (white wider than black)
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, creosote flats, Joshua tree woodland — eastern LA County Mojave
fm.Behavior: Coils and rattles when threatened; ambush predator. Less defensive than Southern Pacific Rattlesnake — may not always rattle before striking.
fm.Diet: Rodents, lizards — ambush predator with heat-sensing pit organs
fm.Venom: NEUROTOXIC + hemotoxic — most dangerous rattlesnake venom in North America. Causes respiratory paralysis. Requires IMMEDIATE emergency care.
fm.vs: Southern Pacific Rattlesnake has WIDE black tail bands. Speckled Rattlesnake has granular speckling. Mojave has NARROW black tail bands and greenish tone.

snakes.desc: Large reddish-brown rattlesnake at extreme northern range edge in southern LA County. Larger and more reddish than Southern Pacific Rattlesnake. Relatively docile for a rattlesnake.

fm.Color: Reddish-brown to brick-red with diamond blotches; black and white banded tail near rattle
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral — southern LA County at northern range edge
fm.Behavior: Generally more DOCILE than Southern Pacific Rattlesnake; crepuscular/nocturnal
fm.Diet: Rodents, rabbits, lizards
fm.Venom: Hemotoxic — same treatment as Southern Pacific. Call 911 if bitten. CroFab antivenom.
fm.vs: More REDDISH and LARGER than Southern Pacific Rattlesnake. Range barely enters southern LA County.

snakes.hp: Heavy-bodied desert rattlesnake at southwestern range limit in LA County. Declining from habitat loss to development in remaining coastal sage scrub. Less commonly encountered than Southern Pacific Rattlesnake — most sightings in southeastern LA County foothills.

snakes.desc: The iconic sidewinding rattlesnake with horn-like scales above each eye. Moves by throwing body loops sideways across sand — leaving distinctive J-shaped tracks. Eastern LA County desert margins only.

fm.Size: 18–30 in — smallest rattlesnake in LA County
fm.Color: Pale tan matching desert sand; raised horn-like SUPRAOCULAR scales above each eye (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Sandy desert flats, dunes — ONLY in eastern LA County desert (Lancaster, Palmdale area)
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; SIDEWINDING locomotion — throws lateral body loops across sand; buries in sand ambushing prey; horn scales over eyes provide shade
fm.Diet: Lizards (primary), small rodents — ambush hunter buried in sand
fm.Venom: Venomous but less potent than Southern Pacific Rattlesnake. Still requires emergency treatment if bitten.
fm.vs: HORN scales above eyes + SIDEWINDING locomotion + small size. The only LA rattlesnake with horns. Desert sand habitat only.

snakes.hp: Only sidewinding snake in LA County — characteristic J-shaped tracks in sand. Horned scales above eyes reduce sand glare. Small desert rattlesnake with relatively mild venom compared to Mojave and Southern Pacific.

snakes.desc: The ONLY dangerously venomous snake in LA County. Found throughout hills and canyons. Responsible for virtually all snakebites in the region. Heat-sensing pit organs detect warm-blooded prey. Rattle warns before striking.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with dark blotched diamond pattern; RATTLE on tail tip
fm.Habitat: Rocky hillsides, chaparral, oak woodland, canyon trails — avoids developed areas
fm.Behavior: Primarily CREPUSCULAR (dawn/dusk); coils and rattles when threatened; ambush predator
fm.Diet: Rodents, lizards, birds; strikes and releases prey, then follows scent trail
fm.vs: Gopher Snake MIMICS rattlesnake by flattening head, hissing, and vibrating tail — but has NO rattle, ROUND pupils, and a narrow head. Rattlesnake has TRIANGULAR head, VERTICAL pupils, HEAT PITS, and RATTLE.
fm.Venom: Hemotoxic — destroys tissue and blood cells. Seek IMMEDIATE medical attention. Do NOT apply tourniquets or try to suck venom. Call 911. CroFab antivenom is standard treatment.
fm.Urban: Common in foothill neighborhoods adjacent to open space. Griffith Park, Santa Monica Mountains, San Gabriel foothills.

snakes.hp: Ambush predator using heat-sensing pit organs to detect prey in total darkness. Juveniles eat lizards; adults specialize on rodents — important rodent population control.

snakes.desc: Master of camouflage — color matches local rock exactly (granite gray, sandstone tan, volcanic brown). Desert canyons at eastern edge of LA County. Smaller and more slender than Southern Pacific Rattlesnake.

fm.Color: Highly variable — matches local ROCK color exactly; speckled pattern; lacks clear diamonds
fm.Habitat: Rocky desert canyons, boulder piles — eastern LA County
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; ambush hunter on rock ledges; excellent rock camouflage
fm.Diet: Rodents, lizards
fm.vs: Lacks clear DIAMOND pattern of Southern Pacific. SPECKLED pattern matches rock. Desert canyon habitat.

snakes.hp: Color-matches local rock substrate — populations on granite are gray, on sandstone are tan. Among the best-camouflaged snakes.

snakes.desc: Introduced watersnake from the southeastern US establishing in LA County waterways. Stout-bodied, semi-aquatic — hunts fish and frogs in streams and ponds. Non-venomous but aggressive when handled. Often mistaken for a cottonmouth/water moccasin (not present in California).
snakes.hp: Introduced via pet trade. Establishing populations in urban waterways. Major threat to native amphibians already stressed by bullfrogs and habitat loss. California has NO native watersnakes — any watersnake encountered is introduced and should be reported to CDFW.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with reddish-brown crossbands; belly patterned
fm.Habitat: Streams, ponds, urban waterways — semi-aquatic
fm.Behavior: Basking on banks and overhanging vegetation; aggressive when handled — flattens head and strikes
fm.Diet: Fish, frogs, tadpoles — aquatic predator
fm.vs: California has NO native watersnakes. Two-striped Garter Snake is slender with yellow stripes. Any stout watersnake is introduced.
fm.Urban: Establishing in LA waterways. Report sightings to CDFW. Non-venomous but will bite defensively.

snakes.desc: Popular pet snake — occasional escapes and small colonies reported in LA County. Beautiful orange/red with dark-bordered red SADDLE blotches. Harmless constrictor. Not definitively established as breeding.

fm.Color: Orange to reddish with dark-bordered RED SADDLE blotches; checkered belly; native to eastern US
fm.Habitat: Pet releases — reported from scattered urban/suburban locations
fm.Behavior: Crepuscular/nocturnal; constricts prey; excellent climber; docile
fm.Diet: Rodents, birds, bird eggs — constrictor
fm.vs: No native LA snake has this ORANGE + RED saddle pattern. Gopher Snake has BROWN blotches on TAN (not red on orange).

snakes.hp: Popular pet species — occasional escapes reported. Not confirmed as self-sustaining breeding population in LA County. Monitor for establishment.

snakes.desc: World's most popular pet snake — occasional escapes but NOT established in LA County. Cannot survive winter temperatures. Included to clarify that despite Florida Burmese Python concerns, pythons cannot establish in SoCal's climate.

fm.Color: Dark brown/black with tan/gold BALL-shaped blotches — distinctive pattern
fm.Habitat: NOT established — individual pet escapes only; cannot survive LA winter
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; curls into BALL when threatened (hence name); constricts prey; docile
fm.Diet: Rodents — constrictor
fm.vs: Heavy body + ball-shaped blotch pattern. No native LA snake looks like this. NOT established — individual escapes only.

snakes.hp: Cannot establish in SoCal — winter temperatures are too cold. Unlike Florida's Burmese Python crisis, LA climate prevents python colonization. Individual escapes are captured/rescued.

snakes.desc: Tiny introduced worm-like snake from Southeast Asia — the world's smallest snake species. All-female parthenogenetic reproduction — every individual can reproduce. Spread worldwide in potted plant soil. Often mistaken for an earthworm but has dry shiny scales and a flickering tongue.
snakes.hp: The most widely distributed snake species on Earth — spread through the global nursery trade in potted plant soil. All individuals are female (parthenogenetic). First documented in LA by NHM RASCals. Harmless — too small to bite humans.

fm.Color: Shiny dark brown-black, smooth scales
fm.Habitat: Soil, leaf litter, garden pots, compost — under flowerpots and debris
fm.Behavior: Burrows in moist soil; surfaces after rain. Entirely subterranean.
fm.Diet: Ant and termite eggs and larvae
fm.vs: Earthworms are segmented, moist, and lack scales. Southwestern Threadsnake (Rena humilis) is larger (6-10 in) and native. Brahminy is smaller, shinier, and always found in garden soil.
fm.Urban: Found in garden soil, potted plants, and compost. Spread via nursery trade. Completely harmless.

turtles.desc: LA County's ONLY native freshwater turtle — and it's in steep decline. Dark brown shell with yellow markings. Basking on logs in clean streams and ponds. Being displaced by introduced Red-eared Sliders throughout LA.

fm.Color: Dark brown to olive shell with pale yellow markings; pale throat
fm.Habitat: Clean streams, ponds, reservoirs with basking logs — requires undisturbed banks for nesting
fm.Behavior: Basking on logs; extremely wary — dives at slightest disturbance; nests on land
fm.Diet: Aquatic invertebrates, fish, algae, aquatic plants — omnivorous
fm.vs: Red-eared Slider (introduced) has bright RED ear patch. Pond Turtle has PALE yellow markings and no red.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Declining due to: 1) Red-eared Slider competition, 2) stream habitat loss, 3) predation by raccoons and coyotes on nests, 4) drought.
fm.Urban: Remaining populations in Malibu Creek, Santa Monica Mountains streams, upper LA River tributaries.

turtles.hp: SSC. Being displaced by Red-eared Sliders throughout LA waterways. Competition for basking sites is the primary mechanism — sliders are more aggressive baskers and reach higher body temperatures. Malibu Creek and upper San Gabriel River hold the strongest remaining populations.

turtles.desc: Federally Threatened desert tortoise at the extreme edge of its range in eastern LA County (Antelope Valley, Mojave Desert fringe). Can live 80+ years. Digs burrows used by dozens of other species.

fm.Color: Brown domed shell; elephantine LEGS with thick scales
fm.Habitat: Creosote bush scrub, Joshua tree woodland — Antelope Valley desert fringe
fm.Behavior: Digs burrows 3–30 ft deep; estivates in summer heat; hibernates in winter; can go a year without water
fm.Diet: HERBIVOROUS — desert wildflowers, grasses, cacti
fm.Conservation: Federally Threatened (FT), State Threatened (ST). Declining from: 1) habitat loss to solar farms and development, 2) Upper Respiratory Tract Disease, 3) raven predation of juveniles, 4) vehicle mortality.
fm.vs: No other tortoise in LA County. Distinguished from turtles by DOMED shell and elephantine LEGS.

turtles.hp: FT/ST. Keystone desert species — burrows provide shelter for 100+ species of invertebrates and vertebrates. Declining from Upper Respiratory Tract Disease, raven predation of juveniles, and habitat loss to solar development. Long-lived (80+ years).

turtles.desc: Large powerful turtle from eastern North America — pet releases in LA waterways. DANGEROUS — powerful bite can sever fingers. Long spiked tail and massive head. Cannot fully retract into shell — compensates with aggression. Largest freshwater turtle likely to be encountered in LA.
turtles.hp: Pet trade releases. Aggressive and dangerous on land — leave undisturbed. In water, generally docile and avoids humans. Apex aquatic predator that may impact native amphibians and fish. Report sightings to CDFW.

fm.Size: 8-18 in shell, up to 35 lbs — very large
fm.Color: Dark brown-black, rough ridged shell, long spiky tail, MASSIVE head
fm.Habitat: Urban ponds, lakes, slow streams — muddy bottoms
fm.Behavior: AGGRESSIVE on land — powerful bite. Do NOT handle. In water, buries in mud.
fm.Diet: Fish, frogs, birds, carrion — apex aquatic predator
fm.vs: No other LA turtle has the massive head, long spiked tail, and aggressive temperament. Cannot retract into shell.
fm.Urban: Pet releases in urban waterways. DANGEROUS — do not handle. Report sightings to CDFW.

turtles.desc: Striking brackish-water turtle from the Atlantic and Gulf coasts. Pet releases in LA — the only turtle in the guide naturally adapted to salt water (besides sea turtles). Distinctive diamond-shaped scute patterns and spotted skin.
turtles.hp: Pet release. Historically overexploited for soup — now protected across native range. Unusual among pet-release turtles in LA for being brackish-water adapted. May survive in coastal lagoons if populations establish.

fm.Color: Gray skin with dark spots; diamond-shaped scute patterns on shell
fm.Habitat: Coastal ponds, potentially lagoons — brackish-water tolerant
fm.vs: Only pet-release turtle in LA adapted to salt/brackish water. Distinctive spotted gray skin.

turtles.desc: Introduced eastern US turtle from pet releases. Named for map-like yellow lines on shell. Small-to-medium turtle commonly sold in pet shops. Basking groups visible on logs in LA park ponds alongside Red-eared Sliders.
turtles.hp: Pet trade releases establishing in LA waterways. Part of a growing assemblage of eastern US turtles displacing native Western Pond Turtle. Map turtles are more cold-tolerant than sliders and may spread further.

fm.Color: Brown shell with yellow map-like lines; yellow striped head
fm.Habitat: Urban ponds, park lakes — pet releases
fm.vs: Red-eared Slider has red ear patch. Map turtles have fine yellow lines on shell and head.
fm.Urban: Park ponds throughout LA — basking on logs with Red-eared Sliders.

turtles.desc: Large colorful turtle from Florida released in LA waterways. Adults develop dark shells but retain distinctive red-orange belly. Herbivorous — feeds primarily on aquatic plants. Can reach 12+ inches.
turtles.hp: Pet trade releases from Florida. Multiple Pseudemys (cooter) species now establishing in LA — all competing with native Western Pond Turtle. Herbivorous diet may impact native aquatic vegetation.

fm.Color: Dark shell; RED-ORANGE belly (diagnostic); red head stripes
fm.Habitat: Urban ponds, park lakes — pet releases
fm.Diet: Primarily aquatic plants — herbivorous (unlike omnivorous sliders)
fm.vs: Red-eared Slider has red ear patch and is omnivorous. Red-bellied Cooter has red belly and is herbivorous.

turtles.desc: Second map turtle species released in LA ponds. Yellow spots behind eyes distinguish it from False Map Turtle. Pet trade releases. Smaller than most other introduced turtles.
turtles.hp: Pet trade releases. Second map turtle in LA — both species from Mississippi River drainage. Accumulating introduced turtle species in LA waterways is displacing native Western Pond Turtle through competition for basking sites and food.

fm.Color: Brown shell with yellow markings; yellow spots behind eyes
fm.Habitat: Urban ponds, lakes — pet releases
fm.vs: False Map Turtle lacks distinct yellow spots behind eyes. Both map turtles smaller than Red-eared Slider.

turtles.desc: Colorful introduced turtle with red and yellow markings on shell edges and head. Small populations from pet releases in LA County ponds.

fm.Color: Dark shell with RED and YELLOW markings on edges; yellow stripes on head; red on plastron
fm.Habitat: Ponds, lakes — pet release populations
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — aquatic plants, insects, fish
fm.vs: Red-eared Slider has RED ear patch. Painted Turtle has red on SHELL EDGES and yellow head stripes.
fm.Urban: Pet releases in park ponds. Small colorful turtle with red/yellow shell edge markings. Less common than Red-eared Slider in LA.

turtles.hp: Introduced from pet releases. Small populations. Less established than Red-eared Slider.

turtles.desc: Florida endemic cooter released as pets in LA. Third Pseudemys species establishing in LA waterways. Similar to other cooters — distinguished by head stripe pattern and shell markings.
turtles.hp: Third Pseudemys cooter in LA — the accumulation of multiple cooter species illustrates the scale of the pet-release turtle problem. All three cooters plus sliders, map turtles, and softshells now compete with native Western Pond Turtle.

fm.Color: Dark shell, yellow-striped head with distinctive hairpin stripe pattern
fm.Habitat: Urban ponds — pet releases
fm.vs: Very similar to other cooters. Hairpin head stripe pattern distinguishes from River Cooter.

turtles.desc: The most commonly seen turtle in LA — invasive pet-trade release. Bright RED patch behind each eye is diagnostic. Basking in huge numbers on logs in parks, ponds, and lakes throughout LA. Displacing native Western Pond Turtle.

fm.Size: Shell 5–11 in length (larger than native Pond Turtle)
fm.Color: Green shell with yellow stripes; bright RED patch behind each eye (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Any standing water — park ponds, LA River, lakes, storm channels, golf course ponds
fm.Behavior: Aggressive basker; outcompetes native turtles for basking sites; prolific breeder
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — aquatic plants, invertebrates, fish, algae
fm.vs: Native Western Pond Turtle has PALE yellow markings and NO red ear patch. Slider has distinctive RED EAR.
fm.Urban: Abundant in every park pond and urban waterway in LA. Originally from southeastern US — released from pet trade.

turtles.hp: Most invasive turtle worldwide. Pet releases have established huge populations that outcompete native Pond Turtles for basking sites and food.

turtles.desc: Large introduced turtle from the eastern US. Brown shell with yellow C-shaped markings. Pet trade releases in LA ponds. Similar to other Pseudemys cooters establishing in urban waterways.
turtles.hp: Another pet-trade cooter joining the growing assemblage of introduced turtles in LA. All cooter species are primarily herbivorous and compete with native Western Pond Turtle.

fm.Color: Brown shell with yellow C-shaped markings; striped head
fm.Habitat: Urban ponds — pet releases
fm.vs: Distinguished from other cooters by shell pattern. All introduced Pseudemys are herbivorous large turtles.

turtles.desc: Eastern US box turtle released as pets in LA. Distinctive domed shell that closes completely — a hinge on the plastron allows the turtle to seal itself inside. Three toes on hind feet (most turtles have four-five). Terrestrial — prefers woodland leaf litter over water.
turtles.hp: Pet release. Only truly terrestrial turtle likely seen in LA besides Desert Tortoise. Box turtles are declining across their native range from habitat loss and collection — released pets in LA cannot establish viable populations due to low density.

fm.Color: Brown-olive domed shell with yellow/orange markings; three hind toes
fm.Habitat: Gardens, parks — terrestrial (unlike most LA turtles)
fm.vs: Desert Tortoise is much larger and desert-restricted. Box Turtle is small with a hinged closing shell.

turtles.desc: Tiny eastern US turtle — nicknamed 'stinkpot' for the musky odor it releases when handled. Pet releases in LA ponds. Very small — rarely exceeds 5 inches. Bottom-walker that rarely basks compared to other turtles.
turtles.hp: Pet release. Among the smallest turtles in North America. Unusual among LA turtles in that it rarely basks — walks along pond bottoms instead. Named for defensive musk secretion.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with two light head stripes; strongly domed
fm.Habitat: Urban ponds — bottom-dwelling
fm.vs: Much smaller than any other LA turtle. Rarely basks. Strong musky odor when handled.

turtles.desc: World's third-largest tortoise — pet releases in LA. Can reach 100+ lbs and 30 inches. Popular pet that quickly outgrows indoor enclosures — owners release them outdoors. Cannot survive cold wet winters long-term but individual escapees persist for years in LA's mild climate.
turtles.hp: Pet release — most commonly abandoned large tortoise in LA. Reaches 100+ lbs, lives 70+ years. Owners underestimate adult size when purchasing hatchlings. Individuals persist for years in LA yards but cold wet winters prevent true establishment.

fm.Size: 18-30 in shell, up to 100+ lbs — ENORMOUS
fm.Color: Sandy-tan shell, thick elephant-like legs with prominent spurs
fm.Habitat: Yards, parks — pet releases. Cannot establish but individuals persist.
fm.vs: Desert Tortoise is much smaller (8-14 in). Sulcata is massive with sandy coloring and leg spurs. Any tortoise over 16 inches in LA is almost certainly a released Sulcata.
fm.Urban: Most commonly abandoned large tortoise in LA. Contact tortoise rescue if found — do not release.

turtles.desc: Large introduced softshell turtle from the southeastern US — pet releases in LA County waterways. Much larger than the Spiny Softshell already in the guide. Flat leathery shell, long snorkel-like snout, and powerful jaws. Can reach 24 inches — largest softshell in North America.
turtles.hp: Pet trade releases. Aggressive — can inflict painful bites. Competing with native Western Pond Turtle for aquatic habitat. Largest softshell in North America — females reach 24 inches shell length.

fm.Size: Females to 24 in shell — MUCH larger than Spiny Softshell
fm.Color: Olive-brown leathery shell, dark bumps on carapace
fm.Habitat: Lakes, slow rivers, urban ponds — pet releases
fm.Behavior: Buries in mud with only snout exposed; powerful bite
