fm.vs: Spiny Softshell has spines on front edge of shell. Florida Softshell is larger with bumps, not spines.
fm.Urban: Pet releases in park ponds and urban lakes. Echo Park, various LA water features.

turtles.desc: Flat pancake-like turtle with leathery SOFT shell (not hard like typical turtles). Long snorkel-like nose. Fast swimmer. Introduced to Colorado River and some LA waterways. Can deliver a painful bite.

fm.Size: Shell 7–17 in (females much larger than males)
fm.Color: Olive-brown, flat, leathery SOFT shell; long pointed SNORKEL nose
fm.Habitat: Rivers, large ponds — sandy or muddy bottoms for burying
fm.Behavior: Buries in sand/mud with only snorkel nose exposed; extremely fast swimmer; bites painfully
fm.Diet: Aquatic invertebrates, fish, frogs — carnivorous
fm.vs: No other turtle in LA has a SOFT leathery shell and pointed snorkel nose.

turtles.hp: The soft leathery shell is unique among LA turtles. Introduced — native to central/eastern US.

frogs.desc: Federally Endangered toad of sandy stream bottoms. Breeds in shallow pools on sandy/gravelly bars in seasonal streams. One of the rarest amphibians in SoCal.

fm.Color: Pale greenish-gray with small dark spots; LIGHT patch between eyes
fm.Habitat: Sandy stream bottoms with shallow breeding pools — seasonal streams with sandy/gravelly bars
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; breeds in shallow pools on sandy streambeds; tadpoles in warm shallows
fm.Diet: Insects — primarily ants
fm.vs: Western Toad has pale DORSAL STRIPE. Arroyo Toad has LIGHT patch between eyes and is smaller.
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered (FE). Declining from: 1) stream habitat loss, 2) introduced bullfrogs and crayfish, 3) dam construction eliminating sandy breeding habitat, 4) off-road vehicles on streambeds.

frogs.hp: Requires sandy seasonal stream habitat for breeding — very specific habitat needs. Bullfrogs are a major predator of tadpoles and adults.
frogs.call: High-pitched musical trill lasting 4-10 seconds. Males call from shallow sandy pools in stream channels, Mar–Jul. One of the most endangered voices in LA County.

frogs.desc: Small toad of desert springs and rocky seeps. Tiny red-orange warts on gray body. Breeds in intermittent desert streams and tinajas (rock pools). Eastern LA County desert canyons.

fm.Color: Gray to olive with small RED-ORANGE warts (glandular — diagnostic); round parotoid glands (flat in other toads)
fm.Habitat: Desert springs, rocky seeps, tinajas (rock pools) — canyon bottoms in arid regions
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; breeds in temporary rock pools after rain; high-pitched trilling call; active night near water
fm.Diet: Insects, ants — active nocturnal forager near water sources
fm.vs: ROUND parotoid glands (other toads have elongated). Small RED-ORANGE warts on gray body. Desert canyon habitat.

frogs.hp: Desert spring specialist — breeds in tinajas (natural rock pools) in desert canyons. Round parotoid glands are unique among LA toads.
frogs.call: High clear trill lasting 4-10 seconds, similar to a cricket. Males call from desert pools after summer thunderstorms. Brief breeding season tied to monsoon rains.

frogs.desc: Large warty toad with pale dorsal stripe. Walks rather than hops. Mountain populations — found in SGM above 3,000 ft. Breeds in mountain ponds and wet meadows.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with WARTS; pale cream DORSAL STRIPE down center of back (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Mountain meadows, forests near ponds — SGM above 3,000 ft
fm.Behavior: WALKS rather than hops; nocturnal/crepuscular; breeds in shallow mountain ponds
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, worms, snails
fm.vs: No other large toad in the mountains. Pale DORSAL STRIPE is diagnostic.
fm.Conservation: Declining rangewide from chytrid fungus (Bd) and habitat loss.

frogs.hp: Mountain species declining from Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis (chytrid fungus). Breeds in shallow mountain ponds.
frogs.call: Soft birdlike peeping — a rapid series of mellow 'pip-pip-pip' notes. Males call from pond edges. Quieter than most LA frogs — easy to overlook.

frogs.desc: The classic 'ribbit' frog — this species provides the standard Hollywood frog sound used in movies worldwide. Small green or brown frog with dark eye stripe. Found in gardens with any standing water. Formerly Pacific Treefrog.

fm.Color: Highly variable — GREEN or BROWN (can change color); dark stripe through EYE to shoulder (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Any habitat near water — gardens, streams, ponds, swimming pools, rain gutters
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal chorus of 'RIBBIT' calls after rain (Nov–Apr); toe pads for climbing; hides in vegetation by day
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, worms
fm.vs: California Treefrog is gray/brown with no eye stripe and found only on boulders in streams. Baja CA Treefrog has EYE STRIPE and is found everywhere.
fm.Urban: The most commonly heard frog in LA — 'ribbit' calls on rainy nights from gardens, parks, and any standing water.

frogs.hp: The Hollywood frog — virtually all movie 'frog sounds' are recordings of this species. Found throughout LA wherever there's moisture.
frogs.call: Loud 'RIBBIT' — the classic Hollywood frog sound. Two-part rising call, repeated every 1-2 seconds. Males call from water edges and vegetation after rain, year-round in LA but peak Jan–Apr.

frogs.desc: Gray rock-matching treefrog found on BOULDERS in streams — camouflaged perfectly against granite. Quacking call sounds like a duck. Unlike Baja CA Treefrog, restricted to rocky stream habitats.

fm.Color: Gray to brown, heavily mottled to match GRANITE boulders; NO eye stripe
fm.Habitat: Rocky stream canyons — specifically on BOULDERS in and near streams
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; QUACKING call; clings to wet boulders; cryptic on granite
fm.Diet: Insects
fm.vs: Baja CA Treefrog has dark EYE STRIPE and is found everywhere. California Treefrog has NO eye stripe and is found only on STREAM BOULDERS.
fm.Conservation: Declining in some areas due to stream habitat loss and introduced predators.

frogs.hp: Rock specialist — camouflage against granite boulders is extraordinary. Found only in stream canyons with exposed rock.
frogs.call: Duck-like quacking — a rapid series of harsh 'WACK-WACK-WACK' notes. Very different from Pacific Treefrog's ribbit. Males call from rocks near streams in foothill canyons, Feb–May.

frogs.desc: Federally Threatened — the frog of Mark Twain's 'Celebrated Jumping Frog.' Once common throughout LA County, now EXTIRPATED from the region. Included for historical context and potential reintroduction efforts.

fm.Size: 3–5 in body — largest native frog in California
fm.Color: Brown-red with dark spots; RED on underside of hind legs (diagnostic); prominent dorsolateral ridges
fm.Habitat: Ponds, marshes, slow streams with emergent vegetation — EXTIRPATED from LA County
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; breeds in still or slow-moving water; long breeding season
fm.Diet: Insects, small invertebrates, small frogs
fm.Conservation: Federally Threatened. EXTIRPATED from LA County. Bullfrog predation and habitat loss are primary causes. Active reintroduction programs in other SoCal counties.
fm.vs: No native frogs of this size remain in LA County. Any large frog seen in LA is an introduced Bullfrog.

frogs.hp: Extirpated from LA County — the Mark Twain frog. Bullfrogs, habitat loss, and chytrid fungus eliminated all LA populations. Reintroduction efforts underway in neighboring counties.
frogs.call: Series of weak grunts — 'uh-uh-uh-uh' lasting 2-3 seconds. Very quiet compared to other ranid frogs. Calls from pond edges. EXTIRPATED from LA County — calls no longer heard here.

frogs.desc: Spotted green frog — historically present in LA County but now likely EXTIRPATED from the region like the California Red-legged Frog. Included for historical context. Any leopard frog seen in LA today is an introduced species.

fm.Color: Green with large dark LEOPARD spots with pale borders; prominent dorsolateral ridges; white belly
fm.Habitat: Ponds, marshes, wet meadows — LIKELY EXTIRPATED from LA County
fm.Diet: Insects, small invertebrates
fm.Conservation: Likely extirpated from LA County. Historical records from San Fernando Valley and other lowland sites.
fm.vs: Any leopard frog seen in LA today is almost certainly the introduced Rio Grande Leopard Frog (L. berlandieri), not the native species.

frogs.hp: Likely extirpated from LA County. Historical range included San Fernando Valley wetlands now developed. Native leopard frogs eliminated by bullfrog predation and habitat loss.
frogs.call: Snoring/chuckling followed by clucking — sounds like a creaking door mixed with finger rubbing a balloon. Calls from pond edges.

frogs.desc: FEDERALLY ENDANGERED frog endemic to the San Gabriel, San Bernardino, and San Jacinto mountains. Once common in high-elevation streams — now reduced to a handful of populations by chytrid fungus (Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis). Active captive breeding and reintroduction programs at LA Zoo and San Diego Zoo.
frogs.hp: Federally Endangered. One of the most endangered amphibians in North America. Chytrid fungus eliminated most populations. Remaining SGM populations are the focus of intensive conservation breeding. Every sighting should be reported to USFS/CDFW — critical monitoring data.

fm.Color: Brown-yellow with dark spots; YELLOW underside of hind legs (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: High-elevation streams and lakes above 4,000 ft — SGM, San Bernardinos
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; found on streamside rocks. Very wary — difficult to approach.
fm.Diet: Aquatic and terrestrial insects
fm.vs: California Red-legged Frog is larger, lower elevation, and has red hind legs. Yellow-legged Frog has YELLOW legs and is higher elevation.
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered. Active breeding programs at LA Zoo. Report ALL sightings to CDFW.

frogs.call: Rarely vocalizes — one of the most silent ranid frogs. Occasional short rasping call underwater. ID is visual, not acoustic.

frogs.desc: Explosive breeder — emerges from underground only during heavy rain to breed in temporary pools. Has a hard keratinous SPADE on each hind foot for digging backwards into soil. Can spend 10 months underground.

fm.Color: Gray-green with faint dark spots; VERTICAL pupils (cat-like); hard black SPADE on each hind foot
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, coastal sage scrub with seasonal rain pools — spends most of year UNDERGROUND
fm.Behavior: EXPLOSIVE breeder: emerges only during heavy winter rain; breeds in temporary pools; tadpoles develop rapidly (2–3 weeks)
fm.Diet: Insects, worms — feeds during brief above-ground periods
fm.vs: VERTICAL pupils distinguish from all True toads (which have horizontal pupils). Hard SPADE on hind foot is diagnostic.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Declining from: 1) loss of temporary pool habitat to development, 2) altered hydrology from urbanization.

frogs.hp: Spends 10+ months underground. Emerges explosively during winter rains. Tadpoles can complete development in 2–3 weeks — adaptation to ephemeral pools.
frogs.call: Short raspy snore — a brief 'WAAAH' repeated every few seconds. Explosive breeder — thousands emerge after heavy winter rains to breed in temporary pools. Choruses can be deafening.

frogs.desc: Tiny Puerto Rican frog with an unmistakable loud two-note 'ko-KEE!' call. Introduced to LA County via nursery plant shipments. Unlike all native LA frogs, eggs develop directly into froglets — no tadpole stage. NHM documented breeding populations in LA.
frogs.hp: Invasive in Hawaii where it has devastated native ecosystems. LA populations documented by NHM — status under monitoring. Direct-developing eggs (no tadpole) allow colonization of areas without standing water. Report sightings to NHM/CDFW.

fm.Color: Brown to tan, variable
fm.Habitat: Gardens, nurseries, greenhouses — arrived in potted plants
fm.Behavior: Males call loudly at night: 'ko-KEE!' — extremely loud for body size
fm.Diet: Small insects, spiders
fm.vs: No native LA frog has the two-note 'ko-KEE' call. Pacific Treefrogs say 'ribbit.' Coquí is unmistakable.
fm.Urban: Listen for the piercing 'ko-KEE!' call at night in gardens. Report to NHM RASCals.

frogs.call: Piercing two-note 'ko-KEE!' — extremely loud for body size (90+ decibels). Males call from vegetation at night. The 'ko' note is territorial, the 'KEE!' attracts females.

frogs.desc: Large introduced tree frog from the Caribbean. The largest tree frog in North America — up to 5.5 inches. Voracious predator that eats native treefrogs, lizards, and even small snakes. Sticky toe pads allow it to climb walls, windows, and utility poles.
frogs.hp: Highly invasive in Florida where it has devastated native treefrog populations. LA populations at early stage — control may still be possible. Secretes irritating skin mucus. Report sightings to CDFW/NHM immediately.

fm.Size: 1.5-5.5 in — MUCH larger than any native treefrog
fm.Color: Tan to gray-green, warty skin; HUGE toe pads
fm.Habitat: Buildings, trees, utility boxes — urban areas
fm.Diet: Native treefrogs, lizards, large insects — voracious predator
fm.vs: Much LARGER than native Pacific Treefrog. Warty skin and enormous toe pads. Report immediately.
fm.Urban: Found on buildings, in utility boxes, around lights. Report to NHM/CDFW — invasive species of concern.

frogs.call: Raspy squawking — 'WRAACK-WRAACK' — harsher than any native treefrog. Calls from buildings, trees, and utility boxes at night.

frogs.desc: Completely aquatic frog with NO tongue, webbed hind feet with black CLAWS, and flattened body. Introduced from Africa — escaped from laboratories and pet trade. Major carrier of chytrid fungus devastating native frogs.

fm.Color: Gray-olive, flattened body; small eyes on TOP of head; webbed hind feet with black CLAWS
fm.Habitat: Permanent ponds, streams, storm drains — completely aquatic, never leaves water
fm.Behavior: Completely AQUATIC; no tongue — uses hands to stuff food in mouth; rasping underwater call
fm.Diet: Aquatic invertebrates, tadpoles, small fish — scavenges anything in water
fm.vs: No native frog is completely aquatic with clawed hind feet and no tongue. Flattened body + dorsal eyes + claws unique.
fm.Urban: Established in streams and ponds throughout lowland LA. Originally from southern Africa.

frogs.hp: Completely aquatic tongueless frog from southern Africa. Primary global vector of Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis (Bd) chytrid fungus devastating amphibian populations worldwide. Laboratory and pet trade escapes established widespread populations in LA streams.
frogs.call: Underwater clicking/trilling — males call from beneath the water surface. Inaudible to most observers unless underwater recording equipment is used. Unique among LA frogs.

frogs.desc: Large invasive frog from eastern US — the most destructive introduced amphibian in western North America. Eats EVERYTHING: native frogs, snakes, birds, fish, mice. Deep 'jug-o-rum' call. Huge — can be over 8 inches.

fm.Size: 3.5–8 in body length — the LARGEST frog in North America
fm.Color: Green to olive-brown; large TYMPANUM (eardrum) behind eye — larger than eye in males
fm.Habitat: Permanent ponds, lakes, slow streams, golf course ponds — any permanent water
fm.Behavior: Deep 'JUG-O-RUM' call; sits at water's edge; leaps into water when disturbed; voracious predator
fm.Diet: ANYTHING that fits in mouth: native frogs, snakes, lizards, small birds, fish, mice, crayfish, insects
fm.vs: No native frog approaches this size. Treefrogs are tiny (1–2 in). Native frogs are much smaller.
fm.Urban: Abundant in every park pond, lake, and permanent waterway. Major cause of native frog decline.

frogs.hp: Most destructive introduced amphibian in western North America. Eats virtually anything that fits in its enormous mouth — native frogs, snakes, fish, birds, mice. Primary carrier of chytrid fungus in California. Eliminating native amphibian populations throughout the state.
frogs.call: Deep resonant 'JUG-O-RUM' — unmistakable bass bellowing audible at 0.5+ miles. The deepest frog call in LA County. Males call from large permanent ponds and lakes, year-round in warm weather.

frogs.desc: Introduced leopard frog established in a few LA County locations. Green with dark spots (leopard pattern). Native to Texas/Mexico. May compete with rare native frogs.

fm.Color: Green to brown with dark LEOPARD spots; pale dorsolateral ridges; white belly
fm.Habitat: Ponds, streams, ditches — permanent water
fm.Diet: Insects, small invertebrates
fm.vs: No native leopard frogs currently in LA County (California Red-legged Frog is extirpated). Any leopard frog in LA is introduced.

frogs.hp: Introduced. No native leopard frogs remain in LA County — the California Red-legged Frog has been extirpated. Any leopard frog seen in LA is introduced.
frogs.call: Short guttural trill — similar to but shorter than Bullfrog. Males call from pond edges. Less commonly heard than Bullfrog in LA.

salamanders.desc: Climbing salamander with prehensile tail that lives in tree hollows, rock crevices, and woodrat nests. Large triangular head with strong jaw muscles — can deliver a painful bite. Lungless.

fm.Color: Brown with small yellow spots; large TRIANGULAR head
fm.Habitat: Tree hollows, rock crevices, under bark, woodrat nests — arboreal
fm.Behavior: CLIMBS trees and rock faces using prehensile tail and toe pads; bites when handled; no lungs
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, isopods
fm.vs: Large triangular head distinguishes from slender salamanders. The only tree-climbing salamander in LA.
fm.Urban: Found in mature oaks in parks. Look in tree hollows.

salamanders.hp: Only truly arboreal salamander in western North America. Prehensile tail grips branches. Powerful jaw muscles can bite hard enough to draw blood — unique among lungless salamanders. Communal egg-laying in tree hollows and rock crevices.

salamanders.desc: Worm-like lungless salamander found under rocks and logs in moist habitats. Breathes entirely through its SKIN. No lungs. Very common in foothill gardens but rarely seen — requires flipping rocks.

fm.Color: Dark brown to black dorsally; dark BELLY; tiny legs
fm.Habitat: Under rocks, logs, leaf litter in moist oak woodland and shady gardens
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; surface-active only when ground is moist; hides under objects during day; NO lungs — breathes through SKIN
fm.Diet: Tiny invertebrates — mites, springtails, small insects
fm.vs: Garden Slender Salamander has PALE belly. Black-bellied has DARK belly. Both are worm-like with tiny legs.
fm.Urban: Common under rocks and potted plants in foothill gardens. Requires moisture.

salamanders.hp: Lungless (Plethodontidae) — gas exchange entirely through skin. Must stay moist. Most diverse salamander family in North America.

salamanders.desc: Endemic to the Channel Islands — one of very few vertebrates unique to the islands. Found on Santa Cruz, Santa Rosa, Anacapa, and San Miguel islands.

fm.Color: Dark brown, slender, worm-like
fm.Habitat: Under rocks and logs on Channel Islands
fm.Diet: Tiny invertebrates
fm.Conservation: Island endemic — limited range makes it vulnerable
fm.vs: Channel Islands endemic. Larger than mainland Garden Slender Salamander. Range does not overlap with other Batrachoseps.
fm.Behavior: Found under rocks and debris; surface-active after rain on islands

salamanders.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Limited to a few islands — one of the most geographically restricted vertebrates in SoCal.

salamanders.desc: Famous ring species — populations encircle the Central Valley with varying colors, from uniform brown to boldly banded. LA populations are mostly brown with orange legs. Constricted tail base stores fat.

fm.Color: Brown with ORANGE legs and tail base; constricted tail base (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, shady canyons — under logs, rocks, leaf litter
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; tail constriction stores fat; can autotomize tail; no lungs
fm.Diet: Insects, spiders, worms
fm.vs: Constricted TAIL BASE is diagnostic — no other LA salamander has this. Orange legs distinguish from slender salamanders.

salamanders.hp: Classic ring species — populations encircle California's Central Valley with a cline of color variation. LA populations intergrade between subspecies.

salamanders.desc: The most common salamander in LA gardens — found under any rock, board, or pot in moist conditions. Worm-like with tiny legs. Breathes through skin. Active on rainy nights.

fm.Color: Brown-gray with PALE belly; tiny legs; worm-like
fm.Habitat: Under rocks, boards, pots, leaf litter in gardens and oak woodland — any moist spot
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; surface-active on rainy nights; hides under objects during day; coils when disturbed
fm.Diet: Tiny invertebrates — springtails, mites, small insects
fm.vs: Black-bellied Slender Salamander has DARK belly. Garden Slender has PALE belly.
fm.Urban: The most commonly encountered salamander in LA — found in virtually every garden with moisture and hiding places.

salamanders.hp: Lungless salamander breathing entirely through skin. Most common amphibian in LA gardens — turn over any rock or pot on a rainy day.

salamanders.desc: Robust slender salamander at extreme range edge in northern LA County mountains. Larger and stockier than other Batrachoseps. High-elevation springs and seeps.

fm.Size: 4–6 in — stockier than other slender salamanders
fm.Color: Dark brown; stockier build than typical slender salamanders
fm.Habitat: High-elevation springs and seeps — at range edge in northernmost LA County mountains
fm.Diet: Invertebrates in moist soil and leaf litter
fm.vs: Kern Plateau endemic — does not overlap with other Batrachoseps in range. Stockier build than other slender salamanders.
fm.Behavior: Surface-active after rain; hides under rocks and logs in pine/fir woodland

salamanders.hp: High-elevation SGM endemic at southern range edge. Survives under bark and in rock crevices in mixed conifer forest. May be isolated from northern populations — genetic studies ongoing. Climate change threatens upslope habitat compression.

salamanders.desc: ENDEMIC to the San Gabriel Mountains — one of the most geographically restricted vertebrates in California. Known from only a handful of locations in moist rocky canyons above 4,000 ft. Lungless. Extremely rare.

fm.Color: Dark brown to black; slender; tiny legs; worm-like
fm.Habitat: Moist rocky canyons in SGM above 4,000 ft — under rocks and logs near springs
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; surface-active only when moist; extremely restricted range
fm.Diet: Tiny invertebrates — springtails, mites
fm.vs: Indistinguishable from Black-bellied Slender Salamander in field — requires genetic confirmation. Range restricted to high SGM.
fm.Conservation: Extremely limited range — known from fewer than 10 localities. Climate change and fire are major threats.

salamanders.hp: One of the most geographically restricted vertebrates in California. SGM ENDEMIC — known from fewer than 10 sites. Described in 1996. Climate change threatening high-elevation moist habitat.

salamanders.desc: Orange-bellied newt that migrates to streams and ponds to breed after winter rains. Skin contains TETRODOTOXIN — the same lethal nerve toxin as puffer fish. Extremely toxic if eaten. The bright orange belly is a warning.

fm.Color: Brown above; bright ORANGE belly (aposematic warning); rough warty skin
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, chaparral near streams — migrates to water for breeding
fm.Behavior: Walks openly (toxic — no need to hide); mass migrations across roads to breeding ponds; curls to flash orange belly when threatened
fm.Diet: Worms, insects, snails, aquatic invertebrates
fm.vs: No other LA salamander has an ORANGE BELLY. The orange warns predators of extreme toxicity.
fm.Venom: Skin contains TETRODOTOXIN — lethal nerve poison. Do NOT handle then touch eyes/mouth. Wash hands after any contact. A single newt contains enough toxin to kill several adult humans if eaten.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Declining from: 1) introduced crayfish eating eggs and larvae, 2) road mortality during migrations, 3) stream habitat loss.

salamanders.hp: Contains tetrodotoxin — same toxin as puffer fish. Road mortality during breeding migrations is a major threat. Newt tunnels under roads have been installed in some areas.

mammals.desc: North America's fastest land animal — capable of 55 mph. Historically common in Antelope Valley (named for them). Now rare/extirpated from most of LA County. Unique pronged horns on both sexes. Not a true antelope — sole surviving member of family Antilocapridae.
mammals.hp: Antelope Valley was named for pronghorn herds that once roamed the western Mojave. Now functionally extirpated from LA County — occasional sightings may be dispersers from Kern County or Edwards AFB populations. Fastest land animal in the Western Hemisphere.

fm.Color: Tan-brown with white underside and rump; white cheek patches; PRONGED horns
fm.Habitat: Open grassland, sagebrush flats — historically Antelope Valley
fm.Behavior: Fastest land animal in Western Hemisphere (55 mph). Herding species.
fm.vs: Mule Deer is larger, forest-dwelling, with antlers not horns. Pronghorn has white rump, pronged horns, and is open-grassland only.

mammals.desc: Desert bighorn sheep in the San Gabriel Mountains and eastern desert margins. Impressive curved horns on rams. Declining from disease, habitat fragmentation, and mountain lion predation.

fm.Color: Brown-gray with pale rump patch; rams have massive CURVED HORNS
fm.Habitat: Steep rocky mountain terrain — SGM, San Bernardino Mountains
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; rams CLASH horns during rut (spectacular); excellent cliff climbers; bands segregated by sex most of year
fm.Diet: Grasses, forbs, shrubs — herbivorous browser/grazer
fm.vs: Only large wild sheep in LA mountains. Massive curved horns on rams are unmistakable.
fm.Conservation: SSC. San Gabriel Mountains population declining from respiratory disease (pneumonia), mountain lion predation, and recreational disturbance.

mammals.hp: San Gabriel Mountains herd is one of the southernmost populations. Respiratory disease from domestic sheep contact is a major threat.

mammals.desc: LA's most visible large predator — thriving in every neighborhood from downtown to the mountains. Extremely adaptable. Primarily nocturnal in urban areas, more diurnal in wildlands. Yipping chorus heard nightly across LA.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with rust-colored legs; bushy tail held DOWN when running (vs up in dogs/wolves)
fm.Habitat: EVERYWHERE — urban neighborhoods, parks, golf courses, industrial areas, mountains, beaches
fm.Behavior: Crepuscular/nocturnal in urban areas; yipping chorus at dusk/night; monogamous pairs; dens in hillside burrows
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — rodents, rabbits, fruit, pet food, garbage, occasional small pets, insects
fm.vs: Distinguished from domestic dogs by: tail held DOWN when running, pointed muzzle, large erect ears, nervous demeanor. Smaller and lankier than wolves.
fm.Urban: The quintessential LA urban carnivore. LA Urban Coyote Project documents their behavior in every LA neighborhood.

mammals.hp: LA's most successful large predator — thrives from downtown to mountain wilderness. The LA Urban Coyote Project documents coyote ecology in one of the world's largest cities. Hazing programs help manage human-coyote conflict in residential areas.

mammals.desc: The only canid that regularly CLIMBS TREES. Grizzled gray with rust-red sides. More secretive than coyotes. Ancient lineage — not closely related to True foxes (Vulpes).

fm.Color: Grizzled gray with RUST-RED sides and neck; black stripe along tail top; white throat/belly
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, oak woodland, riparian corridors — more arboreal than other canids
fm.Behavior: CLIMBS TREES regularly (only canid that does); nocturnal; more secretive than coyotes
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — rodents, rabbits, insects, fruit (especially manzanita berries, figs, cactus fruit)
fm.vs: Coyote is larger, lankier, gray-brown without rust-red sides, and does NOT climb trees.
fm.Urban: Less visible than coyotes. Camera traps show them in foothill canyons and the Santa Monica Mountains.

mammals.hp: Only canid worldwide that regularly climbs trees — unique rotating wrists enable trunk ascent. Basal canid lineage, not closely related to other foxes. Declining in urban LA as coyotes expand — coyotes dominate in open habitats while gray foxes persist in dense brush.

mammals.desc: Miniature fox endemic to the Channel Islands — smallest fox in North America. Six subspecies, one per island. Recovered from near-extinction in one of the fastest endangered species recoveries in US history. Catalina Island population accessible to visitors.

fm.Size: 18–20 in body; 3–6 lbs — size of a house cat
fm.Color: Gray with rust-red sides; short legs; smaller than mainland Gray Fox
fm.Habitat: Channel Islands only — all habitat types from beaches to ridge tops
fm.Behavior: Active day and night; bold around humans on islands; omnivorous; pairs bond for life
fm.Diet: Insects, fruit (especially island cherry), mice, birds, lizards — extremely omnivorous
fm.vs: Much SMALLER than mainland Gray Fox. Island-restricted. Bold around humans.
fm.Conservation: FE (delisted 2016). Recovered from 15 individuals (San Miguel) to healthy populations. Golden eagle predation + canine distemper nearly caused extinction.

mammals.hp: Fastest mammal recovery under ESA — from near-extinction to delisting in ~15 years. Catalina Island population easily seen by visitors.

mammals.desc: Small native desert fox of the Antelope Valley and western Mojave. Smallest canid in North America — only 3-6 lbs. Enormous ears for heat dissipation and acute hearing. Nocturnal burrow-dweller. Declining from habitat loss, rodenticide, and coyote predation.
mammals.hp: Antelope Valley populations declining from urban development, rodenticide poisoning (secondary — from eating poisoned rodents), and coyote predation. The San Joaquin Kit Fox subspecies is federally endangered — LA County populations are not listed but face similar threats.

fm.Size: 15-20 in body, 3-6 lbs — VERY small fox
fm.Color: Tan-gray with white belly; ENORMOUS ears
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, grassland — Antelope Valley, western Mojave
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; dens in burrows during day; monogamous pairs
fm.Diet: Rodents (kangaroo rats, mice), rabbits, insects
fm.vs: Gray Fox is larger and climbs trees. Red Fox is larger and reddish. Kit Fox is tiny with enormous ears and strictly desert.

mammals.desc: Large-eared deer of LA's hills and mountains. Distinctive BOUNDING gait — all four feet leave ground simultaneously (stotting/pronking). Primary prey of mountain lions.

fm.Color: Gray-brown; large MULE-like ears; white rump patch; black-tipped tail; antlers on males (branching, not tined)
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, oak woodland, mountain forests, suburban-wildland edges
fm.Behavior: BOUNDING gait (all 4 feet simultaneously); crepuscular; bucks grow/shed antlers annually; fawns spotted for camouflage
fm.Diet: Browse — shrubs, leaves, acorns, forbs; significant garden damage near wildlands
fm.vs: No other deer species in LA County.
fm.Urban: Common in foothill neighborhoods — significant garden browsing conflict.

mammals.hp: Primary prey of mountain lions. Population size directly affects mountain lion territory sizes and reproductive success.

mammals.desc: Large woodrat with enormous ears — the largest-eared rodent in LA. Builds stick houses like Dusky-footed Woodrat but in rockier habitats. Chaparral and rocky slopes.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with white belly and throat; very LARGE EARS
fm.Habitat: Rocky chaparral, cliff bases — builds stick houses in/near rock crevices
fm.Diet: Leaves, seeds, fruit, cacti
fm.vs: Larger ears than Dusky-footed Woodrat. Rockier habitat. Both build stick houses.

mammals.hp: Stick houses (middens) in rock crevices are important shelter for many other species. The enormous ears may aid thermoregulation.

mammals.desc: Desert mouse with very long tail and large ears. Rocky desert habitats. Tolerates extreme aridity — can survive without drinking water.

fm.Color: Pale gray-brown above, white below; very LONG tail; large ears; large eyes
fm.Habitat: Rocky desert areas, coastal sage scrub — arid habitats with rock crevices
fm.Diet: Seeds, insects, green vegetation
fm.vs: Longer tail than Deer Mouse. Paler overall. Desert/rocky habitat.
fm.Urban: Rocky hillsides and coastal sage scrub in foothill communities. Survives without drinking water. Long tail and large ears distinguish from House Mouse.

mammals.hp: Survives without drinking water — metabolic water from seed digestion is sufficient. Desert aridification specialist.

mammals.desc: Largest Peromyscus in North America. Monogamous — unusual for mice. Pairs bond for life and both parents raise young. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral.

fm.Size: 9–11 in with tail — largest native mouse
fm.Color: Brown above, white below; large ears; long tail
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral — needs dense brush
fm.Behavior: MONOGAMOUS — pairs bond for life; biparental care (both parents raise young); territorial
fm.Diet: Seeds, arthropods, fungi
fm.vs: Larger than all other Peromyscus. Monogamous pair-bonding unusual for mice.
fm.Urban: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral near suburban edges. The largest native mouse — monogamous pairs in dense brush near developments.

mammals.hp: Monogamous pair-bonding is unique among LA mice. Both parents actively raise young — male involvement in pup care is rare in rodents.

mammals.desc: Small stocky rodent making runways through grass. Creates visible trails and tunnel systems in meadow grass. Major prey for raptors and snakes. Population irruptions and crashes.

fm.Color: Brown-gray; short tail; small ears partly hidden in fur; stocky build
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, meadows, marshes — needs dense grass cover
fm.Behavior: Active day and night; creates RUNWAYS (visible trails) through grass; population boom-bust cycles
fm.Diet: Grasses, seeds, roots — herbivorous
fm.vs: Mice have large EARS and LONG tails. Voles have small ears and SHORT tails and stocky builds.
fm.Urban: Common in grasslands and open space preserves.

mammals.hp: Keystone prey species — population irruptions support boom in raptors, snakes, and predators. Crash years cascade through food web.

mammals.desc: Smaller desert counterpart of Dusky-footed Woodrat. Builds stick houses in desert scrub, often in Joshua trees and cholla cactus. Uses cactus spines as home defense.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with white belly and throat; large ears; FURRED tail (not naked like Rattus)
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, Joshua tree woodland, cholla patches — builds stick houses in cactus and rock crevices
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; builds elaborate houses decorated with CACTUS SPINES, bones, glass, and shiny objects
fm.Diet: Cactus pads, seeds, leaves, fruit — can metabolize oxalic acid in cactus
fm.vs: Smaller than Dusky-footed Woodrat. Desert habitat. Houses built IN cactus clumps as spine defense.
fm.Urban: Desert scrub foothill margins. Builds stick houses in Joshua tree woodland and cholla patches. Houses decorated with cactus spines, bones, bottle caps, and foil.

mammals.hp: Incorporates cholla cactus joints around house entrance as predator defense. Can eat cactus — metabolizes oxalic acid that is toxic to most mammals.

mammals.desc: Pack rat that builds enormous stick houses (middens) up to 6 ft tall. The stick houses are multi-generational, lasting decades, and house dozens of other species including insects, lizards, and snakes.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with white belly and throat; large ears; dark-topped dusky feet
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, chaparral — builds large STICK HOUSES (middens)
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; builds elaborate stick houses up to 6 ft tall; collects shiny objects; solitary
fm.Diet: Leaves, nuts, seeds, fungi, berries — primarily vegetarian
fm.vs: Roof Rat (Rattus rattus) has scaly NAKED tail. Woodrat has FURRED tail and larger ears.
fm.Urban: Common in foothill canyons. Stick houses are conspicuous on hillsides.

mammals.hp: Stick houses (middens) are keystone structures — providing shelter for 100+ other species including salamanders, snakes, spiders, and insects.

mammals.desc: Native mouse of forests and grasslands — large eyes, large ears, bicolored tail. Carrier of Hantavirus (Sin Nombre virus) — avoid disturbing rodent droppings in enclosed spaces. Most common native mouse.

fm.Color: Brown above, sharply WHITE below; BICOLORED tail (dark above, white below); large eyes and ears
fm.Habitat: Everywhere — forests, grasslands, chaparral, buildings, cabins
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; agile climber; enters buildings; caches seeds
fm.Diet: Seeds, nuts, insects, fruit
fm.vs: House Mouse (Mus musculus) is gray-brown, uniformly colored, with NAKED tail. Deer Mouse has BICOLORED tail and sharp brown/white contrast.
fm.Urban: Common in foothill homes, cabins, and outbuildings. CAUTION: carrier of Hantavirus — wear N95 mask when cleaning rodent droppings in enclosed spaces.

mammals.hp: LA County populations recently split from P. maniculatus as P. gambelii based on molecular phylogenetics. The most common native mouse in LA — found from beaches to mountain forests.

mammals.desc: Coastal sage scrub subspecies of Desert Woodrat — Species of Special Concern. Builds elaborate stick houses in remaining coastal sage scrub fragments. Declining steeply as sage scrub is converted to development throughout the LA Basin lowlands.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with white belly; smaller than N. fuscipes
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub — builds stick houses in prickly pear and cholla patches at lowland sites
fm.Diet: Cactus pads, sage leaves, seeds, fruit — can metabolize oxalic acid in cactus
fm.vs: Smaller than Dusky-footed Woodrat. Coastal lowland habitat vs. Dusky-footed in foothill oak woodland.

mammals.hp: SSC — coastal sage scrub populations severely reduced by urbanization. Stick houses are keystone structures providing shelter for 100+ invertebrate and vertebrate species. Loss of woodrat middens cascades through the local food web.

mammals.desc: Tiny mouse with grooved upper incisors (diagnostic under hand lens). Builds globular grass nests. Common in grasslands and marshes but rarely seen.

fm.Size: 4.5–6 in with tail; 0.3–0.5 oz — very small
fm.Color: Brown above, white below; bicolored tail; grooved upper incisors
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, salt marshes, weedy fields
fm.Diet: Seeds, green vegetation, insects
fm.Urban: Common in grasslands, salt marshes, and weedy vacant lots throughout lowland LA. Builds globular grass nests. Rarely seen but abundant where grass is uncut.
fm.vs: GROOVED upper incisors are unique among LA mice — diagnostic but requires hand exam. Smaller than Deer Mouse with proportionally longer tail.

mammals.hp: Grooved upper incisors are unique among LA mice — diagnostic but requires hand examination.

mammals.desc: Spotted wild cat with characteristic short 'bobbed' tail. More common than most people realize — camera traps show them in surprisingly urban areas. Primarily hunts rabbits and rodents. Declining from anticoagulant rodenticide exposure.

fm.Color: Tawny-brown with dark SPOTS; short BOBBED tail (4–7 in) with black tip; ear tufts
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, oak woodland, canyon bottoms — uses suburban-wildland edges for hunting
fm.Behavior: Solitary; crepuscular/nocturnal; patient ambush hunter; territorial
fm.Diet: Rabbits (primary), rodents, birds, lizards, occasional deer fawns
fm.vs: Mountain lion is MUCH larger (80–180 lbs), uniform tawny, with LONG tail. Bobcat is smaller (15–35 lbs), spotted, with SHORT tail.
fm.Conservation: Major threat: secondary anticoagulant rodenticide poisoning. Eats poisoned rodents and accumulates toxins. AB 1788 (2020) restricted some rodenticides.
fm.Urban: Camera traps document bobcats in Griffith Park, Santa Monica Mountains, San Gabriel foothills. More common than perceived.

mammals.hp: LA's most commonly photographed wild cat. Thrives in the urban-wildland interface — camera trap studies show bobcats routinely traverse residential neighborhoods at night. Rodenticide exposure is a major mortality factor.

mammals.desc: LA's apex predator — the Santa Monica Mountains population is isolated by freeways and faces genetic collapse. P-22, the famous Griffith Park mountain lion, became a symbol of urban wildlife and the need for wildlife crossings. The Wallis Annenberg Wildlife Crossing at Liberty Canyon aims to reconnect fragmented populations.

fm.Color: Tawny to gray-brown; black-tipped tail; dark markings on face
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, oak woodland, mountain forests — needs large territories (100+ sq miles for males)
fm.Behavior: Solitary, crepuscular/nocturnal; ambush predator; stalks prey then explosive pounce; kittens have spots
fm.Diet: Primarily MULE DEER; also coyotes, raccoons, rabbits, bighorn sheep
fm.vs: Bobcat is much smaller (15–35 lbs) with spotted coat and short bobbed tail. Mountain lion is large, uniform tawny, with long tail.
fm.Conservation: Santa Monica Mountains population genetically isolated by 101 and 405 freeways. Wallis Annenberg Wildlife Crossing (Liberty Canyon) under construction to reconnect populations.
fm.Urban: P-22 (2012–2022) lived in Griffith Park surrounded by freeways. His story drove the $87M wildlife crossing project.

mammals.hp: LA's apex predator. The P-22 story — a mountain lion trapped in Griffith Park for a decade — catalyzed construction of the Wallis Annenberg Wildlife Crossing over the 101 Freeway. Genetic isolation of the Santa Monica Mountains population threatens long-term viability.

mammals.desc: Fossorial rodent responsible for the mounds of fresh dirt appearing on lawns and hillsides throughout LA. Lives entirely underground in extensive tunnel systems. Powerful digging claws. Turns over tons of soil.

fm.Color: Brown; large yellow INCISORS visible when mouth closed; fur-lined CHEEK POUCHES; tiny eyes/ears; massive claws
fm.Habitat: Any soil deep enough to dig — lawns, gardens, hillsides, meadows, chaparral
fm.Behavior: Almost entirely SUBTERRANEAN; pushes fan-shaped soil mounds to surface; solitary; aggressive
fm.Diet: Roots, tubers, bulbs — pulls plants underground from below
fm.vs: Mole (not in LA) makes raised tunnel ridges. Gopher makes FAN-SHAPED mounds with plugged hole off to one side.
fm.Urban: The #1 garden pest in LA. Fan-shaped dirt mounds on lawns are diagnostic.

mammals.hp: Ecosystem engineer — gopher digging aerates soil, improves drainage, and mixes nutrients. Despite garden conflicts, they play a vital ecological role.

mammals.desc: Large kangaroo rat of chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Five toes on hind feet (some kangaroo rats have four). SSC due to habitat loss.

fm.Color: Dark brown above, white below; long tufted tail; large hind feet with 5 toes
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub — open areas with sandy substrate
fm.Diet: Seeds, some insects
fm.Conservation: SSC. Habitat loss from development.
fm.vs: Merriam's Kangaroo Rat is smaller with shorter hind feet. Desert Kangaroo Rat is larger and paler. Agile is mid-sized with long tail and hind feet.
fm.Urban: Foothill chaparral edges near suburban development. Nocturnal — seen on trails at dusk. Long hind feet and tufted tail diagnostic.

mammals.hp: LA County's agile kangaroo rat — SSC declining from coastal sage scrub loss. Bipedal hopping locomotion with long tail for balance. Seed-caching behavior distributes native plant seeds — important for sage scrub regeneration after fire.

mammals.desc: Common pocket mouse of chaparral. Larger and darker than San Diego Pocket Mouse. External cheek pouches. Coarse spiny fur on rump. Active seed cacher.

fm.Color: Brown-gray with pale sides; SPINY hairs on rump; external cheek pouches
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, oak woodland — rocky hillsides with brush
fm.Diet: Seeds — caches in underground burrows
fm.vs: Largest pocket mouse in LA. Dark coloring and spiny rump hairs distinguish from pale San Diego Pocket Mouse. Chaparral specialist.

mammals.hp: Granivorous seed cacher — cheek pouches transport seeds to underground larders. Important seed disperser in chaparral.

mammals.desc: Largest kangaroo rat — pale and sand-colored for desert camouflage. Enormous hind feet for bipedal hopping. Builds elaborate burrow systems in sandy soil. Eastern LA County desert only.

fm.Size: 12–15 in with tail — LARGEST kangaroo rat
fm.Color: Very PALE buff-sand; white belly; enormous hind feet; long tufted tail
fm.Habitat: Sandy desert flats — needs deep loose sand for burrow systems. Antelope Valley.
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; bipedal hopping; creates elaborate burrow systems with multiple chambers; sand-bathing; foot-drumming
fm.Diet: Seeds — caches massive seed stores underground; never drinks water
fm.vs: Much LARGER and PALER than Merriam's Kangaroo Rat. Needs deep sandy soil.

mammals.hp: Largest kangaroo rat. Burrow systems are complex multi-chambered structures. Sand-bathing maintains water-repellent fur that prevents heat loss during cold desert nights.

mammals.desc: Tiny pocket mouse — one of the smallest rodents in North America. LA Basin subspecies declining steeply from habitat loss. Open sandy areas with sparse vegetation.

fm.Size: 4.5–6 in with tail; 0.25 oz — one of the smallest rodents
fm.Color: Pale buff; tiny; soft silky fur; external cheek pouches
fm.Habitat: Open sandy areas with sparse low vegetation — increasingly rare habitat in LA Basin
fm.Diet: Seeds — granivorous
fm.vs: Smaller than San Diego Pocket Mouse. Pale coloring. Overlaps with P. alticolus at higher elevations.
fm.Urban: Antelope Valley desert-edge communities. Rarely seen — nocturnal seed cacher.

mammals.hp: Among the smallest rodents in North America. LA Basin populations severely reduced by urbanization of sandy grassland habitat.

mammals.desc: Small kangaroo rat that hops on enormous hind legs like a miniature kangaroo. Long tufted tail for balance. Can leap 9 feet to escape predators. Desert margins.

fm.Color: Pale brown with white belly; very LONG hind legs; long TUFTED tail; external cheek pouches
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, sandy flats — eastern LA County
fm.Behavior: BIPEDAL hopping on hind legs; leaps up to 9 ft; drums feet as territorial signal; never drinks water
fm.Diet: Seeds — collects and caches in underground burrows
fm.vs: Kangaroo rats are the only LA rodents that hop bipedally on enormous hind legs with long tufted tails.

mammals.hp: Never drinks water — survives entirely on metabolic water from seed digestion. Foot-drumming communicates territorial boundaries underground.

mammals.desc: Small mouse with external fur-lined cheek pouches for carrying seeds. Granivorous. Coastal sage scrub. Spiny hairs on rump.

fm.Color: Brown-gray with pale sides; SPINY hairs on rump; external CHEEK POUCHES
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral with sandy substrate
fm.Diet: Seeds — granivorous; uses cheek pouches to carry seeds to cache
fm.vs: Spiny rump hairs + cheek pouches. Kangaroo rats are larger with long hind legs.
fm.Conservation: SSC. Declining with coastal sage scrub habitat loss.

mammals.hp: External cheek pouches open outside the mouth — used to transport seeds to underground caches. Spiny rump hairs may deter predators.

mammals.desc: Tiny pocket mouse of high mountain meadows — one of the rarest rodents in California. Known from SGM and Tehachapi Mountains. SSC. Extremely limited range and poorly known.

fm.Color: Olive-brown above, white below; prominent WHITE patch behind each ear (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Mountain meadows and open pine forest — SGM above 5,000 ft
fm.Diet: Seeds — granivorous; external cheek pouches
fm.vs: White EAR PATCH distinguishes from other pocket mice. Mountain habitat above 5,000 ft.
fm.Conservation: SSC. Known from very few localities. Extremely limited range. Poorly studied.

mammals.hp: One of the least-known mammals in California. White ear patches and high mountain habitat distinguish from lowland pocket mice.

mammals.desc: Large hare with enormously long ears used for thermoregulation in desert heat. Not a rabbit — a hare (born furred with eyes open). Top speed 35+ mph. Open grasslands and desert edges.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with BLACK top of tail; enormously LONG ears with black tips
fm.Habitat: Open grasslands, agricultural land, desert scrub — needs open running space
fm.Behavior: Powerful LEAPER (20 ft bounds); top speed 35+ mph; ears dissipate heat (thermoregulation); born fully furred (precocial)
fm.Diet: Grasses, forbs, crops, cacti
fm.vs: Cottontails are much SMALLER with shorter ears. Jackrabbits are HARES — much larger, longer-eared, and precocial.
fm.Urban: Common in open space preserves, golf courses, Antelope Valley grasslands.

mammals.hp: Ears are thermoregulatory — blood vessels near surface radiate excess heat in desert conditions. Body heat can be reduced by 1°F through ear cooling alone.

mammals.desc: Smaller and darker than Desert Cottontail. Stays close to dense brush — rarely ventures into open areas. Short ears. Foothill chaparral specialist.

fm.Color: Dark brown-gray; smaller white cottontail; SHORTER ears than Desert Cottontail
fm.Habitat: Dense chaparral and brush — rarely far from cover
fm.Behavior: Stays within a few feet of dense brush; freezes then bolts into nearest brush
fm.Diet: Grasses, clover, forbs, berries
fm.vs: Desert Cottontail is LARGER with LONGER ears and ventures into open areas. Brush Rabbit is smaller, darker, with SHORTER ears and stays near brush.

mammals.hp: Obligate dense-brush dweller — home range rarely exceeds 1 acre of contiguous chaparral. Never ventures far from cover. Declining with chaparral conversion. Unlike cottontails, does not use open ground — absence from clearings is diagnostic.

mammals.desc: Common cottontail rabbit of lowland LA. White cotton-puff tail. Active dawn and dusk. Major prey species for coyotes, bobcats, and raptors. Thriving in suburban edge habitats.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with white COTTON-PUFF tail; pale orange nape; large ears
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral, suburban edges, parks, golf courses
fm.Behavior: Crepuscular; freezes when threatened then bolts in zig-zag; does not dig burrows (hides in brush)
fm.Diet: Grasses, forbs, cacti, bark — herbivorous; coprophagous (eats own fecal pellets for nutrient extraction)
fm.vs: Brush Rabbit is smaller and darker with shorter ears. Jackrabbits are much larger with enormous ears.
fm.Urban: Common in suburban parks and gardens throughout LA.

mammals.hp: Primary prey for coyotes, bobcats, hawks, and owls. Key species in the LA food web.

mammals.desc: Channel Islands subspecies of Western Spotted Skunk — found on Santa Cruz and Santa Rosa islands. Smaller than mainland populations. Does the same dramatic HANDSTAND spray defense. Population stabilized following island ecosystem restoration and removal of feral pigs.

fm.Color: Black with white spots — island form
fm.Habitat: Channel Islands — coastal scrub, grassland, rocky areas on Santa Cruz and Santa Rosa islands
fm.Behavior: Does HANDSTAND before spraying; nocturnal; good climber
fm.Diet: Insects, rodents (deer mice), fruit, bird eggs — more carnivorous than mainland skunks
fm.vs: Smaller than mainland Spotted Skunk. Island-restricted. Same broken white spot/stripe pattern.

mammals.hp: Channel Islands endemic subspecies benefiting from island restoration programs. Feral pig removal eliminated nest predation and habitat destruction — skunk populations rebounded. Important small predator in island food web.

mammals.desc: Small agile skunk with broken white spots and stripes. Does a dramatic HANDSTAND before spraying — kicks up on front legs and aims backward. Much less common than Striped Skunk.

fm.Color: Black with broken white SPOTS and short stripes (pattern unique to each individual)
fm.Habitat: Rocky hillsides, chaparral, oak woodland — more wild-area than Striped Skunk
fm.Behavior: Does a HANDSTAND (walks on front legs) as warning before spraying; nocturnal; good climber
fm.Diet: Insects, rodents, fruit, bird eggs — more carnivorous than Striped Skunk
fm.vs: Striped Skunk has TWO continuous white stripes. Spotted Skunk has BROKEN spots/dashes and does HANDSTANDS.

mammals.hp: The handstand display is one of the most dramatic warning behaviors of any mammal. Declining rangewide — less common than Striped Skunk.

mammals.desc: Black-and-white skunk that sprays foul MUSK up to 10 feet with remarkable accuracy. Common throughout LA suburbs. Warning: stomps front feet and raises tail before spraying. The smell persists for days.

fm.Color: Black with TWO white stripes on back and white head patch
fm.Habitat: Suburban neighborhoods, parks, open woodland — dens under buildings, decks, wood piles
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; WARNING display before spraying: stamps feet, raises tail, turns rear. Spray is accurate to 10+ ft. Good digger.
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — grubs, insects, rodents, bird eggs, fruit, garbage, pet food
fm.vs: Spotted Skunk is smaller with broken white spots/stripes and does a HANDSTAND before spraying.
fm.Urban: Very common in LA suburbs. The sulfur-containing spray (butylmercaptan) causes temporary blindness and nausea.

mammals.hp: Spray contains thiols (sulfur compounds) detectable at 1 mile. Tomato juice doesn't work — hydrogen peroxide + baking soda + dish soap is the effective remedy.

mammals.desc: Powerful flat-bodied digger of open grasslands. Can excavate a ground squirrel burrow in minutes. Formerly common in LA basin grasslands — now restricted to remaining open space at margins.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with distinctive white STRIPE from nose over head; short powerful legs; flat body
fm.Habitat: Open grasslands, agricultural land — needs soft soil for digging. Antelope Valley, northern LA County.
fm.Behavior: Powerful digger — excavates entire ground squirrel burrow systems; nocturnal; solitary; fierce when cornered
fm.Diet: Ground squirrels, pocket gophers, mice — digs prey out of burrows
fm.vs: Low flat body with white head stripe is diagnostic. No other LA mammal has this body plan.
fm.Conservation: SSC. Declining from: habitat loss (grassland development), vehicle mortality, rodenticide exposure.

mammals.hp: Cooperative hunting with coyotes documented — coyote chases ground squirrel to burrow, badger digs it out. Both benefit. One of nature's most remarkable inter-species partnerships.

mammals.desc: Slender fast predator that can pursue prey into burrows. Pound-for-pound one of the most ferocious predators in LA. Takes prey much larger than itself.

fm.Color: Brown above, cream/white below; black-tipped tail; long sinuous body
fm.Habitat: Diverse — meadows, chaparral, woodland, riparian, farmland
fm.Behavior: Active day or night; sinuous hunter enters rodent burrows; fierce — takes prey larger than itself
fm.Diet: Rodents (primary), rabbits, birds, eggs, insects
fm.vs: No other small carnivore in LA has this elongated sinuous body shape and black-tipped tail.

mammals.hp: Enters rodent burrows to hunt underground. Important rodent predator. Ferocious — documented killing rabbits 3x their own weight.

mammals.desc: Masked nocturnal omnivore thriving throughout urban LA. Extremely dexterous front paws. Intelligent problem-solver — opens latches, garbage cans, and pet doors. Major nest predator of native birds and turtles.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with distinctive BLACK MASK across eyes and RINGED tail
fm.Habitat: Everywhere — urban neighborhoods, parks, storm drains, riparian, woodland
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; extremely dexterous HANDS; washes food; excellent climber and swimmer; den in tree hollows, attics, storm drains
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — pet food, garbage, fruit, crayfish, bird eggs, turtle eggs, insects, worms
fm.vs: No other LA mammal has the black MASK and RINGED tail.
fm.Urban: Abundant throughout LA. Major conflict species — garbage raiding, attic denning, pet food theft, koi pond predation.

mammals.hp: Native species thriving in urban environments. Extremely adaptable diet and intelligence. Hands-down the most dexterous non-primate in LA.

mammals.desc: Elusive nocturnal carnivore with an enormously long RINGED TAIL. Called 'miner's cat' — kept as mousers in Gold Rush camps. Related to raccoons. Seldom seen but present throughout LA's mountains.

fm.Size: 24–32 in with tail (tail longer than body); 2–3 lbs
fm.Color: Tawny-brown with large round EYES; enormously long tail with black and white RINGS
fm.Habitat: Rocky canyons, cliff faces, oak woodland — strictly nocturnal, seldom seen
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; expert climber on vertical rock; can rotate hind feet 180° for descending head-first; dens in rock crevices
fm.Diet: Rodents, insects, fruit, birds, lizards
fm.vs: Raccoon is much larger with a BLACK MASK. Ringtail is smaller with enormous EYES and a proportionally much longer tail.
fm.Urban: Present in foothill canyons but almost never seen. Camera traps confirm presence in Griffith Park and Santa Monica Mountains.

mammals.hp: Can rotate ankles 180° — the only carnivore that can descend cliffs head-first. California's state mammal (rarely seen by Californians).

mammals.desc: Large ground squirrel of open grassy areas. Stands upright to scan for predators. Extremely common in parks and trail heads. Blood has evolved partial immunity to rattlesnake venom.

fm.Color: Gray-brown mottled; mantle of darker fur on shoulders; pale ring around eyes
fm.Habitat: Open grasslands, trail margins, parks, road edges, agricultural areas
fm.Behavior: DIURNAL; lives in communal burrow systems; stands upright sentinel-style; alarm calls
fm.Diet: Seeds, nuts, grasses, insects, occasional bird eggs
fm.vs: Tree squirrels (Fox Squirrel) have bushier tails and climb trees. Ground squirrels are ground-dwelling with thinner tails.
fm.Urban: Extremely common in every park and trailhead in LA County.

mammals.hp: Blood contains proteins that bind rattlesnake venom — partial immunity. Heated tail (infrared) used to intimidate rattlesnakes.

mammals.desc: Mountain chipmunk of SGM conifer forests. Smaller and brighter than Merriam's Chipmunk. Facial stripes sharp and well-defined. Active on fallen logs and rocks above 6,000 ft.

fm.Color: Bright reddish-brown with sharp black and white FACIAL STRIPES; pale side stripes
fm.Habitat: Conifer forest above 6,000 ft — SGM pines and firs; on logs, rocks, and trees
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; caches seeds; cheek pouches; chattering alarm call; hibernates in winter
fm.Diet: Seeds, nuts, berries, insects, fungi — omnivorous
fm.vs: Merriam's Chipmunk is DULLER, LARGER, and at LOWER elevation. Lodgepole is brighter and higher.

mammals.hp: Mountain conifer forest specialist above 6,000 ft. Hibernates during SGM winter. Important seed disperser for mountain pines.

mammals.desc: Common foothill chipmunk of chaparral and oak woodland. Larger and duller than Lodgepole Chipmunk. Active on rocks and brush in canyon bottoms. Does not true-hibernate — torpor instead.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with indistinct facial stripes (duller than Lodgepole); brownish side stripes
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, oak woodland, rocky canyon bottoms — 2,000–6,000 ft
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; chattering alarm call from brush pile; does NOT truly hibernate (enters torpor bouts)
fm.Diet: Acorns, seeds, berries, insects
fm.vs: DULLER and LARGER than Lodgepole Chipmunk. LOWER elevation. Indistinct facial stripes.

mammals.hp: Foothill chaparral chipmunk — does not truly hibernate but enters torpor bouts during cold periods. Important acorn cacher in oak woodland.

mammals.desc: STATE THREATENED ground squirrel endemic to the western Mojave Desert. LA County populations in the Antelope Valley area. Small, plain-colored ground squirrel without the white tail stripes of the similar White-tailed Antelope Squirrel. Hibernates 7-8 months of the year.
mammals.hp: State Threatened. Endemic to the western Mojave — nowhere else on Earth. Habitat loss to solar energy development and urban expansion in the Antelope Valley is the primary threat. Hibernates August through February — above ground only 4-5 months per year.

fm.Size: 8-9 in total, stocky — typical ground squirrel
fm.Color: Plain gray-brown, NO white side stripes; short tail
fm.Habitat: Creosote bush scrub, Joshua tree woodland — Antelope Valley
fm.Behavior: Hibernates Aug–Feb (7-8 months!). Active only spring-midsummer.
fm.Diet: Seeds, leaves, flowers of desert plants
fm.vs: White-tailed Antelope Squirrel has WHITE side stripe and curled white tail. Mohave Ground Squirrel is plain gray-brown with no stripes.
fm.Conservation: State Threatened. Report sightings to CDFW — every observation helps.

mammals.desc: Nocturnal squirrel that GLIDES between trees using membrane between front and back legs. Present in SGM conifer forest but rarely seen — strictly nocturnal. Among LA County's most elusive mammals. Eats fungi.

fm.Color: Brown above, white below; large DARK EYES; loose furred GLIDING MEMBRANE (patagium) between front and hind legs
fm.Habitat: Mountain conifer forest — old-growth with tree hollows. SGM above 5,000 ft.
fm.Behavior: Strictly NOCTURNAL; GLIDES up to 150 ft between trees using patagium; nests in tree hollows and woodpecker cavities; truffle specialist
fm.Diet: Truffles (hypogeous fungi — primary food), lichens, seeds, insects, bird eggs — important truffle spore disperser
fm.vs: The only GLIDING mammal in LA County. Large dark eyes + gliding membrane. Never seen during day.

mammals.hp: Critical truffle disperser — eats underground fungi and deposits spores in feces across the forest. The fungi are mycorrhizal with trees, so flying squirrels maintain the tree-fungus mutualism.

mammals.desc: Small unpatterned ground squirrel of open desert. No stripes or spots — uniformly sandy-brown. Round tail (not bushy). Desert flats. Estivates in summer heat.

fm.Color: Uniformly SANDY-BROWN — no stripes, spots, or markings; small round tail
fm.Habitat: Open desert flats, sandy washes — Antelope Valley
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; ESTIVATES (summer hibernation) during hottest months; active early morning; colonial burrows
fm.Diet: Seeds, green vegetation, insects — seasonally variable
fm.vs: No stripes or spots — UNIFORMLY sandy. Round thin tail (not bushy). Desert only.

mammals.hp: Estivates during extreme summer heat — one of few mammals with summer dormancy. Active only during cooler months. Desert sand specialist.

mammals.desc: California's NATIVE tree squirrel — silver-gray with long bushy tail. Being displaced from urban areas by introduced Fox Squirrels. Now restricted to mountains and foothill oak woodlands.

fm.Color: Silver-gray with WHITE belly; very long bushy gray tail
fm.Habitat: Oak and conifer forests — foothill and mountain woodlands
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; shy and less human-adapted than Fox Squirrel; primarily in oak canopy
fm.Diet: Acorns, pine nuts, fungi, seeds
fm.vs: Fox Squirrel is RUSTY-BROWN and bold in urban areas. Western Gray is SILVER-GRAY with white belly and shy.
fm.Conservation: Declining from Fox Squirrel competition and habitat loss.
fm.Urban: Still present in foothill oak woodlands and mountain parks — San Gabriel foothills, Santa Monica Mountains. Shy and declining where Fox Squirrels have invaded.

mammals.hp: Native tree squirrel declining in lowlands due to Fox Squirrel competition. Still common in mountain oak woodlands.

mammals.desc: Hyperactive chipmunk-like desert ground squirrel. Carries tail curled over back showing white UNDERSIDE as it runs — a flash signal. Tolerates extreme desert heat. Antelope Valley.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with white STRIPE on each side; tail carried over back showing WHITE underside (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, Joshua tree woodland — open desert ground. Antelope Valley.
fm.Behavior: Diurnal even in extreme heat; runs with tail over back flashing white; heat-tolerant (body temp reaches 104°F before cooling behavior)
fm.Diet: Seeds, insects, green vegetation, Joshua tree seeds
fm.vs: Only chipmunk-like squirrel in desert lowlands. White tail UNDERSIDE flashed when running is diagnostic. True chipmunks have facial stripes.
fm.Urban: Antelope Valley neighborhoods — hyperactive ground squirrel running with tail curled over back, flashing white underside. Common in Lancaster/Palmdale yards.

mammals.hp: Heat-tolerance specialist — allows body temperature to rise to 104°F before seeking shade to dump heat. The white tail flash may signal alarm to relatives.

mammals.desc: Tiny insectivore with pointed snout. Not a rodent — shrews are insectivores. Must eat constantly — starves within hours without food. Hyperactive metabolism. Venomous saliva.

fm.Color: Brown-gray; tiny; pointed SNOUT; tiny eyes; velvet fur
fm.Habitat: Moist areas — marshes, riparian, moist grassland
fm.Behavior: Active 24/7; must eat every few hours or dies; heart rate 800+ bpm; VENOMOUS saliva paralyzes prey
fm.Diet: Insects, worms, spiders, snails — insectivore (NOT a rodent)
fm.vs: Much smaller than any mouse with a pointed SNOUT. Shrews are NOT rodents.
fm.Urban: Moist areas — marshes, riparian corridors, gardens with dense vegetation near water. Heart rate 800+ bpm. Must eat every few hours or dies. Venomous saliva.

mammals.hp: Highest metabolic rate of any mammal — heart beats 800+ bpm. Venomous saliva subdues earthworm and insect prey. Starves within 3–4 hours without food.

mammals.desc: Underground burrowing insectivore with enormous digging forepaws. Raises tunnel ridges across lawns. Not a rodent. Velvety fur that lies flat in any direction for reversing in tunnels.

fm.Color: Dark gray-brown; enormous paddle-like FOREPAWS; tiny eyes; no visible ears; velvety fur
fm.Habitat: Meadows, gardens, mountain forests — needs moist soil for tunneling
fm.Behavior: Almost entirely SUBTERRANEAN; creates raised tunnel ridges visible on lawn surface; solitary; aggressive
fm.Diet: Earthworms (primary), insects, grubs — insectivore
fm.vs: Mole raises RIDGES on surface. Gopher makes FAN-SHAPED MOUNDS. Moles have paddle-like forepaws; gophers have visible incisors.
fm.Urban: Raised tunnel ridges across foothill lawns and gardens. Deeper tunnels than gophers. Velvety fur. Eats earthworms, not plant roots (unlike gophers).

mammals.hp: Recently split from S. latimanus. Southern California populations are the newly described S. occultus. Same lawn-tunneling habits — raised ridges across foothill gardens.

mammals.desc: Increasingly common in San Gabriel Mountains foothills — bears are expanding into suburban foothill communities. Attracted to garbage, fruit trees, and bird feeders. LA County has the densest human-bear interface in California.

fm.Color: Black to brown (color varies); brown muzzle; no shoulder hump (distinguishes from grizzly)
fm.Habitat: Mountain forests, chaparral — increasingly in foothill suburban neighborhoods
fm.Behavior: Crepuscular/nocturnal in suburban areas; climbs trees; hibernates in winter at higher elevations; attracted to human food sources
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — 85% plant matter (berries, acorns, grasses); also insects, carrion, garbage, bird seed, fruit trees
fm.vs: No other bear species in LA. No shoulder hump (grizzly bears were extirpated from CA by 1924).
fm.Urban: San Gabriel foothill communities (La Cañada, Pasadena, Monrovia, Arcadia) have regular bear encounters. Secure garbage and remove bird feeders.

mammals.hp: Expanding into suburban foothills. Bear-proof garbage containers now required in many SGM foothill communities. Never feed bears — a fed bear is a dead bear.

mammals.desc: 14 bison brought to Catalina Island in 1924 for a movie that was never filmed. Population grew to 600+ before being managed down to ~150 by relocation. Now a tourist attraction and managed herd.

fm.Size: 7–12 ft; 800–2,000 lbs — largest land animal on Catalina
fm.Color: Dark brown; massive SHOULDER HUMP; curved horns in both sexes
fm.Habitat: Catalina Island grasslands and oak woodland — managed herd of ~150
fm.Behavior: Grazing; wallowing; bulls spar during rut; dangerous — maintain safe distance
fm.Diet: Grasses, forbs — herbivorous grazer
fm.vs: Only bison in LA County. On Catalina Island only. Massive size unmistakable.
fm.Urban: Catalina Island only — visible on interior grasslands from hiking trails and the Airport in the Sky road. Managed herd of ~150. DANGEROUS — maintain safe distance.

mammals.hp: Introduced for a 1924 movie (The Vanishing American). Never removed. Managed by Catalina Conservancy — birth control and relocation keep population at ~150 to limit island habitat damage.

mammals.desc: Introduced red fox established along LA's coastal lowlands — Playa del Rey, Marina del Rey, Palos Verdes, San Pedro. NOT the native Sierra Nevada Red Fox. Predator of ground-nesting birds including endangered Snowy Plovers and Least Terns.

fm.Color: Orange-red with black legs and black-backed EARS; bushy white-tipped TAIL; white chin
fm.Habitat: Coastal lowlands, beaches, wetlands — Ballona, Palos Verdes, San Pedro
fm.Behavior: Crepuscular/nocturnal in urban areas; dens in coastal bluffs; raids shorebird nests
fm.Diet: Rodents, rabbits, birds (including ground-nesting shorebird eggs and chicks), fruit, garbage
fm.vs: Gray Fox has GRIZZLED gray coat and RUST sides with NO white tail tip. Red Fox is RED-ORANGE with BLACK legs and WHITE tail tip.
fm.Urban: Coastal lowlands — Playa del Rey, Marina del Rey, Palos Verdes, San Pedro. Dens in coastal bluffs. Major threat to endangered Snowy Plover and Least Tern nesting colonies.

mammals.hp: INTRODUCED — not the native Sierra Nevada Red Fox. Major threat to endangered Snowy Plover and California Least Tern nesting colonies on LA beaches. Active removal programs at nesting sites.

mammals.desc: North America's ONLY marsupial — carries young in a pouch. Introduced to California around 1910. 'Playing possum' — involuntary catatonic state when threatened. Immune to most snake venom. Eats thousands of ticks.

fm.Color: Gray-white with hairless PINK snout, ears, and PREHENSILE tail; appears rat-like
fm.Habitat: Urban neighborhoods, parks, riparian corridors — dens in attics, under decks, brush piles
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; 'plays possum' (involuntary catatonic state); prehensile tail; 50 teeth (most of any NA land mammal)
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — fruit, insects, snails, carrion, garbage, pet food, bird eggs, small rodents, TICKS (eats ~5,000/season)
fm.vs: No other LA mammal has a hairless PREHENSILE TAIL and PINK snout. Only marsupial in North America.
fm.Urban: Extremely common in urban LA. Beneficial — eats ticks, snails, and cockroaches. Rarely carries rabies due to low body temperature.

mammals.hp: North America's only marsupial. Introduced to California — native to eastern US. Famous 'playing possum' thanatosis defense. Immune to rattlesnake venom and most pit viper bites. Highly resistant to rabies due to low body temperature.

mammals.desc: Feral domestic rabbit establishing populations in LA County — particularly the San Juan Capistrano and coastal Orange County areas adjacent to southern LA County. Variable coloration (wild-type agouti brown to domestic black, white, spotted). Smaller and more compact than native Cottontail and Jackrabbit.
mammals.hp: Feral populations descending from released pet rabbits. Breeds prolifically — a single pair can produce 30+ offspring per year. Competes with native Desert Cottontail and Brush Rabbit for food and cover. Habitat modification through overgrazing documented in established populations.

fm.Size: 14-18 in, 3-5 lbs — smaller and more compact than Cottontail
fm.Color: Variable — agouti brown (wild-type), black, white, spotted, broken patterns
fm.Habitat: Urban parks, golf courses, coastal scrub near suburban areas
fm.Behavior: Digs burrows (unlike native cottontails which use forms/brush piles)
fm.Diet: Grass, herbs, garden plants — can damage landscaping
fm.vs: Desert Cottontail has brown agouti coloring, longer ears, and does NOT burrow. European Rabbit has variable colors, shorter ears, and digs burrows.
fm.Urban: Feral colonies in parks and coastal communities. Variable coloration from domestic ancestry is diagnostic.

mammals.desc: Small introduced mouse found in every building in LA. Gray-brown with naked scaly tail. Distinctive musky odor. The world's most widely distributed mammal after humans.

fm.Color: Uniformly gray-brown; NAKED scaly tail; small eyes relative to Peromyscus
fm.Habitat: Inside buildings — walls, attics, cabinets, garages
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; excellent climber; squeezes through 1/4-inch gaps; musky ODOR
fm.Diet: Seeds, grains, anything — omnivorous scavenger
fm.vs: Deer Mouse has BICOLORED tail (dark above/white below) and sharp white belly. House Mouse is UNIFORMLY gray-brown with naked scaly tail.
fm.Urban: In every building in LA — walls, attics, cabinets, garages. Distinguished from native Deer Mouse by uniform gray-brown color, naked scaly tail, and musky odor.

mammals.hp: Commensal — dependent on human structures. Distinguished from native Deer Mouse by uniform coloring and musky odor.

mammals.desc: Larger introduced rat — the Sewer Rat or Brown Rat. Ground-dwelling and burrowing. Storm drains and sewers. Less common in residential attics than Roof Rat in LA.

fm.Size: 15–20 in with tail; tail SHORTER than body; heavier than Roof Rat
fm.Color: Brown-gray; small ears; thick NAKED tail shorter than body; blunt muzzle
fm.Habitat: Storm drains, sewers, burrows along foundations — ground-dweller
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; burrower; strong swimmer; less agile climber than Roof Rat
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — garbage, pet food, anything available
fm.vs: Roof Rat is more SLENDER with tail LONGER than body — a climber. Norway Rat is HEAVIER with tail SHORTER than body — a burrower.
fm.Urban: Sewer rat — storm drains, burrows along building foundations, dumpster areas. Heavier and ground-dwelling compared to the climbing Roof Rat.

mammals.hp: Global invasive — present in every city on Earth. Sewer-dwelling. The lab rat (albino Norway Rat) is this species.

mammals.desc: Introduced Old World rat — the Black Rat or Ship Rat. Agile climber. Major pest in LA attics, palm trees, and citrus orchards. Spreads disease. Predator of native birds and eggs.

fm.Size: 13–18 in with tail; tail LONGER than body
fm.Color: Black to brown; large ears; scaly NAKED tail longer than body; pointed muzzle
fm.Habitat: Attics, palm trees, citrus orchards, dense vegetation — excellent CLIMBER
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; superb climber; nests in attics, palm tree crowns, dense ivy; gnaws wiring (fire hazard)
fm.Diet: Fruit (especially citrus and avocado), pet food, garbage, bird eggs, garden produce
fm.vs: Norway Rat is LARGER, heavier, with tail SHORTER than body, and is a ground-dweller. Roof Rat is more slender with tail LONGER than body and is an arboreal CLIMBER.
fm.Urban: In every palm tree, attic, and citrus orchard in LA. Superb climber. Gnaws electrical wiring (fire hazard). The dominant rat pest in residential LA. Major predator of native bird eggs.

mammals.hp: Major urban pest. Predates native bird eggs in palm trees. Carries diseases including plague, typhus, leptospirosis. Gnaws wiring — fire hazard.

mammals.desc: The common tree squirrel of LA neighborhoods — introduced from the eastern US in the early 1900s. Large, rusty-colored, with bushy tail. Displacing native Western Gray Squirrel.

fm.Color: Rusty-brown to orange; large bushy tail
fm.Habitat: Urban neighborhoods, parks, campuses — anywhere with trees
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; arboreal; caches acorns and walnuts; bold around humans; raids bird feeders
fm.Diet: Nuts, seeds, fruit, bird seed, garbage — very adaptable
fm.vs: Native Western Gray Squirrel is GRAY with white belly. Introduced Fox Squirrel is RUSTY-BROWN.
fm.Urban: The default LA neighborhood squirrel. Introduced from eastern US ~1904.

mammals.hp: Introduced to LA in early 1900s by veterans' organizations. Now dominates every urban park, displacing native Western Gray Squirrel. Major nest predator of songbirds. The most commonly seen wild mammal in urban LA after the house mouse.

mammals.desc: Introduced eastern tree squirrel establishing at scattered LA locations. Second introduced tree squirrel after the Eastern Fox Squirrel which dominates LA. Gray coloration and smaller size distinguish from the larger reddish Fox Squirrel.
mammals.hp: Second invasive tree squirrel — joins Fox Squirrel in displacing native Western Gray Squirrel. In eastern US and UK, Gray Squirrel has devastated native squirrel populations. LA populations still small — monitoring critical.

fm.Size: 9-12 in body — smaller than Fox Squirrel
fm.Color: Gray with white belly — NOT reddish like Fox Squirrel
fm.Habitat: Urban parks with large trees — establishing at scattered sites
fm.vs: Fox Squirrel is larger and reddish-brown. Western Gray Squirrel is larger, silvery, and restricted to mountain forests. Eastern Gray is medium-sized, gray, and urban.
fm.Urban: Establishing in parks. Smaller and grayer than the ubiquitous Fox Squirrel.

mammals.desc: Destructive invasive — rooting behavior devastates native vegetation and stream banks. Present on Santa Catalina Island (now removed) and periodically on mainland. Major threat to native ecosystems wherever established.

fm.Color: Variable — black, brown, spotted, or grizzled; TUSKS in males; coarse bristly hair
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, chaparral, riparian — any habitat; periodically detected in LA County
fm.Behavior: ROOTING — plows up soil with snout destroying vegetation and stream banks; aggressive; omnivorous; prolific breeder
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — roots, acorns, invertebrates, eggs, small animals, crops, carrion
fm.vs: Only wild pig in LA County. Stocky build, coarse hair, tusks, and ROOTING damage are diagnostic.
fm.Urban: Periodically detected in LA County wildlands. Rooting damage to native vegetation is diagnostic. Report sightings to CDFW — active removal program.

mammals.hp: Most destructive invasive mammal in California. Rooting destroys native plant communities, increases erosion, and degrades stream habitat. Eradicated from Catalina Island; mainland occurrences are monitored and removed.

bats.desc: Large free-tailed bat — the second Nyctinomops species in LA after the Pocketed Free-tailed Bat. Roosts in high cliff crevices and tall buildings. Audible echolocation clicks can be heard from ground level. Migratory — appears in LA primarily in late summer and fall.
bats.hp: Rare migratory visitor. Cliff-roosting specialist — also uses tall buildings. One of the largest bats in LA. Distinctive low-frequency echolocation calls audible to humans standing below roost. Declining from loss of cliff-face roost sites.

fm.Size: Wingspan 17-18 in — one of LA's largest bats
fm.Color: Dark brown, large ears joined at base, tail extends beyond membrane
fm.Habitat: Cliff crevices, tall buildings — high roost sites. SGM canyon walls.
fm.Behavior: Audible echolocation clicks from ground level. Cannot take flight from flat surface — drops from height.
fm.Diet: Large moths, beetles — late emerger, high fast flight
fm.vs: Western Mastiff Bat is even larger and also audible. Pocketed Free-tailed is smaller. Big Free-tailed is intermediate in size.

bats.desc: The most common bat in LA — roosts in buildings, bridges, and bat boxes by the thousands. Tail extends beyond tail membrane (hence 'free-tailed'). Flies fast and high. Single colony can eat TONS of insects per night.

fm.Color: Brown; ears wide and forward-facing; tail extends BEYOND tail membrane (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Roosts in buildings, bridges, stadiums, bat boxes — emerges at dusk in swirling columns
fm.Behavior: Fast direct flight at high altitude; roosts in large colonies (hundreds to millions); insectivorous echolocation
fm.Diet: Moths, beetles, flying ants, mosquitoes — a single colony eats TONS of insects nightly
