fm.vs: Big Brown Bat is LARGER and slower-flying. Free-tailed has tail extending BEYOND membrane and wider ears.
fm.Urban: LA's most common bat. Watch for evening emergence from building roosts at sunset.

bats.hp: Eats enormous quantities of agricultural pest insects. Colonies save farmers billions in pest control annually. Fastest horizontal flight of any bat — 100+ mph recorded.

bats.desc: Medium free-tailed bat with ears joined at base across forehead. Rocky desert canyons. Named for small skin pocket on inner thigh of wing membrane.

fm.Color: Brown; ears JOINED at base across forehead (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Desert canyons, cliff faces — rocky roost sites
fm.Diet: Moths — moth specialist
fm.vs: Ears joined at BASE across forehead distinguish from Mexican Free-tailed (ears separate).
fm.Urban: Rocky canyon neighborhoods — eastern SGM foothills. Roosts in cliff crevices near residential areas. Moth specialist emerging after dark.

bats.hp: Rocky canyon specialist. Moth specialist — echolocation tuned to moth wing-beat frequencies.

bats.desc: Largest bat in North America — wingspan to 22 inches. Roosts on tall cliff faces. Audible echolocation clicks (most bat calls are ultrasonic). Must drop from height to achieve flight — cannot take off from ground.

fm.Size: Wingspan 20–22 in; body 7 in — LARGEST North American bat
fm.Color: Brown; enormous EARS that extend forward past nose; wrinkled lips; large free tail
fm.Habitat: Cliff faces, tall buildings — needs vertical surface to drop from for launch; rocky canyons
fm.Behavior: Cannot take off from GROUND — must drop from cliff/building; AUDIBLE echolocation clicks; high fast flight
fm.Diet: Moths, beetles, flying ants — catches large insects at high altitude
fm.vs: MUCH larger than any other LA bat. Audible clicking during flight. Huge ears extending past nose.
fm.Conservation: SSC. Cliff-roosting limits habitat. Building demolition removes roost sites.
fm.Urban: Roosts on tall cliff faces and buildings — audible echolocation clicks heard from ground level. Cannot take off from flat ground; must drop from vertical surface.

bats.hp: Largest bat in North America by wingspan. Cliff-face rooster — cannot launch from flat ground, must drop from vertical surfaces. Audible echolocation clicks detectable by human ears from ground level. SSC — building demolition removes urban roost sites.

bats.desc: Large robust bat common in urban areas — roosts in attics, behind shutters, and in palm tree skirts. Slower, more maneuverable flight than free-tailed bats. Important beetle predator.

fm.Size: Wingspan 13–16 in; body 4–5 in — largest common bat in LA
fm.Color: Brown; broad muzzle; slow maneuverable flight; does NOT have tail beyond membrane
fm.Habitat: Buildings, palm trees, bridges — adapts well to urban structures
fm.Behavior: Slower more maneuverable flight than free-tailed; roosts individually or in small groups in buildings
fm.Diet: Beetles (primary), moths, flying ants — important agricultural pest predator
fm.vs: Free-tailed bat has tail BEYOND membrane and higher faster flight. Big Brown Bat is LARGER with broader muzzle.
fm.Urban: Very common in LA — often found in attics and behind shutters.

bats.hp: Important beetle predator — eats cucumber beetles, June bugs, and other crop pests. One of the most cold-tolerant North American bats.

bats.desc: Tiny bat — smallest in LA County. Roosts in rock crevices, tree bark, and building cracks. Slow fluttering flight close to vegetation. Very common but rarely noticed.

fm.Size: Wingspan 9 in; body 3 in — smallest LA bat
fm.Color: Light brown; small size; pointed tragus in ear
fm.Habitat: Roosts in rock crevices, tree bark fissures, building cracks — diverse roost sites
fm.Behavior: Slow fluttery flight close to vegetation; maneuvers through dense canopy; small foraging area
fm.Diet: Small moths, flies, midges, mosquitoes
fm.vs: Very small. Similar to other Myotis species — requires hand examination for positive ID in most cases.
fm.Urban: Common throughout LA. Roosts in building crevices and bark fissures.

bats.hp: One of the smallest and most common bats in LA County. Roosts in rock crevices, tree bark, and buildings. Often the first bat species detected in urban bat surveys. Flight is slow and fluttery near vegetation.

bats.desc: Medium Myotis with distinctive FRINGE of stiff hairs along trailing edge of tail membrane. Oak woodland and conifer forest. Roosts in buildings, caves, and tree hollows.

fm.Color: Brown; FRINGE of stiff short hairs along trailing edge of tail membrane (diagnostic — needs hand exam)
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, conifer forest — roosts in buildings, mines, caves, tree hollows
fm.Behavior: Gleaner — picks insects from vegetation; also aerial hawking; colonial maternity roosts
fm.Diet: Beetles, moths, spiders — both aerial and gleaning
fm.vs: The FRINGE of hairs on tail membrane is diagnostic but requires hand examination. Medium-sized brown Myotis.
fm.Urban: Found in foothill oak woodland neighborhoods. Roosts in old buildings, barns, and tree hollows. Colonial maternity roosts vulnerable to building renovation.

bats.hp: Tail membrane fringe function debated — may improve flight aerodynamics or help with gleaning insects from surfaces. Colonial maternity roosts vulnerable to disturbance.

bats.desc: Largest bat in LA — wingspan to 16 inches. Frosted silver-brown fur. Long-distance migrant. Tree rooster. The bat most commonly killed by wind turbines during migration.

fm.Size: Wingspan 15–16 in; body 5.5 in — largest LA bat
fm.Color: Brown with FROSTED silver-white tips (hoary); yellow-orange throat patch; furred ears
fm.Habitat: Roosts singly in tree foliage; migrates through LA; forages in open areas and over water
fm.Behavior: Solitary; highly migratory; fast powerful flight; late emerger — comes out well after sunset
fm.Diet: Moths (primary), beetles, dragonflies — catches large insects
fm.vs: Largest bat in LA with distinctive FROSTED fur. No other bat approaches this size.
fm.Conservation: Major wind turbine mortality during migration — leading cause of bat mortality at wind farms.
fm.Urban: Migrates through LA in spring and fall. Roosts in tall urban trees. Late emerger — appears well after sunset. The bat most commonly killed at wind turbine sites.

bats.hp: Most commonly killed bat at wind energy facilities — migratory tree-roosting bats are disproportionately affected. Important for long-distance moth suppression.

bats.desc: Large-eared Myotis of mountain conifer forests. Gleans insects from vegetation — hovers near leaves and bark to pluck prey. Long ears extend well past nose.

fm.Color: Pale brown; LONG EARS extending well past nose when pressed forward
fm.Habitat: Mountain conifer forests — roosts in bark, snags, buildings
fm.Behavior: GLEANER — hovers near vegetation to pluck insects from leaves and bark; slow maneuverable flight
fm.Diet: Beetles, moths, spiders — plucked from vegetation surfaces
fm.vs: Long ears extending past nose. Mountain forest habitat. Gleaning behavior vs aerial hawking.

bats.hp: Gleaning specialist — hovers near vegetation surfaces to pluck prey. Long ears enhance passive sound detection of insect movements on leaves.

bats.desc: Medium Myotis with proportionally longer legs and furred underside of wing membrane near body. Fast direct flight. Mountain conifer forests. Roosts under bark and in tree hollows.

fm.Color: Brown; fur extends onto underside of WING MEMBRANE near body (diagnostic); longer legs
fm.Habitat: Mountain conifer forests — roosts under bark, in tree hollows, buildings
fm.Behavior: FAST direct flight (faster than most Myotis); aerial hawker catching moths in open forest
fm.Diet: Moths — specialist moth predator in mountain forests
fm.vs: Fur on wing membrane underside distinguishes from other Myotis. Faster flight than California or Small-footed Myotis.

bats.hp: Fast aerial hawker in mountain forests. Fur on wing membrane underside is unique among LA Myotis — possibly improves thermoregulation during flight.

bats.desc: Unique ground-hunting bat — lands on the ground to capture scorpions, centipedes, and large insects. Immune to scorpion venom. Huge ears. Distinctive musky smell. Species of Special Concern.

fm.Color: Pale cream-yellow; huge EARS; large eyes; pink face
fm.Habitat: Rocky outcrops, desert scrub, oak woodland — roosts in rock crevices, mines, buildings
fm.Behavior: GROUND-HUNTING: lands on ground to capture scorpions and centipedes; also gleans from vegetation; distinctive musky smell
fm.Diet: Scorpions, centipedes, large beetles, crickets, Jerusalem crickets — hunts ON THE GROUND
fm.vs: No other LA bat routinely hunts on the GROUND. Enormous ears and pale color are diagnostic.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Declining from roost disturbance and habitat loss.
fm.Urban: Found in foothill communities near rocky outcrops. Listen for audible crunching sounds as it eats scorpions on the ground. Distinctive musky odor near roost sites.

bats.hp: Ground-gleaning bat — hunts scorpions, centipedes, and beetles on the ground rather than catching insects in flight. Immune to scorpion venom. Distinctive musky odor near roost sites. SSC — sensitive to roost disturbance.

bats.desc: Frosted black fur with silver tips. Migratory tree rooster. One of the slowest-flying bats — catches insects with slow deliberate flight in forest canopy.

fm.Color: Dark blackish-brown with SILVER-TIPPED fur (frosted appearance)
fm.Habitat: Oak and conifer forest; roosts in tree bark, hollows; migratory
fm.Behavior: Slow deliberate flight in forest canopy; solitary; migratory
fm.Diet: Moths, beetles, flies — forest canopy gleaner
fm.vs: Silver-frosted fur is distinctive. Only dark bat with silver-tipped hairs.
fm.Urban: Migratory — passes through LA in spring and fall. Slow deliberate flight through tree canopy in parks and cemeteries. Silver-frosted fur distinctive if seen at close range.

bats.hp: Slow methodical flight through forest canopy — catches insects by gleaning from vegetation. Migratory — passes through LA in spring and fall.

bats.desc: Large-eared Myotis of desert canyons. Larger ears than California Myotis. Gleans insects from vegetation. Rocky desert at eastern LA County edge.

fm.Color: Brown; LARGER EARS than other Myotis (extending past nose when pressed forward)
fm.Habitat: Desert canyon rock faces — eastern LA County
fm.Behavior: Gleaner — plucks insects from rock faces and vegetation; slow maneuverable flight
fm.Diet: Moths, beetles, spiders gleaned from surfaces
fm.vs: Larger ears than California or Small-footed Myotis. Not as large-eared as Townsend's Big-eared Bat.

bats.hp: Desert canyon gleaner. Large ears enhance passive listening for insect movements on rock faces. At western range limit in LA County.

bats.desc: The most visually spectacular bat in North America — jet black with THREE large WHITE SPOTS on back and ENORMOUS pink ears. Echolocation audible to humans. Extremely rare. One of the most sought-after wildlife sightings.

fm.Color: Jet BLACK with 3 large WHITE SPOTS on back (2 on shoulders, 1 on rump); enormous PINK ears
fm.Habitat: Rocky cliffs and canyons — roosts alone in cliff crevices. Very rare.
fm.Behavior: AUDIBLE echolocation (10 kHz — humans can hear it); solitary; cliff-roosting; high slow flight
fm.Diet: Moths — specialist moth hunter at high altitude
fm.vs: Absolutely UNMISTAKABLE — the only black bat with white spots and huge pink ears. Echolocation clicks audible to humans.
fm.Conservation: SSC. Very rare. Cliff-roosting limits habitat. Individual roosts — never colonial.

bats.hp: Most visually distinctive bat in North America. Audible echolocation at 10 kHz. Always solitary — never forms colonies. Among the rarest and most sought-after wildlife sightings in SoCal.

bats.desc: Dramatic enormous EARS — nearly as long as the body. Very sensitive to roost disturbance. Mines and caves. Declining steeply. Species of Special Concern.

fm.Color: Brown; enormously long EARS (over 1 in); two prominent nose lumps
fm.Habitat: Caves, mines, old buildings — extremely sensitive to roost disturbance
fm.Behavior: Hovers while gleaning moths from vegetation; curls enormous ears when roosting; extremely sensitive to human disturbance
fm.Diet: Moths — specialist moth hunter using passive listening (gleaning)
fm.vs: No other bat has proportionally ENORMOUS EARS like this species. Two nose lumps also diagnostic.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Extremely sensitive to disturbance — entire colonies abandon roosts if disturbed. Historic mine closures eliminating roost sites.

bats.hp: Among the most disturbance-sensitive bats — a single human visit can cause colony abandonment. Bat-compatible mine gates are critical conservation tool.

bats.desc: Tiny bat — among the smallest in North America. First bat to emerge each evening, often visible while still light. Erratic fluttery flight like a butterfly. Common in desert canyons.

fm.Size: Wingspan 8 in; body 2.5 in — one of the tiniest bats
fm.Color: Pale yellow-brown; tiny size; black face mask
fm.Habitat: Desert canyons, rocky cliffs — roosts in rock crevices
fm.Behavior: FIRST bat to emerge each evening — often seen before dark; erratic butterfly-like flight; forages over water
fm.Diet: Small flies, midges, mosquitoes
fm.vs: Extremely small with pale color. Earliest flier each evening distinguishes from other small bats.
fm.Urban: FIRST bat to emerge each evening — often seen before full dark. Erratic butterfly-like flight over desert canyon neighborhoods. Extremely small — frequently mistaken for a moth.

bats.hp: Earliest evening emerger — often mistaken for a large moth or butterfly in fading light. Desert canyon specialist.

bats.desc: Beautiful brick-red bat that roosts singly in tree foliage — hanging from leaves like a dead leaf. Migratory. Among the most colorful bats in North America.

fm.Color: Brick RED (males) to chestnut-brown (females); furred tail membrane
fm.Habitat: Roosts in tree foliage — hangs from leaves singly (not colonial); riparian corridors, orchards, urban trees
fm.Behavior: Solitary roost in tree leaves; migratory; moth specialist; often hunts around street lights
fm.Diet: Moths primarily; also beetles, crickets
fm.vs: No other LA bat is brick RED. Tree-roosting (not building-roosting) distinguishes from most urban bats.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Vulnerable to tree removal and pesticides. Migratory — status poorly understood.
fm.Urban: Roosts in urban tree foliage — sycamores, oaks, liquidambars. Hunts around street lights. Vulnerable to tree removal and pruning during maternity season (May–Aug).

bats.hp: Solitary tree rooster — hangs from leaves like a dead leaf. Color makes it one of the most beautiful bats. Vulnerable to tree removal.

bats.desc: Tiny Myotis with distinctive black FACE MASK and very small feet. Rocky desert canyons and foothills. Roosts in rock crevices. One of the smallest bats in LA County.

fm.Size: Wingspan 9 in; body 3 in — very small
fm.Color: Pale brown; distinctive black FACE MASK (dark ears and facial skin); very small feet
fm.Habitat: Rocky canyons, cliff faces — roosts in narrow rock crevices; desert foothills
fm.Behavior: Slow maneuverable flight close to rock faces; emerges late; rock crevice rooster
fm.Diet: Small moths, beetles, flies
fm.vs: Black FACE MASK distinguishes from California Myotis (which lacks dark face). Very small feet.
fm.Urban: Rock crevice rooster in foothill canyons. Black face mask visible at close range. One of the smallest bats in LA — sometimes mistaken for a large moth.

bats.hp: Rock crevice specialist — tiny body and small feet allow roosting in extremely narrow crevices inaccessible to larger bats. Black face mask diagnostic in the field.

bats.desc: Yellowish bat that roosts in PALM TREE skirts — the quintessential LA bat roost. Dead palm fronds provide ideal crevice roosts. Expanding range northward with planted palms.

fm.Color: Yellowish-brown; furred tail membrane
fm.Habitat: PALM TREES — roosts under dead palm frond skirts; expanding range with planted palms
fm.Behavior: Solitary tree rooster; associates specifically with fan palms (Washingtonia)
fm.Diet: Moths, beetles, flying insects
fm.vs: Yellowish color among the most distinctive of LA bats. Palm tree roost association is diagnostic.
fm.Urban: The quintessential LA bat — roosts under dead palm frond skirts. Removing palm skirts eliminates roost habitat. Associated specifically with Washingtonia fan palms.

bats.hp: Expanding range northward by exploiting planted Washingtonia fan palms as roost habitat. Dead palm frond skirts are critical roost sites — palm grooming by landscapers eliminates this bat from managed palm rows. The quintessential LA palm-roosting bat.

bats.desc: Small bat that forages low over water — specializes in aquatic insects. Roosts in buildings, bridges, and mines. Very common along rivers and lakes.

fm.Color: Pale brown; smaller and paler than Big Brown Bat
fm.Habitat: Near water — rivers, lakes, ponds; roosts in bridges, buildings, mines
fm.Behavior: Forages low over WATER surface — nearly touches surface catching insects; colonial roosts
fm.Diet: Aquatic insects — midges, mosquitoes, mayflies
fm.vs: Forages specifically over WATER. Very similar to California Myotis — requires hand examination for definitive ID.
fm.Urban: Very common along LA River, reservoirs, and urban lakes. Watch for bats skimming low over water at dusk — nearly touching the surface to catch midges and mosquitoes.

bats.hp: Water surface specialist — skims insects from just above water. Important mosquito predator along LA rivers and reservoirs.

marine_mammals.desc: The LARGEST animal ever to live on Earth — up to 100 ft and 200 tons. Feeds on krill off LA coast in summer. Heart is the size of a golf cart. Visible from whale-watching boats off Long Beach and San Pedro.

fm.Color: Blue-gray mottled; tiny dorsal fin far back on body; very tall columnar BLOW (30+ ft)
fm.Habitat: Deep offshore waters — feeds on krill concentrations off Continental shelf
fm.Behavior: Feeds by LUNGE-GULPING krill; blow is tall and columnar; rarely shows tail fluke; heartbeat audible for miles
fm.Diet: KRILL — up to 4 tons of krill per day during feeding season
fm.vs: Much LARGER and BLUER than any other whale. Tiny dorsal fin far back. Tall columnar blow. Mottled blue-gray coloring.
fm.Urban: Visible from whale-watching boats off Long Beach and San Pedro, Jul–Oct. The LARGEST animal ever to live on Earth. Tall columnar blow visible at great distance.

marine_mammals.hp: Largest animal in Earth's history. Off LA coast in summer following krill. Heart weighs 400 lbs, aorta large enough for a human to crawl through.

marine_mammals.desc: Second largest animal EVER — up to 85 feet. Asymmetric jaw coloring: right lower jaw is WHITE, left is dark. Increasingly seen off LA coast feeding on krill and schooling fish. Fast swimmer — 'greyhound of the sea.'

fm.Size: 65–85 ft; 40–80 tons — SECOND largest animal ever
fm.Color: Dark gray; ASYMMETRIC jaw — right lower jaw WHITE, left dark (unique among whales); tall falcate dorsal fin far back
fm.Habitat: Deep offshore waters — increasingly seen in SoCal during feeding season
fm.Behavior: Fast — up to 25 mph (greyhound of the sea); lunge-feeds; tall thin blow; rarely shows tail fluke
fm.Diet: Krill, schooling fish (anchovies, herring) — lunge-feeder
fm.vs: Larger than Humpback/Gray. ASYMMETRIC jaw coloring unique. Tall thin blow. Rarely shows tail fluke (unlike Humpback).
fm.Urban: Increasingly seen from whale-watching boats off LA coast during feeding season. Second largest animal ever. Asymmetric jaw coloring visible when lunging at surface.

marine_mammals.hp: Asymmetric jaw coloring is unique in the animal kingdom — right lower jaw white, left dark. Purpose debated — may help herd prey schools. Fastest large whale.

marine_mammals.desc: Increasingly common off LA — feeding on anchovy aggregations in Santa Monica Bay. Famous for complex SONGS lasting hours. Enormous white PECTORAL FINS (longest of any whale). Dramatic breaching.

fm.Color: Dark gray-black with WHITE pectoral fins (up to 15 ft long — longest of any whale); unique tail fluke pattern
fm.Habitat: Nearshore feeding areas — increasingly in Santa Monica Bay summer/fall
fm.Behavior: Spectacular BREACHING; complex SONGS lasting hours; bubble-net feeding; shows tail fluke when diving
fm.Diet: Krill, small schooling fish (anchovy, sardine) — lunge-feeds through bait balls
fm.vs: Long WHITE pectoral fins distinguish from all other whales. Gray Whale has NO dorsal fin. Blue Whale is much larger and blue.

marine_mammals.hp: Increasingly common off LA coast since ~2010 — following anchovy schools into Santa Monica Bay. Each tail fluke pattern is unique like a fingerprint.

marine_mammals.desc: Smallest baleen whale off LA — only 25-30 feet. Pointed snout. White band on pectoral flippers diagnostic. Often solitary. Can be difficult to spot due to small size and inconspicuous blow.

fm.Size: 25–30 ft; 6–10 tons — smallest LA baleen whale
fm.Color: Dark gray with white BAND on pectoral flippers (diagnostic); pointed triangular snout
fm.Habitat: Nearshore to offshore — sometimes in harbors and close to shore
fm.Behavior: Solitary or pairs; inconspicuous low blow; curious — sometimes approaches boats; lunges through bait balls
fm.Diet: Krill, small schooling fish
fm.vs: Much SMALLER than other baleen whales. White PECTORAL BAND diagnostic. Pointed snout.
fm.Urban: Occasionally enters harbors and close to shore. Curious — sometimes approaches stationary boats. Low inconspicuous blow makes it easy to miss from shore.

marine_mammals.hp: Smallest baleen whale in SoCal waters. Curious — sometimes approaches stationary boats. Low blow makes them difficult to spot from shore.

marine_mammals.desc: The dolphin of Santa Monica Bay — resident pods visible from shore year-round. Surfs waves, bow-rides boats, and feeds in kelp beds. Extremely intelligent. LA's most visible marine mammal.

fm.Color: Gray dorsally fading to lighter belly; bulbous forehead (melon); prominent DORSAL FIN
fm.Habitat: Coastal waters, Santa Monica Bay, harbors — resident year-round
fm.Behavior: Rides bow waves of boats; surfs ocean waves; cooperative feeding; highly social pods; leaping and spinning
fm.Diet: Fish, squid — cooperative herding of fish schools
fm.vs: Common Dolphin is smaller with hourglass PATTERN on sides. Bottlenose is larger, UNIFORM gray, with more robust build.
fm.Urban: Visible from Manhattan Beach, Santa Monica Pier, Malibu. Resident pods in Santa Monica Bay year-round.

marine_mammals.hp: Resident pods in Santa Monica Bay — among the most urbanized dolphin populations. Extremely intelligent — complex social structures and cooperative hunting.

marine_mammals.desc: Fast-swimming dolphin with distinctive yellow-tan HOURGLASS pattern on sides. Forms enormous pods — sometimes thousands. Often seen in massive 'superpods' offshore.

fm.Color: Dark gray back with distinctive yellow-tan HOURGLASS pattern on sides (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Offshore and nearshore waters — can come close to shore
fm.Behavior: Very fast; leaps high; forms massive SUPERPODS of hundreds to thousands; high-energy swimming
fm.Diet: Sardines, anchovies, squid — follows schools of small fish
fm.vs: Bottlenose is LARGER and UNIFORM gray. Common Dolphin is smaller with HOURGLASS pattern on sides.
fm.Urban: Visible from whale-watching boats. Superpods occasionally seen from Palos Verdes cliffs.

marine_mammals.hp: Can form superpods of 1,000+ individuals during seasonal fish concentrations. Among the fastest cetaceans — 37 mph documented.

marine_mammals.desc: Apex marine predator — transient pods occasionally pass through LA waters hunting gray whale calves, sea lions, and dolphins. Dramatic black-and-white coloring. Seen from whale-watching boats several times per year.

fm.Size: 20–32 ft; males up to 12,000 lbs. Males have TALL dorsal fin (up to 6 ft)
fm.Color: Jet BLACK with WHITE patches behind eye, on belly, and behind dorsal fin; white eye patch; tall dorsal fin
fm.Habitat: Open ocean — transient pods pass through SoCal waters hunting marine mammals
fm.Behavior: Transient pods HUNT gray whale calves, sea lions, dolphins; highly intelligent; cooperative hunting strategies; matrilineal pods
fm.Diet: Marine mammals (transient ecotype) — gray whale calves, sea lions, dolphins, seals
fm.vs: Unmistakable — BLACK AND WHITE, tall dorsal fin, massive size. The only dolphin this large.

marine_mammals.hp: LA sightings typically transient (mammal-eating) ecotype hunting gray whale calves during northbound migration (Mar–May). Seen several times per year from whale-watching boats.

marine_mammals.desc: Acrobatic dolphin with distinctive pale gray side patches and hooked dorsal fin. Often in large pods offshore. More pelagic than Bottlenose — seen from whale-watching boats.

fm.Color: Dark back with pale GRAY SIDE PATCHES; hooked dorsal fin (unique bi-colored — dark leading edge, pale trailing)
fm.Habitat: Offshore waters — more pelagic than Bottlenose
fm.Behavior: Highly ACROBATIC — frequent aerial spins, flips, and belly-flops; large pods; bow-rides
fm.Diet: Squid, anchovies, hake
fm.vs: Bottlenose is larger, uniform gray, nearshore. Pacific White-sided has pale SIDE PATCHES and is more offshore and acrobatic.
fm.Urban: Seen from whale-watching boats — large acrobatic pods of 100+. Frequent aerial spins and belly-flops. More pelagic than Bottlenose dolphins.

marine_mammals.hp: Highly acrobatic offshore dolphin — belly-flops, spins, and somersaults more than any other LA cetacean. Large pods (100+) travel with Northern Right Whale Dolphins. More pelagic than Bottlenose — requires boat trips to see.

marine_mammals.desc: Large heavily scarred dolphin — adults are nearly WHITE from accumulated tooth-rake scars. Blunt head with no beak. Deep-diving squid specialist. Seen from whale-watching boats off LA.

fm.Color: Gray when young, becoming increasingly WHITE with age from accumulated SCARS; blunt head; no beak
fm.Habitat: Deep offshore waters — shelf edge and beyond
fm.Behavior: Deep diver; squid specialist; slower than other dolphins; adults distinctively scarred from conspecific raking
fm.Diet: SQUID specialist — deep-diving predator
fm.vs: Much LARGER than other dolphins. Blunt head (no beak). Adults nearly WHITE from scarring — no other dolphin looks like this.
fm.Urban: Deep offshore — seen from whale-watching boats. Old adults nearly white from tooth-rake scars. Blunt head with no beak. Squid specialist.

marine_mammals.hp: Deep-water squid specialist rarely seen from shore. Each individual identifiable by unique tooth-rake scar pattern — heavily scarred adults appear nearly white. Pods typically 5-30, smaller than Common Dolphin schools.

marine_mammals.desc: The great migration — 10,000+ miles from Alaska to Baja breeding lagoons, passing close to LA shores Dec–Apr. Visible from cliffs at Point Dume, Palos Verdes, and Point Vicente. One of the great wildlife spectacles of LA.

fm.Color: Gray with white mottled patches (barnacle scars); NO dorsal fin — series of knuckle-like bumps
fm.Habitat: Coastal migration close to shore (within 1–2 miles); breeding lagoons in Baja California
fm.Behavior: Southbound Dec–Feb, northbound Mar–May (mothers with calves closest to shore northbound); BLOW is heart-shaped (two blowholes); breaching, spy-hopping
fm.Diet: Amphipods from sea floor mud — the only bottom-feeding baleen whale
fm.vs: Blue whale is much LARGER and BLUE. Humpback has long WHITE flippers. Gray Whale has MOTTLED gray pattern and NO dorsal fin.
fm.Urban: Point Vicente Interpretive Center (Palos Verdes) is premier whale watching spot. Peak southbound: Jan. Peak northbound with calves: Mar–Apr.

marine_mammals.hp: One of the longest migrations of any mammal — 10,000+ miles round trip. Bottom feeder — scoops amphipods from seafloor mud, filtering through baleen.

marine_mammals.desc: Small deep-diving whale occasionally found stranded on LA County beaches. Much smaller than the Sperm Whale — only 10-11 ft. Underslung lower jaw gives a shark-like profile. Carries a reddish-brown fluid in its intestine that it releases as a defensive 'ink cloud' when startled.
marine_mammals.hp: Most commonly stranded cetacean in LA County — deep-water species that comes ashore when sick or disoriented. Squid specialist diving to 1,000+ ft. The defensive ink cloud is unique among cetaceans. Beach strandings should be reported to NOAA/Channel Islands Marine & Wildlife Institute.

fm.Size: 10-11 ft, 700-900 lbs — much smaller than Sperm Whale
fm.Color: Dark blue-gray above, lighter below; false gill marking behind eye
fm.Habitat: Deep offshore waters; seen alive from boats very rarely. Known mainly from beach strandings.
fm.Diet: Deep-water squid, octopus — dives to 1,000+ ft
fm.vs: Sperm Whale is MUCH larger (40-60 ft). Dwarf Sperm Whale (K. sima) is slightly smaller. Both Kogia species have underslung jaw and false gill mark.

marine_mammals.desc: Federally Threatened sea otter slowly expanding its range southward toward LA. Occasional sightings in Malibu and Santa Monica Bay. Uses rocks as tools to crack shellfish on chest. Keystone species of kelp ecosystems.

fm.Color: Brown to black; flat tail; dense waterproof FUR (densest of any mammal — 1 million hairs/sq in)
fm.Habitat: Kelp beds — floats on back at surface; rarely comes ashore
fm.Behavior: Floats on BACK; uses ROCKS as tools to crack shellfish on chest; wraps in kelp to sleep; holds hands with mate
fm.Diet: Sea urchins (critical), abalone, crabs, clams, mussels — uses tools
fm.vs: Sea lions are much LARGER and haul out on shore. Otters are smaller, float on BACKS in kelp, and use TOOLS.
fm.Conservation: Federally Threatened. Slowly expanding range south. Occasional sightings in Malibu suggest future LA County colonization. Main threats: oil spills, shark bites.

marine_mammals.hp: Keystone species — otter predation on sea urchins prevents urchin barrens, allowing kelp forests to thrive. One otter protects acres of kelp.

marine_mammals.desc: The classic 'seal' of LA — the barking, fish-catching pinnipeds on docks and breakwaters. Technically a sea LION (has external ear flaps and can walk on flippers). Visible at every harbor in LA County.

fm.Color: Brown to tan; males develop large forehead BUMP (sagittal crest); external EAR FLAPS (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Harbors, breakwaters, buoys, docks, rocky shores — hauls out on any flat surface
fm.Behavior: BARKING calls; gregarious; walks on all four flippers; porpoises through surf; intelligent — trained for shows
fm.Diet: Fish, squid, octopus
fm.vs: Harbor Seal has NO ear flaps and CANNOT walk on flippers (scoots). Sea Lion HAS ear flaps and WALKS on flippers.
fm.Urban: Abundant at King Harbor (Redondo), Marina del Rey, San Pedro, Long Beach. Watch from any harbor pier.

marine_mammals.hp: Males establish breeding territories on Channel Islands. Non-breeding animals (mostly males) haul out on LA harbors year-round.

marine_mammals.desc: Rare fur seal from Guadalupe Island, Mexico — occasional vagrant to Southern California. Darker and smaller than California Sea Lion with pointed snout and dense underfur. Recovering from near-extinction.

fm.Size: 4–6 ft; 80–300 lbs — smaller than CA Sea Lion
fm.Color: Dark brown to black; pointed SNOUT; dense underfur (FUR seal — hunted for fur historically)
fm.Habitat: Rocky shorelines — vagrant from Guadalupe Island, Mexico. Occasional LA County hauled-out individuals.
fm.Behavior: Less gregarious than sea lions; pointed snout profile; dense fur visible when dry
fm.Diet: Squid, fish
fm.vs: Darker and smaller than CA Sea Lion. Pointed SNOUT (Sea Lion has blunt muzzle). Dense underfur visible when dry.

marine_mammals.hp: Hunted to near-extinction for fur — population dropped to ~200 by 1950s. Recovering. Occasional vagrants to SoCal from Guadalupe Island breeding colony.

marine_mammals.desc: Largest sea lion species — males reach 11 ft and 2,500 lbs. Occasional visitor to LA County coast, more common in central/northern California. Much larger and paler than the ubiquitous California Sea Lion. Deep roaring vocalization unlike the barking of California Sea Lions.
marine_mammals.hp: Occasional winter visitor to LA coast. Listed as Near Threatened globally. Western DPS is federally endangered (not in LA range). Distinguished from California Sea Lion by much larger size, lighter color, and deeper roaring calls.

fm.Size: Males 10-11 ft, 1500-2500 lbs — MUCH larger than California Sea Lion
fm.Color: Tawny to light brown — paler than dark California Sea Lion
fm.Habitat: Rocky coastline, breakwaters, channel markers — occasional visitor
fm.Behavior: Deep roaring calls (not barking). Less gregarious than California Sea Lion.
fm.vs: California Sea Lion is smaller (7 ft), darker brown, and BARKS. Steller is huge, pale, and ROARS.

marine_mammals.desc: Spotted 'True seal' — round head, no ear flaps, scoots on belly (cannot walk on flippers). Less gregarious than sea lions. Hauls out on isolated beaches and rocks. Leo Carrillo and Point Dume.

fm.Color: Gray with dark SPOTS; round head; NO external ear flaps (True seal); large dark eyes
fm.Habitat: Rocky shores, isolated beaches, breakwaters — less tolerant of disturbance than sea lions
fm.Behavior: CANNOT walk on flippers — scoots on belly; less vocal than sea lions; curious but wary; nurses pups on shore
fm.Diet: Fish, squid, crustaceans
fm.vs: Sea Lion HAS ear flaps and WALKS on flippers. Harbor Seal has NO ear flaps and SCOOTS on belly.
fm.Urban: Point Dume, Leo Carrillo, Carpinteria. Pupping season (Feb–May) — maintain 50 ft distance from hauled-out seals.

marine_mammals.hp: Pupping season Feb–May. Keep 50+ ft distance — mothers abandon pups if disturbed. Do NOT 'rescue' lone pups on beaches — mother is likely nearby.

marine_mammals.desc: Massive — males reach 5,000 lbs with enormous PROBOSCIS nose. Breeds on Channel Islands. Occasionally hauls out on mainland beaches. Dives to 5,000+ ft depth — among the deepest diving mammals.

fm.Color: Gray-brown; males have enormous floppy PROBOSCIS nose (inflated during territorial displays)
fm.Habitat: Channel Islands for breeding; pelagic at sea; occasional mainland beach haul-outs
fm.Behavior: Males fight violently for harems; dives to 5,000+ ft for 1–2 hours; molts catastrophically (peels off skin)
fm.Diet: Squid, fish, rays, small sharks — deep water feeding
fm.vs: MUCH larger than any other seal or sea lion. Male's enormous PROBOSCIS nose is unmistakable.
fm.Urban: Breeding colony at San Nicolas and San Miguel Islands. Occasional hauled-out individuals on LA County beaches.

marine_mammals.hp: Recovered from near-extinction (~100 individuals in 1890s) to 175,000+ today. Conservation success story.

marine_mammals.desc: Stocky fast porpoise — creates distinctive ROOSTER-TAIL splash when surfacing at speed. Black-and-white body pattern resembles a small orca. Seen from whale-watching boats, especially in cooler months.

fm.Color: Black with large WHITE FLANKS patch; small triangular dorsal fin with white tip
fm.Habitat: Cool offshore waters — more common in cooler months
fm.Behavior: Creates distinctive ROOSTER-TAIL spray when surfacing at speed; rarely leaps clear; stocky fast swimmer; bow-rides briefly
fm.Diet: Squid, small fish
fm.vs: Stocky black-and-white body. ROOSTER-TAIL spray diagnostic. Does NOT leap clear of water like dolphins. Much smaller than Orca.

marine_mammals.hp: Fastest porpoise — creates diagnostic rooster-tail splash visible at distance. More common off LA in winter when waters cool.

marine_mammals.desc: Largest toothed whale — males reach 60 ft. Deep-water species occasionally seen from whale-watching boats far offshore or found stranded on LA beaches. Enormous square head (contains spermaceti organ) is unmistakable. Can dive to 7,000+ ft — the deepest-diving mammal.
marine_mammals.hp: Deepest-diving mammal on Earth — dives exceed 7,000 ft lasting 90+ minutes hunting giant squid. Occasional LA coast strandings. The spermaceti organ in the massive head may function in echolocation focusing and buoyancy control. Historically hunted nearly to extinction for spermaceti oil.

fm.Size: Males 50-60 ft — largest toothed animal on Earth
fm.Color: Dark gray-brown, wrinkled skin; ENORMOUS square head (1/3 of body length)
fm.Habitat: Deep offshore waters. Rarely seen from shore. Known from strandings and deep-water whale watching.
fm.Diet: Giant squid, deep-water fish — dives to 7,000+ ft
fm.vs: No other whale has the massive square head. Blue/Fin/Humpback whales have baleen and different head shapes. Sperm Whale head is unmistakable.

freshwater_fish.desc: Iconic LA beach-spawning fish — rides waves onto sandy beaches on the highest tides of spring and summer to lay eggs in wet sand. Eggs incubate in sand for two weeks, hatching when the next high tide series washes them free. One of the most spectacular wildlife events in LA County.
freshwater_fish.hp: Grunion runs are a uniquely Southern California wildlife spectacle — thousands of fish surf onto beaches on full and new moon high tides, March–August. Cabrillo Marine Aquarium hosts public grunion watching events. Protected during April–May spawning peak (no take without license).

fm.Color: Silver-green with blue-green back
fm.Habitat: Sandy beaches for spawning; coastal waters otherwise. Cabrillo Beach, Dockweiler, many LA beaches.
fm.Behavior: Spawns ON BEACH — females dig tail-first into sand during highest tides; males curl around to fertilize. 2-3 nights after full/new moons.
fm.Diet: Plankton, small crustaceans in ocean
fm.vs: Jack Silverside and Topsmelt are similar silversides but do NOT spawn on beaches. Grunion runs are unique.
fm.Urban: Watch grunion runs at Cabrillo Beach, Dockweiler, Manhattan Beach — March through August, 2-3 nights after full/new moons.

freshwater_fish.desc: Largest silverside on the Pacific coast — reaching 18 inches. Native to bays, estuaries, and coastal lagoons. Important recreational species — targeted by pier anglers. Schools near surface in harbors and marina areas.
freshwater_fish.hp: Largest West Coast silverside — an important forage and recreational species in LA harbors and bays. Declining in some areas from water quality degradation in coastal lagoons. Good indicator of estuary health.

fm.Color: Silver with blue-green back; prominent lateral stripe
fm.Habitat: Bays, harbors, estuaries, pier pilings — King Harbor, Marina del Rey
fm.vs: Topsmelt is smaller (5-8 in). Inland Silverside is freshwater and smaller. Jack Silverside is the largest at 10-18 in.

freshwater_fish.desc: Native silverside of estuaries, lagoons, and coastal waterways. Schooling fish visible from shore as silvery flashes in shallow water. Important forage species for larger predators. Found in Malibu Lagoon, Ballona Creek estuary, and harbor areas.
freshwater_fish.hp: Key forage fish in coastal lagoon ecosystems — connects marine and freshwater food webs. Indicator of lagoon health. Schools can number in the thousands. Tolerates wide range of salinity from full seawater to nearly fresh.

fm.Color: Silver with blue-green iridescent back; lateral silver stripe
fm.Habitat: Estuaries, lagoons, harbors — Malibu Lagoon, Ballona Creek, marina areas
fm.Diet: Algae, small crustaceans, plankton — omnivorous surface feeder
fm.vs: Jack Silverside is larger (to 18 in) and more elongate. Inland Silverside is smaller and freshwater. Topsmelt is medium-sized and estuarine.
fm.Urban: Visible in harbors, lagoons, and beach-adjacent waterways. Schools flash silver in shallows.

freshwater_fish.desc: Federally Threatened native fish of LA County streams. Small bottom-feeding sucker requiring clean gravelly streams. One of the most endangered freshwater fish in California. San Gabriel River populations are a last stronghold.

fm.Color: Dark olive above, pale below; subterminal (downward-facing) mouth for bottom feeding
fm.Habitat: Clean clear streams with gravel and cobble bottom — requires specific substrate
fm.Behavior: Bottom feeder; scrapes algae and invertebrates from rocks; needs flowing water with gravel substrate
fm.Diet: Algae, diatoms, small invertebrates scraped from rocks
fm.vs: Arroyo Chub is deeper-bodied with terminal (forward-facing) mouth. Sucker has subterminal (downward) mouth.
fm.Conservation: Federally Threatened. Restricted to upper San Gabriel River, Big Tujunga Creek, and a few other streams. Threats: urbanization, water diversion, introduced predators, sedimentation.

freshwater_fish.hp: San Gabriel River contains one of the last viable populations. Requires clean, flowing, gravel-bottomed streams — habitat increasingly rare in LA County.

freshwater_fish.desc: Native sucker of larger streams — related to Santa Ana Sucker but more widespread. Bottom feeder with downward-facing mouth. Declining but more common than Santa Ana Sucker.

fm.Color: Dark olive above, pale below; subterminal (downward) mouth with fleshy lips for bottom feeding
fm.Habitat: Streams, rivers — rocky and gravel bottoms; cleaner than what carp tolerate
fm.Diet: Algae, diatoms, small invertebrates scraped from rocks — bottom feeder
fm.vs: Similar to Santa Ana Sucker but larger and more widespread. Both have downward-facing SUCKER mouth.
fm.Urban: Native bottom-feeder in tributaries of the LA River and San Gabriel River. Declining but still present in some urban-adjacent streams.

freshwater_fish.hp: Native bottom-feeder persisting in some LA County tributaries. More widespread than Federally Threatened Santa Ana Sucker.

freshwater_fish.desc: Native sculpin of estuaries and coastal lagoons. Named for the antler-like spine on each gill cover. Bottom-dwelling ambush predator. Found in Ballona Creek estuary, Malibu Lagoon, and harbor areas. Tolerates low-salinity water and enters coastal streams.
freshwater_fish.hp: Native estuarine predator. Antler-like preopercular spines are unique among LA County sculpins. Can enter fresh water — found upstream in coastal creeks. Important food web link between marine and freshwater ecosystems.

fm.Color: Olive-brown, mottled; ANTLER-LIKE spines on gill covers
fm.Habitat: Estuaries, lagoons, lower coastal streams — Ballona, Malibu Lagoon
fm.Diet: Small fish, crabs, shrimp — bottom ambush predator
fm.vs: Prickly Sculpin is freshwater-only and lacks antler-like spines. Staghorn has distinctive branched preopercular spine.

freshwater_fish.desc: Native bottom-dwelling fish of clean streams. Flat head with large fan-like pectoral fins. Hides under rocks. One of few native fish persisting in LA County streams. Indicator of good water quality.

fm.Color: Mottled brown-olive; flat broad HEAD; large fan-like PECTORAL fins; prickly scales on skin
fm.Habitat: Clean streams with rocky bottom — hides under rocks in riffles; requires cool clean water
fm.Behavior: Bottom-dwelling; ambush predator hiding under rocks; nests under rocks — male guards eggs
fm.Diet: Aquatic insects, small crustaceans, fish eggs
fm.vs: Only sculpin in LA County freshwater. Flat head + large pectoral fins + bottom-dwelling is diagnostic.
fm.Urban: Presence indicates clean stream habitat — a water quality indicator species. Found in upper San Gabriel River and tributaries.

freshwater_fish.hp: Water quality indicator — one of few native fish that cannot tolerate degraded urban stream conditions. Feeds by ambush from under rocks at night. Populations in upper San Gabriel River tributaries are conservation priorities.

freshwater_fish.desc: Native minnow of LA's coastal streams. Deep-bodied, olive-colored. Species of Special Concern. One of few native freshwater fish persisting in LA County streams. Tolerates warm, slow water better than Santa Ana Sucker.

fm.Color: Olive-gray with faint lateral stripe; deep body; terminal mouth
fm.Habitat: Slow to moderate streams, pools — more tolerant of warm water than Santa Ana Sucker
fm.Behavior: Schooling; spawns over gravel in spring; tolerates wider range of conditions than most native fish
fm.Diet: Algae, aquatic invertebrates, small crustaceans
fm.vs: More deep-bodied than introduced minnow species. Terminal mouth distinguishes from bottom-feeding suckers.
fm.Conservation: Species of Special Concern. Displaced by introduced fish throughout former range. Malibu Creek, LA River tributaries.
fm.Urban: Native minnow persisting in Malibu Creek, upper LA River tributaries, and Santa Clara River. Indicator of relatively intact stream habitat in urban-adjacent areas.

freshwater_fish.hp: One of the most tolerant native freshwater fish — persists in streams where other natives have been eliminated. Important indicator of stream health.

freshwater_fish.desc: Large native minnow — can reach 4 feet. Formerly in upper LA River system. Now extremely rare or extirpated from LA County. Major predator in native fish assemblages.

fm.Size: 12–36 in; can reach 4 ft — largest native minnow
fm.Color: Olive-bronze; deeply forked tail; large mouth for a minnow — piscivorous
fm.Habitat: Deep pools in larger streams and rivers — needs deep water
fm.Diet: Fish, crayfish, frogs — top predator among native fish
fm.vs: Much LARGER than any other minnow. Piscivorous — unusual for Cyprinidae.

freshwater_fish.hp: Top native predatory fish in SoCal streams. Virtually extirpated from LA County. Among the largest minnows in North America.

freshwater_fish.desc: Small native minnow of mountain streams. Dark speckled pattern. One of few remaining native freshwater fish in LA County mountain streams.

fm.Color: Dark olive with fine dark SPECKLES; breeding males develop orange on lips and fin bases
fm.Habitat: Mountain streams with rocky bottoms — needs clean flowing water
fm.Diet: Aquatic insects, algae scraped from rocks
fm.vs: Smaller and more speckled than Arroyo Chub. Mountain stream habitat.

freshwater_fish.hp: Recently split from R. osculus. LA County populations are the newly described R. gabrielino, endemic to the Los Angeles Basin. Genetically distinct from other speckled dace populations.

freshwater_fish.desc: Small native killifish of coastal lagoons and salt marshes. Important indicator of wetland health. Found in Ballona Wetlands, Malibu Lagoon, and other coastal habitats. Tolerates extreme salinity and temperature fluctuations.
freshwater_fish.hp: Key indicator species for coastal wetland health. Ballona Wetlands populations are among the southernmost. Tolerates salinity from fresh to twice seawater concentration — one of the most euryhaline fish in California.

fm.Color: Olive-green with silver belly; breeding males have dark bars
fm.Habitat: Salt marshes, coastal lagoons — Ballona Wetlands, Malibu Lagoon
fm.Diet: Mosquito larvae, small crustaceans, algae
fm.vs: Mosquitofish is freshwater and gives live birth. Killifish is estuarine/saltwater and lays eggs.
fm.Conservation: Indicator species for coastal wetland health. Ballona Wetlands restoration will benefit this species.

freshwater_fish.desc: Coastal/anadromous form with full lateral armor plates. Distinguished from the Federally Endangered unarmored subspecies by presence of bony PLATES along sides.

fm.Color: Silver-green with 3 dorsal spines; full set of lateral ARMOR PLATES; breeding males develop red throat
fm.Habitat: Coastal streams, estuaries, lagoons — anadromous or resident
fm.Diet: Aquatic invertebrates, plankton
fm.vs: Armored form has full set of lateral PLATES. Endangered unarmored subspecies LACKS plates.
fm.Urban: Coastal streams and lagoons — Malibu Lagoon, Ballona Creek estuary. Breeding males with red throats visible in spring in shallow pools.

freshwater_fish.hp: The armored coastal form — distinct from the Federally Endangered unarmored inland subspecies. Male nest-building is a classic study in animal behavior.

freshwater_fish.desc: Federally and State Endangered — one of the most endangered fish in California. Lacks the bony armor plates of coastal stickleback. Males build elaborate NESTS and perform courtship dances. Upper Santa Clara River population.

fm.Color: Olive to silver; males develop bright RED throat and blue EYES during breeding; three dorsal spines; LACKS lateral armor plates
fm.Habitat: Slow pools and backwaters of upper Santa Clara River and tributaries
fm.Behavior: Males build elaborate NESTS of plant material; courtship zig-zag DANCE; males guard eggs
fm.Diet: Aquatic invertebrates, algae, plankton
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered (FE), State Endangered (SE). One of the rarest fish in California. Restricted to upper Santa Clara River drainage and a few other populations. Threats: water diversion, introduced predators, habitat degradation.
fm.vs: Coastal Threespine Stickleback is larger and has full lateral plates. Unarmored form lacks plates — CRITICALLY ENDANGERED. Range determines ID.

freshwater_fish.hp: The unarmored subspecies is one of the rarest vertebrates in California. Male nest-building and courtship behavior is a classic study system in animal behavior.

freshwater_fish.desc: Native trout of mountain streams — the same species as steelhead (anadromous form). Mountain populations are resident (non-migratory). Cold clean mountain streams in SGM.

fm.Color: Dark spots on olive back; pink-red RAINBOW stripe along sides; spotted tail
fm.Habitat: Cold clear mountain streams — SGM above 3,000 ft
fm.Behavior: Requires cold (<68°F), well-oxygenated water; feeds on insects at surface and in drift
fm.Diet: Aquatic insects, terrestrial insects that fall in water, small fish
fm.vs: Brook trout (introduced) has lighter spots on dark background and square tail. Rainbow has dark spots on lighter background and slightly forked tail.

freshwater_fish.hp: The non-migratory form of steelhead trout. Mountain stream populations represent important genetic diversity. Stocking of hatchery fish threatens wild genetics.

freshwater_fish.desc: Federally Endangered anadromous form of rainbow trout. Once ran up Malibu Creek, LA River, San Gabriel River, and Santa Clara River in the thousands. Now functionally extirpated from most LA County streams. Restoration efforts ongoing.

fm.Color: Silver in ocean; develops rainbow stripe in fresh water; sea-run adults are larger and more silvery
fm.Habitat: Ocean (feeding) → coastal streams (spawning); needs passage from ocean to upstream gravel beds
fm.Behavior: ANADROMOUS: lives in ocean, migrates up coastal streams to spawn in winter/spring; some survive to spawn multiple times
fm.Diet: Ocean: fish, squid, crustaceans. Stream: aquatic insects
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered. Southern DPS. Functionally extirpated from most LA County streams due to: dams, channelization, water diversion. Restoration efforts in Malibu Creek, Topanga Creek.
fm.vs: Brown Trout has dark spots with light halos. Brook Trout has worm-like marks on back. Steelhead has small black spots, pink lateral stripe, and no halos.

freshwater_fish.hp: Historic runs in Malibu Creek numbered in the thousands. Rindge Dam removal (Malibu Creek) is a landmark restoration project to reconnect ocean to spawning habitat.

freshwater_fish.desc: Small silvery schooling fish in LA County lakes and channels. Horizontal silver STRIPE along sides. Surface feeder. Extremely common but overlooked.

fm.Color: Translucent with bright silver STRIPE along sides; greenish back
fm.Habitat: Lakes, channels, estuaries — surface and midwater schools
fm.Diet: Plankton, small insects, mosquito larvae
fm.vs: Silver STRIPE along sides. Surface schooling. Smaller and more translucent than Threadfin Shad.
fm.Urban: Surface schools visible from shore at most LA lakes and reservoirs. Important forage for bass and herons. Look for silvery flashes in shallows.

freshwater_fish.hp: Important forage fish — eaten by bass, herons, terns. Surface schooling makes them visible from shore.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced freshwater silverside spreading through LA waterways. Originally stocked for mosquito control (ineffective). Now competing with native Inland Silverside and Arroyo Chub. Surface-schooling fish visible in urban lakes and channels.
freshwater_fish.hp: Introduced for mosquito control — ineffective at controlling mosquitoes and now competing with native fish. Spreading through LA waterways. Distinguished from native Inland Silverside by slightly different body proportions and scale counts.

fm.Color: Silver with lateral stripe — very similar to Inland Silverside
fm.Habitat: Lakes, reservoirs, urban channels — spreading through LA waterways
fm.vs: Very similar to native Inland Silverside. Distinguished by scale counts (not field-identifiable). Assume Inland Silverside unless confirmed by expert.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced panfish — irregular dark speckles (no vertical bars like White Crappie). Deep body. Common in LA County lakes and reservoirs.

fm.Color: Silvery with irregular dark SPECKLES (no vertical bars); deep compressed body; 7–8 dorsal spines
fm.Habitat: Lakes, reservoirs — clearer water than White Crappie
fm.Diet: Small fish, aquatic insects
fm.vs: White Crappie has VERTICAL BARS and 5–6 spines. Black Crappie has irregular SPECKLES and 7–8 spines.
fm.Urban: In clearer LA reservoirs. Similar to White Crappie but prefers cleaner water. Schools near underwater structure.

freshwater_fish.hp: Prefers clearer water than White Crappie. 7–8 dorsal spines (vs 5–6 in White Crappie). Popular panfish.

freshwater_fish.desc: Small pan-shaped sunfish introduced from eastern US. Now in every lake and pond. Dark ear flap. Nest-guarding males. Important forage fish for bass and birds.

fm.Color: Dark olive with orange breast; dark BLUE-BLACK ear flap; deep compressed body
fm.Habitat: Lakes, ponds, slow streams — any standing water with vegetation
fm.Behavior: Nest-guarding males; schooling; feeds at all water levels
fm.Diet: Insects, small crustaceans, worms, small fish, algae
fm.Urban: In every lake and pond in LA County. Common fishing target for children.
fm.vs: Green Sunfish has blue face streaks and larger mouth. Redear Sunfish has red margin on ear flap. Bluegill has solid dark ear flap and small mouth.

freshwater_fish.hp: Introduced from eastern US. Now one of the most abundant fish in LA County lakes. Important forage base for largemouth bass.

freshwater_fish.desc: Aggressive introduced sunfish found in every warm waterway in LA. Larger mouth than Bluegill. Very tolerant of poor water quality. Predator of native fish larvae.

fm.Color: Olive-green with blue-green sheen; dark ear flap with pale ORANGE margin; large MOUTH
fm.Habitat: Any warm water — streams, ponds, ditches, channels
fm.Diet: Insects, small fish, crayfish — aggressive predator
fm.vs: Larger MOUTH than Bluegill. Blue-green cheek bars. Orange margin on dark ear flap.
fm.Urban: In every warm ditch, storm channel, and urban creek in LA County. Tolerates water quality too degraded for other species. Common in the concrete LA River channel.

freshwater_fish.hp: Extremely tolerant of degraded water. Aggressive predator that eliminates native fish from small streams and pools.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced game fish now in every lake and pond in LA County. Voracious predator of native fish, frogs, and crayfish. Major driver of native fish decline. Popular sport fish.

fm.Color: Green to olive with dark lateral stripe; large MOUTH extends past eye; deep body
fm.Habitat: Lakes, ponds, slow rivers, reservoirs — any standing or slow water
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; hides in vegetation and structure; explosive strikes on prey
fm.Diet: Fish, frogs, crayfish, insects, small mammals — voracious generalist predator
fm.vs: Mouth extends PAST eye (smallmouth bass mouth does not reach past eye). Dark lateral stripe.
fm.Urban: Stocked in every lake and reservoir in LA County for sport fishing. Major predator of native fish.

freshwater_fish.hp: Dominant warmwater predator in every LA County reservoir. Stocked aggressively for sportfishing — displaces native fish throughout range. Tournament bass fishing is a major recreational activity at Hansen Dam, Puddingstone, Santa Fe Dam.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced sunfish with distinctive RED margin on ear flap. Snail specialist — crushes snail shells with pharyngeal teeth. Also called 'shellcracker.'

fm.Color: Olive-green with dark ear flap bordered by bright RED (diagnostic); deeper body than Green Sunfish
fm.Habitat: Lakes, ponds, reservoirs — prefers clear water with snails
fm.Diet: SNAILS — crushes shells with pharyngeal teeth; also insects, crayfish
fm.vs: RED margin on ear flap (vs orange in Green Sunfish, blue-black in Bluegill). Snail-crushing diet unique.
fm.Urban: In clearer LA County reservoirs and lakes. Less common than Bluegill. Look for the distinctive red margin on the ear flap.

freshwater_fish.hp: Introduced from southeastern US for sportfishing. Shell-crushing pharyngeal teeth specialize on snails — ecological role distinct from other sunfish. Prefers cleaner water than Bluegill or Green Sunfish.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced game fish preferring cooler, clearer water than Largemouth Bass. Mouth does NOT extend past eye. Bronze coloring. Upper San Gabriel River.

fm.Color: Bronze to brown with dark vertical bars; red EYES; mouth does NOT extend past eye
fm.Habitat: Cooler clear streams and reservoirs — upper San Gabriel River
fm.Diet: Crayfish, small fish, insects — aggressive predator
fm.vs: Mouth does NOT extend past eye (Largemouth extends PAST eye). More bronze, vertical bars, red eyes.
fm.Urban: Stocked in some LA reservoirs. Bronze coloring and red eye distinguish from Largemouth Bass. Prefers rocky bottom over weedy areas.

freshwater_fish.hp: Prefers cooler water than Largemouth. Voracious predator of crayfish and native fish in mountain streams.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced panfish stocked in LA County lakes. Compressed deep body with dark vertical bars. Popular sport fish. Schools near underwater structure.

fm.Color: Silvery with dark VERTICAL bars; deep compressed body; 5–6 dorsal spines
fm.Habitat: Lakes, reservoirs — near submerged structure, brush piles
fm.Diet: Small fish, aquatic insects, crustaceans
fm.Urban: Stocked in LA County lakes. Schools near submerged brush piles and dock pilings. Popular panfish target at Hansen Dam, Puddingstone.
fm.vs: Longer, more pointed snout than Black Crappie. Vertical bars less distinct. Both crappies school near submerged structure.

freshwater_fish.hp: Introduced panfish popular with anglers. Schools near submerged structure. 5–6 dorsal spines (Black Crappie has 7–8).

freshwater_fish.desc: Third tilapia species establishing in LA — the most cold-tolerant of the three. Blue-gray coloration with metallic sheen. Can survive water temperatures that kill the other two tilapia species, allowing range expansion into cooler waterways.
freshwater_fish.hp: Most cold-tolerant tilapia in LA — can survive temperatures below 50°F that kill Mozambique and Nile tilapia. This cold tolerance makes it the most likely tilapia to spread beyond warm urban waterways into natural streams.

fm.Color: Blue-gray with metallic sheen; NO tail bars (unlike Nile Tilapia)
fm.Habitat: Urban waterways — more cold-tolerant than other tilapia
fm.vs: Nile Tilapia has tail bars. Mozambique has rounded tail. Blue Tilapia has blue-gray metallic coloring and NO tail bars.

freshwater_fish.desc: Central American cichlid established from aquarium releases in warm LA County streams. Named for bold black vertical bars. Aggressive and territorial — displaces native fish from prime habitat. Thrives in warm, shallow urban waterways.
freshwater_fish.hp: Aquarium release now established in warm streams and storm channels. Aggressive cichlid that displaces native species from shelter and food. Breeds year-round in warm water — rapid population growth in suitable habitat.

fm.Color: Gray-blue with bold BLACK vertical bars ('convict stripes')
fm.Habitat: Warm streams, storm channels, urban waterways
fm.Behavior: Territorial and aggressive; biparental care — both parents guard eggs and fry
fm.Diet: Insects, algae, small invertebrates — omnivorous
fm.vs: Mozambique Tilapia is larger, lacks convict bars. Convict Cichlid has distinctive black-and-gray barred pattern.
fm.Urban: Established in warm urban waterways and storm channels. Aggressive — displaces natives.

freshwater_fish.desc: Second tilapia species in LA — larger and more aggressive than Mozambique Tilapia. Introduced from aquaculture and aquarium releases. Can hybridize with Mozambique Tilapia. Dark vertical bars on tail are diagnostic.
freshwater_fish.hp: More cold-tolerant than Mozambique Tilapia — may spread further. Hybridizes with Mozambique Tilapia producing fertile offspring with mixed traits. Aquaculture escapees and intentional releases.

fm.Size: 6-16 in — can grow larger than Mozambique Tilapia
fm.Color: Olive-gray with dark VERTICAL BARS on tail (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Warm urban waterways, reservoirs, storm channels
fm.vs: Mozambique Tilapia lacks tail bars. Nile Tilapia has diagnostic dark VERTICAL BARS on caudal fin.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced tropical fish thriving in warm LA waterways — particularly the LA River and San Gabriel River. Mouth-brooding: males carry eggs and fry in mouth. Tolerates poor water quality and high temperatures.

fm.Color: Dark gray-olive; breeding males develop white chin and dark body
fm.Habitat: Warm slow waterways — LA River concrete channel, urban lakes, warm springs
fm.Behavior: Mouth-BROODING: males carry eggs and fry in mouth; tolerates warm, low-oxygen water
fm.Diet: Algae, aquatic plants, invertebrates, detritus — omnivorous
fm.vs: Deeper-bodied than native minnows. Characteristic cichlid profile with protrusible mouth.
fm.Urban: Abundant in LA River, particularly in warm-water stretches. Thrives in degraded urban waterways.

freshwater_fish.hp: Tropical introduction thriving in LA's warm concrete channels. Tolerates conditions lethal to most native fish — high temperature, low oxygen, poor water quality.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced forage fish stocked in reservoirs as food base for game fish. Silvery schooling fish with thread-like last dorsal fin ray. Massive schools in open water.

fm.Color: Silvery with blue-green back; dark shoulder spot; last dorsal fin ray extended into THREAD
fm.Habitat: Open water in lakes and reservoirs — massive schools
fm.Diet: Plankton, algae — filter feeder
fm.vs: Thread-like dorsal fin ray is diagnostic. Massive silvery schools in open water.
fm.Urban: Massive schools in LA reservoirs — sometimes visible as dark clouds in open water. Major die-offs during cold snaps leave thousands on shorelines.

freshwater_fish.hp: Stocked as forage fish for bass and other game fish. Massive die-offs during cold snaps — cannot tolerate water below 45°F.

freshwater_fish.desc: Elongate eel-like freshwater fish from East Asia. Established in LA River and tributaries from aquarium releases. Barbels around mouth for bottom-feeding. Can breathe air and survive in mud during droughts. Named for alleged increased activity before storms.
freshwater_fish.hp: Aquarium release established in LA River system. Can survive drought conditions by burrowing into mud and breathing air through intestinal respiration. This drought tolerance gives it a competitive advantage over native fish.

fm.Color: Brown-olive with dark mottling; 10 barbels around mouth
fm.Habitat: LA River, tributaries, urban streams — muddy bottoms
fm.Behavior: Burrows in mud; can breathe air; reportedly more active before storms
fm.Diet: Bottom invertebrates, detritus — barbel-guided foraging
fm.vs: No native LA fish is elongate with barbels like this. Catfish have barbels but are much larger and differently shaped.
fm.Urban: Established in LA River system. Can survive in degraded urban waterways.

freshwater_fish.desc: East Asian carp species recently split from Common Carp (C. carpio). Most 'Common Carp' in western US are now considered C. rubrofuscus based on genetics. Same large bottom-feeding carp degrading water quality in LA waterways.
freshwater_fish.hp: Taxonomic update — most LA 'Common Carp' populations are genetically C. rubrofuscus, not the European C. carpio. Same ecological impacts: uprooting vegetation, increasing turbidity, degrading habitat for native species.

fm.Color: Olive-brown to golden; large scales; barbels at corners of mouth
fm.Habitat: Lakes, reservoirs, slow rivers — every major LA waterway
fm.vs: Distinguished from Common Carp by genetics only — field indistinguishable. Goldfish is smaller and lacks barbels.

freshwater_fish.desc: Large introduced fish that muddies waters and destroys aquatic vegetation by rooting in bottom sediment. Found in every lake and slow waterway in LA. Can live 20+ years and reach 40+ lbs.

fm.Color: Golden-bronze to olive; large scales; two BARBELS at corners of mouth
fm.Habitat: Lakes, ponds, slow rivers, channels — tolerates poor water quality
fm.Behavior: Bottom ROOTER — stirs up sediment, destroys vegetation, muddies water; can jump barriers
fm.Diet: Omnivorous bottom feeder — aquatic plants, invertebrates, seeds, detritus
fm.vs: Goldfish (Carassius auratus) are smaller and lack BARBELS. Carp have barbels at mouth corners.
fm.Urban: Extremely common in LA River, lakes, and reservoirs. Degrades water quality through bottom-rooting.

freshwater_fish.hp: Introduced in 1800s. Bottom-rooting behavior destroys aquatic vegetation and increases turbidity, harming native fish that need clear water.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced bait minnow now established in LA waterways. Males develop fat HEAD PAD and tubercles during breeding. Used extensively as bait and feeder fish.

fm.Color: Olive-brown; breeding males develop dark coloring and fleshy HEAD PAD
fm.Habitat: Ponds, slow streams, ditches — tolerates poor water quality
fm.Diet: Algae, small invertebrates, detritus
fm.Urban: Bait shop escapee now in virtually every standing water body in LA. Sold as feeder fish — released by aquarists. Thrives in degraded urban waterways.
fm.vs: Breeding males develop dark head and fleshy pad on nape — unlike any other LA minnow. Females/non-breeding males plain olive.

freshwater_fish.hp: Bait shop escapee now established throughout LA waterways. Tolerates extremely degraded water quality.

freshwater_fish.desc: Released pet goldfish thriving in LA waterways. Revert to wild coloring (olive-brown) within generations. Can grow surprisingly large in the wild — 12+ inches. Damages habitat like carp.

fm.Size: 4–12+ in in the wild (much larger than pet store)
fm.Color: Wild-type: olive-brown (reverts from orange within generations); some retain orange/gold
fm.Habitat: Ponds, lakes, slow waterways — any standing water
fm.Diet: Omnivorous — aquatic plants, invertebrates, algae, detritus
fm.vs: Lacks BARBELS (Carp has barbels). Smaller than Carp. Wild-type coloring often olive, not gold.
fm.Urban: Released pet goldfish in every park pond and urban lake. Echo Park Lake, MacArthur Park, Balboa Lake. Revert to olive-brown wild coloring within generations. Can reach 12+ inches.

freshwater_fish.hp: Released pet goldfish revert to wild coloring in 1–2 generations. Grow much larger in the wild than in aquariums. Root in sediment like carp.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced bait minnow with males developing brilliant RED fins during breeding. Aggressive competitor displacing native minnows. Spreading through LA waterways.

fm.Color: Silver; breeding males develop brilliant RED fins and blue-purple body sheen
fm.Habitat: Streams, ponds, channels — tolerant of varied conditions
fm.Diet: Insects, algae, small invertebrates
fm.vs: Breeding males with RED fins are distinctive. Non-breeding fish resemble other small minnows.
fm.Urban: Bait shop releases spreading through LA waterways. Breeding males with brilliant red fins visible in clear shallow streams. Displacing native Arroyo Chub.

freshwater_fish.hp: Bait shop release spreading aggressively through LA waterways. Breeding males develop brilliant red fins. Displacing native Arroyo Chub through competition and possibly hybridization. One of the most damaging introduced fish in southern California streams.

freshwater_fish.desc: Small dark catfish in muddy ponds and slow streams. More tolerant of poor water quality than Channel Catfish. Dark barbels and squared tail.

fm.Color: Dark brown to black; dark BARBELS; squared (not forked) tail
fm.Habitat: Muddy ponds, slow ditches — tolerates very low oxygen
fm.Diet: Bottom omnivore — insects, worms, plant matter
fm.vs: Channel Catfish has FORKED tail and spots. Black Bullhead has SQUARED tail and no spots.
fm.Urban: In the muddiest, most degraded urban ponds and ditches. Survives where virtually nothing else can — near-anoxic conditions.

freshwater_fish.hp: Tolerates extremely degraded water — survives in nearly anoxic conditions. Thrives where other fish cannot.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced whiskered catfish stocked in LA County lakes. Nocturnal bottom feeder. Barbels ('whiskers') taste food. Stinging pectoral spine can puncture skin.

fm.Color: Gray-olive with dark spots (young); deeply forked tail; 8 BARBELS around mouth; adipose fin
fm.Habitat: Lakes, reservoirs, slow rivers — bottom-dwelling
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; bottom feeder; barbels detect food by taste/smell in murky water; venomous pectoral SPINES
fm.Diet: Bottom omnivore — fish, crayfish, insects, plant matter, carrion
fm.vs: Only catfish commonly stocked in LA. Barbels + deeply forked tail + adipose fin diagnostic.
fm.Urban: Stocked in every major LA County reservoir — Hansen Dam, Santa Fe Dam, Puddingstone, Legg Lake. Night fishing with stink bait on the bottom is the classic LA catfishing technique.

freshwater_fish.hp: Stocked for sport fishing. Pectoral spine can inflict painful wound — handle with care. Nocturnal bottom feeder using barbels as taste sensors.

freshwater_fish.desc: Large predatory fish from the Atlantic coast — stocked in some LA County reservoirs. Anadromous in native range but landlocked in LA lakes. Silver with prominent dark horizontal stripes. Important sportfish that can reach 30+ lbs.
freshwater_fish.hp: Stocked in select reservoirs for sportfishing. Native to Atlantic coast — introduced throughout California. Large predator that may impact native fish populations in stocked waters.

fm.Color: Silver with 7-8 dark HORIZONTAL stripes along body
fm.Habitat: Reservoirs, coastal waterways — stocked
fm.vs: No native LA freshwater fish has dark horizontal stripes. Striped Bass is distinctive and large.

freshwater_fish.desc: Ubiquitous aquarium fish from South America — released feral populations in warm LA waterways. Males are tiny and brilliantly colored (orange, blue, black spots). Gives live birth. Only survives in warm water — near thermal outflows and in warm seasons.
freshwater_fish.hp: Aquarium releases. Cannot survive cold water — restricted to thermally stable sites like warm springs, power plant outflows, and sheltered urban channels. Third livebearing Poeciliid in guide after Mosquitofish and Sailfin Molly.

fm.Color: Males: tiny, brilliantly colored (orange, blue, black). Females: plain olive.
fm.Habitat: Warm streams, thermal outflows — requires warm water year-round
fm.vs: Mosquitofish is plain olive without bright colors. Sailfin Molly males have enlarged dorsal fin. Male Guppies are tiny and brilliantly multicolored.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced tropical livebearer from aquarium trade. Males have tall dorsal SAIL fin. Established in warm-water outflows and thermal springs. Related to guppies.

fm.Color: Olive to silvery; males develop tall dorsal SAIL FIN and sometimes bright orange/blue coloring
fm.Habitat: Warm water — thermal outflows, warm springs, heated effluent channels
fm.Behavior: Live-bearing (gives birth to live young); males display sail fin in courtship
fm.Diet: Algae, small invertebrates, detritus — primarily herbivorous
fm.vs: Tall dorsal SAIL FIN in males is diagnostic. Live-bearing like Mosquitofish but larger with sail fin.
fm.Urban: Established in warm-water outflows — power plant discharge channels, warm springs. Cannot survive cold natural waterways.

freshwater_fish.hp: Livebearing fish requiring warm water year-round — restricted to thermally stable sites. Males display enlarged sailfin dorsal fin during courtship. Can tolerate extreme salinity ranges from fresh to twice seawater.

freshwater_fish.desc: Small ornamental livebearer from Mexico — aquarium releases in warm LA waterways. Males are brightly colored (orange, yellow, red). Related to swordtails. Can only survive in warm water.
freshwater_fish.hp: Aquarium releases. Fourth livebearer in LA waterways (after Mosquitofish, Sailfin Molly, Guppy). Like Guppy, restricted to warm water sites.

fm.Color: Males: bright orange/yellow/red. Females: olive.
fm.Habitat: Warm streams, thermal outflows
fm.vs: Guppy males are smaller and multicolored. Platyfish are stockier with variable solid colors. Mosquitofish is plain olive.

freshwater_fish.desc: Tiny live-bearing fish deliberately introduced throughout LA for MOSQUITO CONTROL. Now in every body of water. Despite good intentions, displaces native fish and is less effective at mosquito control than native species.

fm.Color: Gray-olive; females larger with dark gravid spot near anal fin; upturned mouth
fm.Habitat: EVERYWHERE — any standing water including ditches, storm drains, garden ponds
fm.Behavior: Live-bearing (gives birth to live young, not eggs); surface-feeding; aggressive; very prolific
fm.Diet: Mosquito larvae, algae, small invertebrates — but also eats native fish larvae and frog eggs
fm.vs: Similar to native topminnows but native topminnows are extremely rare/extirpated from LA.
fm.Urban: Deliberately distributed by LA County Vector Control for mosquito control. In virtually every water body.

freshwater_fish.hp: Introduced worldwide for mosquito control but often does more ecological harm than good — displaces native fish and is actually less effective than many native species.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced eastern char (not a True trout) stocked in SGM mountain streams. Distinctive WORM-LIKE markings on dark back and red spots with blue halos. Square tail. Preys on native fish.

fm.Color: Dark olive-green with pale WORM-LIKE vermiculations on back; RED spots with BLUE halos on sides; white leading edges on lower fins; SQUARE tail
fm.Habitat: Cold mountain streams — SGM above 4,000 ft. Requires coldest, cleanest water.
fm.Diet: Insects, small fish — preys on native Rainbow Trout fry
fm.vs: Rainbow Trout has DARK spots on LIGHT background + forked tail. Brook Trout has LIGHT markings on DARK background + SQUARE tail.

freshwater_fish.hp: Introduced — competes with native Rainbow Trout. Requires the coldest water of any LA fish. Light markings on dark background is opposite of Rainbow Trout's pattern.

freshwater_fish.desc: Introduced European trout in SGM mountain streams. Dark spots surrounded by pale HALOS. More wary and harder to catch than Rainbow Trout. Predator of native fish.

fm.Color: Brown with dark spots surrounded by pale HALOS; some red spots on sides; adipose fin
fm.Habitat: Cool mountain streams — SGM above 3,000 ft
fm.Diet: Insects, small fish, crayfish — more piscivorous than Rainbow Trout
fm.vs: Dark spots with pale HALOS distinguish from Rainbow Trout (which has spots without halos). No pink side stripe.

freshwater_fish.hp: Introduced European trout competing with native Rainbow Trout/Steelhead. More piscivorous — preys on native fish.
